colored areas of an apple and a pear.\n\nDESCRIPTION OF THE PREFERRED EMBODIMENT\n\nProperties of Watercolor\n\nFor centuries, ground pigments have been combined with water soluble binding materials and used in painting. The earliest uses of watercolor were as thin colored washes painstakingly applied to detailed pen-and-ink or pencil illustrations. The modern tradition of watercolor, however, dates back to the latter half of the eighteenth century, when artists began to experiment with new techniques such as wiping and scratching out, and with the immediacy and spontaneity of the medium. In order to simulate watercolors effectively, it is important to study not only the physical properties of the medium, but also the characteristic phenomena that make watercolor so popular to artists. A simulation of this medium will only be successful if it can achieve many of the same effects. The following discussion addresses this problem.\n\nTo properly simulate watercolor images, it is first necessary to understand the components used in creating such images. A watercolor is created in the conventional manner by applying a suspension of pigment particles in a solution of water, binder, and sulfactant to paper. A pigment is a solid material in the form of small, separate particles. Watercolor pigments are typically ground in a milling process into a powder made of grains ranging from about 0.05 to 0.5 microns. Pigments can penetrate into the paper, and arc helped in this regard by the surfactant. However, once in the paper they tend not to migrate far from the point of application. Pigments vary in density, and lighter pigments tend to stay suspended in water longer than heavier ones, and thus can spread farther across paper. Staining power, an estimate of the pigment's ability to adhere to or coat paper fibers, also varies between pigments. Certain pigments exhibit granulation, in which particles settled into the hollows of rough paper. Others exhibit flocculation, in which particles are drawn together into clumps, usually by electrical effects. Flocculation is similar in appearance to granulation, and can occur on smooth as well as rough paper.\n\nThe binder is used in watercolors to help bind the pigment to the paper (which is also known as \u201cadsorption of the pigment by the paper\u201d). The binder also plays a role in binding the pigment to itself\u2014particularly when thick layers of paint are applied. Without a binder, most of the pigment particles would blow away once the paper dries. However, for thin layers of paint, the mechanical ability of paper fibers to hold pigment particles is at least as significant as the effect of the binder, as anyone who has tried to scrub away watercolor paint knows. The most common binder is gum arabic, which is derived from the stems of certain acacia trees.\n\nSurfactants, or wetting agents, are added to lower the surface tension between two materials\u2014in this case, between the pigment and the binder, as well as between the water and the paper. The surfactant improves the uniform flow of water over the surface of the paper and also helps the water to penetrate into the paper. In this latter sense, the surfactant counteracts the effect of sizing in the paper, which is described below. The surfactant also increases the rate of evaporation of the water, which in turn reduces granulation and flocculation effects of pigments. Oxgall is a commonly used wetting agent for watercolor.\n\nWatercolor images are created by the application of water and paint to paper. Watercolor paper is typically not made from wood pulp, but instead from linen or cotton rags pounded into small fibers. The paper itself is mostly air, laced with a microscopic web of these tangled fibers. Such a substance is obviously extremely absorbent to liquids, and so the paper is impregnated with sizing so that liquid paints may be used on it without immediately soaking in and diffusing. Sizing is usually made of cellulose. It form's a barrier that slows the rate of water absorption and diffusion. For watercolor papers, sizing is typically applied sparingly and just coats the fibers and fills some of the pores, leaving the paper surface still rough.\n\nThe final appearance of watercolor derives from the interaction between the movements of various pigments in a flowing medium, the adsorption of these pigments by the paper, the absorption of water into the paper, and the eventual evaporation of the water medium. Obviously, these interactions are quite complex in nature and can be used by a skilled artist to achieve a wide variety of effects, as described in the next section.\n\nWatercolor Effects\n\nWatercolor can be used in many different ways, but there are three basic brushing techniques, including:\n\nWet-on-dry: A wet brush is applied to dry paper. An example of this technique is shown in Plate **2**A. The sizing in the paper, coupled with the surface tension of water, does not allow the brushstroke to spread. Instead, in a gradual process, the interior of the painted region lightens slightly, and the edges of the painted region darken significantly as the paint begins to dry. It is suspected that this phenomenon is caused by faster evaporation at the edges of the painted region due to a relatively greater surface area, which in turn pulls more water and pigment to the edges. Whatever its causes, the edge darkening is an important effect that watercolor artists rely upon and that paint manufacturers take pains to ensure in their watercolor paint formulations. Granulation is another important wet-on-dry effect for certain pigments.\n\nDry brush: A brush that is almost out of paint can be used to create strokes with a rough, ragged texture, with the paint adhering only to the raised areas of the rough paper. In this case, white pinpoints of dry paper continue to show through. Plate **2**B illustrates this technique.\n\nWet-on-wet: A wet brush is applied to wet paper. The wet surface allows the brushstrokes to spread, exhibiting soft, feathery flow patterns. Some pigments will also tend to granulate where the paper is particularly wet, as illustrated in Plate **2**C.\n\nIn addition, there are a number of standard effects that can be reliably employed by the watercolor expert. These include:\n\nIntentional backruns: Dark, branching reticulations around a light area are created as a puddle of water in a damp region of paint dries. This effect is illustrated in Plate **2**D.\n\nSettling out and separation of pigments: Settling out of pigments yields a kind of grainy texture. Separation exhibits a splitting of colors, with the lighter-weight pigments generally traveling farther from the initial brush position. These two effects are illustrated respectively in Plates **2**E and **2**F.\n\nPlates **2**A-**2**F are scanned-in images of real watercolors. Plates **1**A-**1**B and **3**A-**3**F illustrate similar effects obtained with watercolor simulations created using the present invention.\n\nOne other very important technique in watercolor is the process of color glazing. Glazing is the process of adding very thin, pale layers, or washes, of watercolor, one over another, in order to achieve a very clear and even effect. Each layer of watercolor is added after the previous layer has dried. More expensive watercolor paints are specially formulated to have a low resolubility, which not only allows thin uniform washes to be overlaid, but in fact allows any type of brushing technique to be employed over a dried wash (including dry brush and wet-on-wet) without disturbing the underlying layers.\n\nGlazing is different from ordinary painting in that the different pigments are not mixed physically, but optically\u2014in their superposition on the paper. Glazes yield a pleasing effect that is often described as \u201cluminous,\u201d or as \u201cglowing from within.\u201d It is believed that this subjective impression arises from the wet-on-dry effect in which a watercolor brushstroke becomes slightly lighter on its interior and markedly darker at its boundary. This effect is intensified with multiple wet-on-dry washes. These subtle variations in tone are probably responsible for this \u201cluminous\u201d appearance.\n\nComputer Generated Watercolor Model\n\nImplementing all of these artistically important effects automatically presents an interesting challenge, particularly given the paucity of available information on the physical processes involved. A model must be developed to artificially simulate the physical and optical behavior of watercolors. The details of this model are described below. In the description of the present invention and in the claims that follow, the watercolor simulation is discussed in terms that at times may seem to be directed to an actual watercolor rather than a simulation. The watercolor simulation is defined based upon certain real parameters that would affect the appearance of a real watercolor, but it should be understood that these parameters are virtual parameters. Thus, for example, when the following discussion refers to the \u201ctexture of the paper\u201d on which the watercolor simulation is being rendered, it should be apparent that this reference simply applies to the modeling of the surface on the watercolor simulation is rendered\u2014not to an actual paper. Similarly, references to a \u201cwet-area\u201d refer to an area that is modeled as wetted by water\u2014and not to an actual wetted region. These and many other terms used below that seem to relate to aspects of an actual watercolor thus simply refer to those aspects in the model used to produce the watercolor simulation.\n\nThe approach employed in building a watercolor model has been to start with physically-based equations, such as the shallow water equations, and then to add terms as necessary to create the desired effects. Thus, while the model is motivated by the physical phenomena, it is not a strict physical simulation. It does, however, do a reasonably good job of synthesizing the most salient watercolor effects.\n\nA complete painting is preferably represented in the model as an ordered set of translucent layers, or washes, applied over a sheet of rough paper. Each wash may contain various pigments in varying quantities over different parts of the image. Information about these pigments is stored in a data structure called a \u201cglaze.\u201d In the present invention, a glaze is a set of floating-point image arrays with one array, or channel, for each pigment in the wash. The floating-point numbers in a given channel represent the quantity of the corresponding pigment present at each pixel in the watercolor image.\n\nEach glaze is created independently by running a fluid simulation that computes the flow of paints over the paper. FIG. 1 presents an overview **20** of the functional components employed in this process for producing a three-layer fluid simulation **22**. The simulation takes as input pigment parameters **24** that control the physical properties of pigments (size, rate of adsorption, etc.), physical properties **26** of the paper (surface height, water capacity, etc.), and simulation parameters **30** (size of time step, etc.). In addition, the simulation makes use of wet-area masks **28**, which represent the damp areas of paper. These masks control where water is allowed to flow, and therefore the limits of the fluid flow computation. The masks may expand during simulation if water diffusing through the bottom layer would have caused the paper to become damp. The next section describes this fluid simulation in detail. Once the glazes are computed, they are optically composited using a Kubelka-Munk (KM) color model to provide the final visual effect, as described below.\n\nThe Fluid Simulation\n\nIn this simulation, each individual wash is simulated using a three-layer model **32**, as illustrated in FIG. **2**. From top to bottom, as shown in this view, these three layers include:\n\na shallow water layer **36**\u2014where water and pigment flow above the surface of a paper **34**;\n\na pigment deposition layer **38**\u2014where pigment is deposited onto (\u201cadsorbed by\u201d) and lifted (\u201cdesorbed\u201d) from the paper.\n\na capillary layer **40**\u2014where water that is absorbed into the paper is diffused by capillary action.\n\nThe shallow water layer is represented by a spatially-discretized height field, together with a set of arrays representing the quantities of different pigments at each spatial location. A modified version of the shallow water equations is used to compute fluid flow above the paper. The pigment deposition layer represents pigment attached to the paper. Shallow water layer **36** interacts only with the shallow water layer above it, and not with the capillary layer below. The capillary layer uses another height-field representation of water flowing within the paper. This layer is used to expand or shrink the wet-area masks, which creates backrun effects.\n\nIn the following derivations and pseudo code, letters in italics (such as u and v) are used to represent scalar quantities, and bold italics (such as u and v) are to represent arrays of these quantities, discretized over a discrete set of cells in the simulation.\n\nMain Logic Loop of the Computer Model\n\nThe main loop of the simulation takes as input the initial quantities of water in the shallow water and capillary layers, denoted p and q, respectively; and the initial amount of each pigment i in the shallow water and pigment deposition layers, denoted gs[i] and gd[i], respectively. It also uses two scalar parameters u and k, which control the viscosity of the fluid, and friction of the fluid against the paper.\n\nThe main loop iterates over a specified number of time steps, performing various aspects of the fluid simulation and outputting a frame at the end of each step. (These frames can be concatenated into an animated sequence for inclusion into video. As is typical of an animation, one or more objects depicted in the simulations may change position over a series of such frames, so that when the frames are successively displayed sufficiently fast, the object appears to move.) Alternately, just the final frame can be used to display just a finished watercolor painting. A flow chart **50** illustrating the logic implemented in the main loop is provided in FIG. 6, and the following pseudo code includes references to blocks in this Figure. The code proceeds as follows:\n\nproc MainLoop(u,v,gs,gd):\n\nforeach time step do\n\n/* Move water and pigments in shallow water layer, as indicated in a block **52** */\n\nMoveFluid(u, v,p, u, k, paperslope)\n\n/* Redistribute fluid into neighboring cells to reduce divergence, as noted in a block **54** */\n\nReduceDivergetice(u, v, p, relax, maxdiv, maxsteps)\n\n/* Reduce pigment in each cell based on rate of fluid movement out of cell, as noted in a block **56** */\n\nMovePigment(gs[1. . . npigs], u, v, \u0394t)\n\n/* Adsorb pigment into paper, in accord with a block **58** */\n\nAdsorbPigment(gs[1. . . npigs], gd[1. . . npigs], b, deposit[1. . . npigs], lift[1. . . npigs], exposure[1. . . npigs])\n\n/* As water evaporates, darken edges slowly, as provided in a block **60** */\n\nDarkenEdges(p, mask, edgeKer, darkening)\n\n/* Move water in capillary layer, to simulate diffusion, as noted in a block **62**. For backrun effects only. */\n\nDiffuseWater(q, mask, absorption, \u03b5, \u03b4, \u03c3)\n\n/* Use the KM model to render the simulation by compositing the layers, as provided in a block **64** */\n\nRender(gs[1. . . npigs], gd[1. . . npigs])\n\n/* As indicated in a decision block **66**, continue looping within the FOR loop to block **52** until the time steps have expired */\n\nEndfor\n\n/* After all of the time steps have expired, stop the process */\n\nendproc\n\nMoving Water in the Shallow Water Layer\n\nDetails of block **52** are illustrated in FIG. **7**. However, before discussing the logical steps employed in moving water in the shallow water layer, certain parameters that are used in the simulation should be defined and explained. Let p be the fluid pressure in a cell, which is proportional to the quantity of fluid in the cell (denoted by p in the pseudo code). Also let u and v denote the fluid velocities in the x and y directions. The following modified shallow water equations are used in the simulation:  <!-- MathML: <math overflow=\"scroll\"> <mtable> <mtr> <mtd> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mo>\u2202</mo> <mi>u</mi> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>v</mi> </mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mfrac> <mrow> <mo>\u2202</mo> <mi>p</mi> </mrow> <mrow> <mo>\u2202</mo> <mi>y</mi> </mrow> </mfrac> <mo>-</mo> <mrow> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <msub> <mi>k</mi> <mi>d</mi> </msub> <mo>\ue89e</mo> <msup> <mi>\uf74d</mi> <mrow> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>0.1</mn> </mrow> <mo>\ue89e</mo> <mi>p</mi> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> <mo>\ue89e</mo> <mi>v</mi> \u2202  y 2     )  +  u \ue89e  \u2003  \ue89e   \u0394  2  \ue89e u  -   \u2202 p   \u2202 x   -   (   k d  \ue89e  \uf74d   - 0.1  \ue89e p    )  \ue89e u       ( 1 )         \u2202 u   \u2202 t   =   (    \u2202   v  2    \u2202   y  2    +   \u2202 uv   \u2202  x 2     )  +  u \ue89e  \u2003  \ue89e   \u0394  2  \ue89e v  -   \u2202 p   \u2202 y   -   (   k d  \ue89e  \uf74d   - 0.1  \ue89e p    )  \ue89e v       ( 2 )\n\n[![Figure US06198489-20010306-M00001](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/e6/8e/a9/59e382a0ad8318/US06198489-20010306-M00001.png)](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/e6/8e/a9/59e382a0ad8318/US06198489-20010306-M00001.png)\n\nThese equations are spatially discretized on a staggered grid. The staggered grid representation stores velocity values at grid cell boundaries and other values (pressure, pigment, etc.) at grid cell centers. The standard notation for staggered grids is used: integer indices (like the \u201ci\u201d and \u201cj\u201d in pi, j) are grid cell centers, and indices incremented or decreinented by \u00bd (like \u201ci+\u00bd\u201d in ui+\u00bdj) j) are used for grid cell boundaries. The equations are discretized in time and solved forward using Euler's Method, with an adaptive step size. The step size is reduced if velocities exceed one pixel per time step.\n\nA gravity term is added to account for the local slope paperslope of the surface of the paper. This term is added to the velocities before the fluid step is computed.\n\nThe final pseudo code (with reference to the corresponding blocks in FIG. 7) for integrating the fluid simulation forward one time unit is as follows:\n\nproc MoveFluid(u, v, p, k, u, \u0394t, paperslope)\n\n/* Subtract paper slope from velocities u and v, as noted in a block **68** */\n\nu\u2212=paperslopex\n\nv\u2212=paperslopey\n\n/* Determine if either velocity is greater than zero, as provided in a decision block **70** */\n\nif (max(u)>1 or max(v)>1)\n\n/* If so, then reduce the step size, in accord with a block **72** */\n\nthen reduce stepsize \u0394t\n\n/* Otherwise, set time to zero, as noted in a block **74**, get the first cell, as noted in a block **76**, and begin a loop */\n\nfor t from 0 to 1 by \u0394t do\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\n/* Update the velocity in current cell using Euler integration, as indicated in a block **78** */\n\n *\u00fb* i+\u00bd,j *=u* i+\u00bd,j+\u0394t(\u00bc(*u* i\u2212\u00bd,j *+u* i+\u00bd,j)2\u2212\u00bc(*u* i+\u00bd,j *+u* i+{fraction (3/2)},j)2\n\n+\u00bc(*u* i+\u00bd,j\u22121 *+u* i+\u00bd,j)(*v* i,j\u2212\u00bd *+v* i\u22121,j+\u00bd)\n\n\u2212\u00bc(*u* i\u2212\u00bd,j *+u* i+\u00bd,j+1)(*v* i,j+\u00bd *+v* i+1,j+\u00bd)\n\n+*u*(*u* i+{fraction (3/2)},j *+u* i\u2212\u00bd,j *+u* i+\u00bd,j+1 *+u* i+\u00bd,j\u22121\u22124*u* i+\u00bd,j)\n\n+(*p* i,j *\u2212p* i+1,j)\u2212ke\u22120.1(pi,j+pi+1,j)/2 *u* i+\u00bd,j)\n\n{circumflex over (v)}i,j+\u00bd *=v* i,j+\u00bd+\u0394t(\u00bc(*v* i,j\u2212\u00bd *+v* i,j+\u00bd)2\u2212\u00bc(*v* i,j+\u00bd *+u* i,j+{fraction (3/2)})2\n\n+\u00bc(*u* i\u2212\u00bd,j *+u* i\u2212\u00bd,j+1)(*v* i\u22121,j+\u00bd *+v* i,j+\u00bd)\n\n\u2212\u00bc(*u* i+\u00bd,j *+u* i+\u00bd,j+1)(*v* i,j+\u00bd *+v* i+1,j+\u00bd)\n\n+*u*(*v* i+1,j+\u00bd *+v* i\u22121,j+\u00bd *+v* i,j+{fraction (3/2)} *+v* i,j\u2212\u00bd\u22124*v* i,j+\u00bd)\n\n+(*p* i,j *+p* i,j+1)\u2212ke\u22120.1(pi,j+pi,j+1)/2 *v* i,j+\u00bd)\n\n/* Determine if there are any more cells, as noted in a decision block **80**, and if so, get the next cell, as provided in a block **82**. Once all cells have been processed, add a step to time, as provided in a block **84** */\n\nendfor\n\n/* Determine if current time is greater than or equal to one, in a decision block **86**, and if so, stop; otherwise, loop back block **76** in an outer FOR loop, to repeat the step in block **78** for each cell /*\n\nendfor\n\nu=\u00fb\n\nv={circumflex over (v)}\n\nendproc\n\nReducing Divergence\n\nThe present invention provides for reducing the divergence of the velocity field (\u2202u/\u2202v+\u2202v/\u2202y) after each time step until it is less than maxdiv by redistributing the fluid into neighboring grid cells. This step does not have as clear a physical basis in the two-dimensional (2D) shallow water simulation as it has in a related prior art three-dimensional (3D) simulation. However, the step appears to make a significant difference for reducing ringing artifacts in the simulation, and has thus been found to be useful. In this approach, relax, maxdiv and maxsteps are parameters that control aspects of the simulation (see the table in FIG. 3 for the symbols/values used in a current preferred embodiment, their definitions, and an indication of the procedure in which these symbols/values are used). The steps implemented in reducing divergence are shown in FIG. **8** and the blocks describing these steps and corresponding pseudo code are as follows:\n\nproc ReduceDivergence(u, v, p, relax, maxdiv, maxsteps)\n\nlocal dp, div, count, maxdp;\n\n/* A block **90** sets a count to zero to initialize the process. The divergence is determined, as noted in a block **92**, and a decision block **94** provides for determining whether the divergence is less than or equal to the maximum divergence, maxdiv, and if so, the procedure stops. If not, a decision block **96** determines if the count is greater than a maximum count, maxsteps, and if so, the procedure stops */\n\nwhile ((div>maxdiv) and (count<1maxsteps)) do\n\n/* If the response to both decision blocks **94** and **96** is negative, a block **98** provides for getting a first cell, as the initial step in an inner loop */\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\n/* Fluid pressure is dispersed locally around current cell, as noted in a block **100** */\n\ndp=-relax*(ui+\u00bd,j\u2212ui+\u00bd,j *+v* i+\u00bd,j\u2212vi+\u00bd,j\u2212)\n\npi,j+=dp /* Note: p modified in place */\n\n\u00fbi+\u00bd,j+=dp\n\n\u00fbi\u2212\u00bd,j\u2212=dp\n\n{circumflex over (v)}i,j+\u00bd+=dp\n\n{circumflex over (v)}i,j\u2212\u00bd\u2212=dp\n\nmaxdp=max(dp, maxdp)\n\n/* A decision block **102** determines if there are any more cells to be processed, and if so, a block **104** provided for getting the next cell for input to the process in block **100** */\n\nendfor\n\n/* If there are not any more cells to be processed in decision block **102**, a block **106** provides for incrementing the count, and then the process loops back to block **92** */\n\ncount+=1\n\nendwhile\n\nu=\u00fb\n\nv={circumflex over (v)}\n\nreturn maxdiv\n\nendproc\n\nMoving Pigments in the Shallow Water Layer\n\nPigments move within the top layer as specified by the velocity field u, \u03bdcomputed for the water set forth above. In this part of the simulation, the relative quantity of pigment in each cell is reduced according to the rate of fluid movement out of the cell. This pigment is then distributed to the neighboring cells appropriately, as defined by the steps indicated in the blocks of FIG. **9** and by the following pseudo code, which corresponds to the blocks as indicated:\n\nproc MovePigment(gs[1. . . upigs], u, v, \u0394t)\n\n/* A block **110** provides for setting the variable NSTEPS to the reciprocal of the time step */\n\nnsteps=1/\u0394t\n\n/* A block **112** provides for getting the first pigment included in the pigments used, a block **114** sets the step to zero, and a block **116** provides for getting the first cell */\n\nforall p\u03b5npigs do\n\nfor nsteps do\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}=gs\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\n/* A block **118** provides for dispersing the current pigment locally around the current cell; a decision block **120** then determines if there are any more cells, and if so, a block **122** provides for getting the next cell, so that the dispersion process implemented in block **118** can be repeated for the next cell. If not, a block **124** indicates that the step variable is incremented before proceeding with a decision block **126** */\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i+1,j\u2212=max(ui+\u00bd,j gs[p]i,j 0)\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i\u22121,j\u2212=max(\u2212ui\u2212\u00bd,j gs[p]i,j 0)\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i,j+1\u2212=max(vi,j+\u00bd gs[p]i,j 0)\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i,j\u22121\u2212=max(vi,j\u2212\u00bd gs[p]i,j 0)\n\n{circumflex over (gs)}[p]i,j\u2212=max(ui+\u00bd,jgs[p]i,j 0)+max(\u2212ui\u2212\u00bd,j gs[p]i,j,0)+max(*v* i,j+\u00bd gs[p]i,j,0)+max(*v* i,j\u2212\u00bdgs[p]i,j,0)\n\n/* Decision block **126** determines if the current value of the step variable is less than that of the variable nsteps, and if so, the logic loops back to block **116**. Otherwise, the logic proceeds to a decision block **128**, which determines if there are any more pigments to be processed, and if so, a block **130** provides for getting the next pigment, before looping back to block **114** */\n\nendfor\n\ngs={circumflex over (gs)}\n\nendfor\n\nendfor\n\n/* A negative response to decision block **128**, terminates the process */ p={circumflex over (p)}\n\nendproc\n\nPigment Adsorption and Desorption\n\nAt each step of the simulation, pigment is also adsorbed by the pigment deposition layer at a certain rate, and desorbed back into the fluid at another rate. Note that any initial pigment placed outside of any wet-area mask **28** is immediately entirely adsorbed by the pigment deposition layer. The parameters deposit[p] and lift[p], which are included in the table of FIG. 3, are scalars representing the rate of adsorption and desorption. These parameters determine how quickly pigment is deposited onto the paper. The exposure[p] parameter determines how much the paper height b affects lift and deposit rates, and its value can be selected so as to imitate pigments that are heavier and tend to sediment out into the valleys of the paper. FIG. 10 shows the logical steps implemented in this adsorption/desorption process, and these steps are reflected in the following pseudo code, which defines the AdsorbPigments procedure:\n\nproc AdsorbPigment(gs[1. . . npigs], gd[1. . . npigs], b, deposit[1. . . npigs], lift[1. . . npigs],\n\nexposure[1. . . npigs])\n\n/* A block **132** in the Figure provides for getting the first pigment, while a block **134** provides for getting the first cell */ local Ddown, Dup\n\nforall p\u03b5npigs do\n\nforall cells(i,j) do\n\n/* As provided in a block **136**, the pigment in the current cell is desorbed, while a block **138** notes that the pigment in the current cell is absorbed */\n\nDdown=gd[p] lift[p] (1+exposure[p](b\u22121))\n\nDup=gs[p] deposit[p] (1\u2212exposure[p]b)\n\nif ((gd[p]+Ddown)>1)\n\nthen Ddowin=max(1\u2212gs[p], 0)\n\nif ((gs[p]+Dup)>1)\n\nthen Dup=max(1\u2212gd[p], 0)\n\ngd[p]+=Ddown\u2212Dup\n\ngs[p]+=Dup\u2212Ddown\n\n/* A decision block **140** determines if there are any more cells, and if so, a block **142** provides for getting the next cell prior to repeating the desorption and adsorption process of  block  **136** and **138**. If there are no more cells, the logic advances to a decision block **144** */\n\nendfor\n\n/* Decision block **144** determines if there are any more pigments, and if so, advances to a block **146**, which provides for getting the next pigment */\n\nendfor\n\n/* A negative response to decision block **144** terminates the process */\n\nendproc\n\nEdge Darkening\n\nIt is conjectured that edge darkening arises from increased evaporation near the edges of a small puddle, due to the relatively larger surface area of fluid per unit area of paper near the edge. The following routine, which corresponds to the logic shown in FIG. 11, simulates this effect, i.e., removing water from the edges. The water removal causes a slight fluid flow toward the edges and in turn brings in more pigment. The procedure and corresponding blocks are as follows:\n\nproc DarkenEdges(p, mask, edgeKer, darkening)\n\n/* A block **150** provides for getting the first cell */\n\nblur=a Gaussian blurred version of mask with kernel edgeKer\n\n/* A decision block **152** determines if the mask at the current position is>zero, i.e., if the cell is within the mask area */\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\nif (maski,j>0)\n\n/* If the response to decision block **152** is affirmative, a block **154** calls for reducing fluid pressure in the current cell in proportion to the Gaussian blur of the mask at the current cell position */\n\nthen pi,j\u2212=max(min(blurij* darkening, pi,j), 0)\n\n/* The logic proceeds to a decision block **156** if the response to decision block **152** is negative or after the step in block **154**. Decision block **156** determines if there are any more cells, and if so, a block **158** provides for getting the next cell before looping back to decision block **152** */\n\nendfor\n\n/* A negative response to decision block **156** results in termination of the process */\n\nendproc\n\nDiffusing Water through the Capillary Layer\n\nThe following routine is used in the present invention to simulate the diffusion of water through the bottom, capillary layer, according to the paper's holding capacity pcap, which specifies the volume not occupied by paper fibers. This routine can also expand wet-area mask **28**, which creates synthetic \u201cbackrun\u201d effects. The routine takes as its parameters: \u03b5, the minimum saturation a pixel must have before it can diffuse to its neighbors; \u03b4, a value below which a pixel will not receive diffusion; and \u03c3, the minimum saturation required to expand the wet-area to include a pixel. The logic implemented in the routine is illustrated in FIG. 12, with reference to the following pseudo code:\n\nproc DiffuseWater(mask, pcap, q, absorption, \u03b5, \u03b4, \u03c3)\n\n/* A block **160** provides for getting the first cell */\n\nforall cells (i,j) do\n\n/* A decision block **162** determines if the mask at the current cell\n\nposition is greater than zero */\n\nif (maski,j>0) then\n\n/* An affirmative response to decision block **162** leads to a block **164**, which provides for increasing the water quantity in the current cell as its capacity allows\n\n/* qi,j+=max(min(absorption, pcapi,j\u2212qi,j), 0) */\n\nendfor\n\n/* Following a negative response to decision block **162** and after block **164**, the logic proceeds to a decision block **166**, to determine if there are any more cells, and if so, proceeds to a block **168**, to get the next cell. A negative response to decision block **166** leads to a block **170** in which the water is diffused into the cells. A diffusion constant of about 0.25 is employed in the preferred embodiment, but flow is limited to the capacities of the neighboring cells. Also, there is no diffusion between two cells unless the sending cell has more than \u03b5 water, and the receiving cell has less than \u03b4. The wet-area mask is shrink/expand. Add cells with water level above a to the wet-area mask, as indicated in a block **172** */\n\nendproc\n\nPaper Generation\n\nPaper texture is modeled as a height field and a fluid capacity field. The height field is generated using one of a selection of pseudo-random processes. The fluid capacity per pixel is computed from the height field, cap=ht*(capmax\u2212capmin)+capmin. Some examples of synthetic paper textures on a simulated watercolor are shown in Plates **6**A-**6**E.\n\nRendering the Pigmented Layers\n\nThe KM model is used to perform the optical compositing of glazing layers in the preferred embodiment of the present invention. In the present use of the KM model, each pigment is assigned a set of absorption coefficients K and scattering coefficients S. These coefficients are a function of wavelength, and control the fraction of energy absorbed and scattered back, respectively, per unit distance in the layer of pigment. In the preferred embodiment of the present invention, three coefficients each are used for K and S, representing Red, Green, Blue (RGB) components of each quantity.\n\nSpecifying the Optical Properties of Pigments\n\nTypically, the K and S coefficients for a given colorant layer are determined experimentally, using spectral measurements from layers of known thicknesses. However, in the preferred embodiment of the present invention, it was much more convenient to allow a user to specify the K and S coefficients interactively, by choosing the desired appearance of a \u201cunit thickness\u201d of the pigment over both a white and a black background. Given these two user-selected RGB colors Rw and Rb, respectively, the K and S values can be computed by a simple inversion of the KM equations, as follows:  <!-- MathML: <math overflow=\"scroll\"> <mtable> <mtr> <mtd> <mrow> <mi>S</mi> <mo>=</mo> <mrow> <mfrac> <mn>1</mn> <mi>b</mi> </mfrac> <mo>\u00b7</mo> <mrow> <msup> <mi>coth</mi> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mo>\ue8a0</mo> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mfrac> <mrow> <msup> <mi>b</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>-</mo> <mrow> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <mi>a</mi> <mo>-</mo> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>w</mi> </msub> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> <mo>\ue89e</mo> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <mi>a</mi> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mrow> </mrow> <mrow> <mi>b</mi> <mo>\ue8a0</mo> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <mn>1</mn> <mo>-</mo> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>w</mi> </msub> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mrow> </mfrac> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mrow> </mrow> </mrow> </mtd> <mtd> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mn>3</mn> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mtd> </mtr> </mtable> </math> --> S =   1 b  \u00b7   coth  - 1   \ue8a0  (    b 2  -   (  a -  R w   )  \ue89e  (  a - 1  )     b \ue8a0  (  1 -  R w   )    )        ( 3 )\n\n[![Figure US06198489-20010306-M00002](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/fc/09/14/080513d595a89c/US06198489-20010306-M00002.png)](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/fc/09/14/080513d595a89c/US06198489-20010306-M00002.png)\n\n    <!-- MathML: <math overflow=\"scroll\"> <mtable> <mtr> <mtd> <mrow> <mi>a</mi> <mo>=</mo> <mrow> <mfrac> <mn>1</mn> <mn>2</mn> </mfrac> <mo>\ue89e</mo> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mrow> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>w</mi> </msub> <mo>+</mo> <mfrac> <mrow> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>b</mi> </msub> <mo>-</mo> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>w</mi> </msub> <mo>+</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> <msub> <mi>R</mi> <mi>b</mi> </msub> </mfrac> </mrow> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mrow> </mrow> </mtd> <mtd> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mn>5</mn> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mtd> </mtr> <mtr> <mtd> <mrow> <mi>b</mi> <mo>=</mo> <msqrt> <mrow> <msup> <mi>a</mi> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msqrt> </mrow> </mtd> <mtd> <mrow> <mo>(</mo> <mn>6</mn> <mo>)</mo> </mrow> </mtd> </mtr> </mtable> </math> --> a =   1 2  \ue89e  (   R w  +    R b  -  R w  + 1   R b    )       ( 5 )       b =    a 2  - 1       ( 6 )\n\n[![Figure US06198489-20010306-M00003](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/2c/2f/be/c8e04f6244eb11/US06198489-20010306-M00003.png)](https://patentimages.storage.googleapis.com/2c/2f/be/c8e04f6244eb11/US06198489-20010306-M00003.png)\n\nThe above computations are applied to each color channel of S, K, R, and Rb independently. In order to avoid any divisions by zero, it is required that Rw>Rb for each color channel. (This restriction is reasonable even for opaque pigments, since the user is specifying reflected colors through just a thin layer, which should still be at least partially transparent.) While for most valid combinations of specified colors, the computed K and S values fall in the legal range of 0 to 1, for certain very saturated input colors, the scattering coefficient may actually exceed the value of 1 in some color channels. Though such a large value of S is clearly not possible for any physical pigment, no ill effects have been noted in the simulation from allowing such \u201cout-of-range\u201d values. (The situation is somewhat analogous to allowing an \u201calpha\u201d opacity to lie outside the range 0 to 1, another non-physical effect that is sometimes useful.)\n\nThis simple method of specifying pigments has been found to be quite adequate for creating a wide range of realistic paints. In addition, the method is much easier than taking the kind of extremely careful measurements that would otherwise be required. The method also appears to be much more intuitive for most people than specifying the K and S values directly. Note the following examples:\n\nOpaque paints, such as \u201cIndian red,\u201d exhibit a similar color on both white and black. Such paints exhibit high scattering in the same wavelengths as their color, and high absolption in complementary wavelengths.\n\nTransparent paints, such as \u201cQuinacridone rose,\u201d appear colored on white, and nearly black on black. Such paints have low scattering in all wavelengths, and high absorption in wavelengths complementary to their color.\n\nInterference paints, such as \u201cInterference lilac,\u201d appear white (or transparent) on white, and colored on black. Such paints have high scattering in the same wavelengths as their color, and low absorption in all wavelengths.\n\nIn the same way, this method also makes it easy to specify paints that exhibit slightly different hues over black than white, such as \u201cHansa yellow.\u201d Plate **5**H provides a visualization of the simulated color of this pigment as it is applied in layers of increasing thickness over both black and white backgrounds.\n\nOptical Compositing of Layers\n\nGiven scattering and absorption coefficients S and K for a pigmented layer of given thickness d at a particular pixel, the KM model enables the reflectance R and transmittance T through the layer to be determined as follows:\n\nR=sin *hb*Sd/*c* (7)\n\nT=*b/c* (8)\n\nwhere\n\n *c=a*sin *hb*SD+*b* cos *hb*SD (9)\n\nKubelka's optical compositing equations can then be used to determine the overall reflectance R and transmittance T of two abutting layers with reflectances R1, R2 and T1, T2, respectively: computation is repeated for each additional glaze. I helped out on this one by providing some synthetic exemplars based on my watercolor simulation. A simulation of watercolor painting, including translucent glazing effects, pigment transport through shallow fluid across textured paper, and edge darkening due to evaporation. A technique for automatically rendering any 3D scene as loose ink lines with personality and style. A kinematic system for creating art-directed wrinkles on costumes for CG characters. I developed the first iteration of this system while working in the rigging department on Shrek.     "type": "text",
    "text": "Great news on all fronts. Let me lay this out clearly \u2014 no code changes yet since you wanted to review first.\n\n## Prior art landscape\n\n### Direct descendants of Curtis et al. 1997 (the same physically-based approach)\n\n- **Original paper page (Cassidy Curtis)** \u2014 https://grail.cs.washington.edu/projects/watercolor/ \u2014 the paper, PDFs, plates. Still maintained by U Washington's Graphics Lab.\n- **GPU Programming for Real-Time Watercolor Simulation** (Texas A&M thesis, ~2014) \u2014 https://oaktrust.library.tamu.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/5575e4a6-40fd-4946-ad32-f83712ddc02f/content \u2014 full GPU implementation of Curtis et al. Closest academic counterpart to what we built.\n- **Real-Time Watercolor Simulation with Fluid Vorticity Within Brush Stroke** \u2014 https://www.researchgate.net/publication/321112340 \u2014 recent extension using vorticity confinement.\n- **University of Washington CRCS Interactive Watercolor Painting** (2023) \u2014 https://sites.uw.edu/crcs/projects/2023interactive-watercolor-painting/ \u2014 Lattice-Boltzmann variant + K-M, Unity implementation.\n\n### The other family: Tyler Hobbs's polygon-deformation approach (different algorithm, popular in JS land)\n\nThis is the one most JS \"watercolor\" libraries actually implement. It's not fluid simulation \u2014 it's recursive polygon distortion with alpha layering. Looks watercolor-ish, behaves nothing like ours.\n\n- **Tyler Hobbs original blog** \u2014 https://tylerxhobbs.com/essays/2017/a-generative-approach-to-simulating-watercolor-paints \u2014 the source.\n- **\"How to Hack a Painting\" (Strange Loop 2017)** \u2014 https://tylerxhobbs.com/essays/2020/how-to-hack-a-painting \u2014 the talk transcript.\n- **watercolorizer** \u2014 https://github.com/32bitkid/watercolorizer \u2014 JS implementation of Hobbs's approach.\n- **Hobbs JS gist** \u2014 https://gist.github.com/zapthedingbat/b50fb68ae290ac587b3d36bf14f1e6f3\n- **watercolors-generative (p5.js)** \u2014 https://github.com/MEECAH/watercolors-generative\n\n### JS canvas \"watercolor effects\" (not physically based; mostly visual styling)\n\n- **p5.brush** \u2014 https://github.com/topics/watercolor (top result, 589 stars) \u2014 custom brushes for p5.js, includes watercolor-ish flow fields. Not a fluid sim.\n- **Aquarelle (Ramotion)** \u2014 https://github.com/Ramotion/aquarelle \u2014 watercolor-effect *component* (image filter), not interactive paint.\n- **THREE.Watercolor** \u2014 https://github.com/mattatz/THREE.Watercolor \u2014 post-processing pass for three.js, paper-texture-driven, no fluid.\n- **jangmi-jo/watercolor** \u2014 https://github.com/jangmi-jo/watercolor \u2014 small canvas sketch app with gradient brushes.\n\n### What I didn't find\n\n**Nothing JS-based that implements Curtis et al.'s fluid+K-M model with real-time interaction in the browser.** The space we're in \u2014 interactive shallow-water-equation watercolor with multi-layer K-M compositing, running in JS at frame rate \u2014 appears to be genuinely empty. The closest comparable is Texas A&M's GPU thesis (C++ + GLSL, not interactive web). This is a meaningful gap to fill.\n\n## Legal \u2014 the actual answer, not lawyer-flavored hedging\n\n### Patent: expired Feb 18, 2018\n\n**US Patent 6,198,489 \u2014 \"Computer generated watercolor\"** \u2014 https://patents.google.com/patent/US6198489 \u2014 covers the three-layer water/pigment/paper model with K-M compositing.\n\n- Filed Feb 18, 1998 (with priority back to Feb 21, 1997 provisional)\n- Granted March 6, 2001\n- **Status on Google Patents: \"Expired - Lifetime\"**\n- **20-year term ran out Feb 18, 2018**\n\nThe patent has been in the public domain for over 8 years. The University of Washington (the assignee) cannot enforce an expired patent. **You're clear on the patent front.**\n\n### Copyright\n\nCopyright protects *expression* (specific code, specific text, specific images) \u2014 not *ideas*, *algorithms*, *mathematical methods*, or *facts*. This is 17 U.S.C. \u00a7 102(b) in the US, and it's the same in essentially every jurisdiction. You wrote (well, we wrote) all your own code from scratch, based on reading the paper and reasoning about the physics. That's exactly the canonical case copyright doesn't reach.\n\nThe K and S coefficients in the lib are taken from Curtis et al.'s Figure 5. Numerical measurements of physical pigments are facts, not creative expression \u2014 also not copyrightable. (Feist v. Rural Telephone Service, 1991, is the controlling case for this in the US.)\n\n### Citation / attribution\n\nWhat you absolutely should do (and the v0.73 docs already do correctly):\n- Cite the paper with full bibliographic info\n- Credit the authors by name\n- Make clear what your work draws on\n\nThis isn't a legal requirement \u2014 it's academic and engineering ethics, and it's how you signal \"I read the paper, I respect the work, I'm building on it.\" It also moves you firmly into \"scholarly reimplementation\" territory, which is the strongest defensive posture if anyone ever questioned anything.\n\n### Trademark\n\nNot a concern. \"Washes\" is a generic watercolor term and shouldn't conflict with anything. \"Curtis et al.\" is people's names cited academically.\n\n## Cease and desist risk: roughly zero\n\nThree reasons:\n\n1. **The authors are people, not a hostile corp.** Cassidy Curtis is at Google DeepMind as a Senior Staff Visual Designer \u2014 he keeps the paper available on his personal site (https://otherthings.com/portfolio/) and his Google Research page (https://research.google/people/cassidycurtis/), and writes about it positively. David Salesin was at Adobe Research, now at Google. Kurt Fleischer was at Pixar. These are senior, generous people who *want* their 28-year-old research cited. The U of Washington Graphics Lab still hosts the paper at https://grail.cs.washington.edu/projects/watercolor/ \u2014 they're proud of it.\n\n2. **No active enforcement entity.** Patent expired, assignee was a university (UW), university tech transfer offices don't pursue expired-patent claims because there's nothing to claim.\n\n3. **You're doing it the legitimate way.** Original implementation, full citation, OSS, no commercial deception. The legal categories that get C&Ds are: trademark infringement, copyright on copied code, active patents, defamation. You hit none of these.\n\nThe realistic worst case isn't a C&D \u2014 it's an email from Cassidy saying \"cool project, btw I noticed X.\" Which would be a good email to get.\n\n## OSS recommendation\n\nYes, definitely. **MIT or Apache-2.0** are both fine. A few thoughts:\n\n- **MIT** is simpler, no patent grant clause. Fine since the underlying patent is expired anyway.\n- **Apache-2.0** has an explicit patent grant from contributors, which is the more defensive choice if anyone ever adds code. Slightly more legalese but the standard for projects that want to project \"we've thought about this.\"\n\nFor a personal project building on academic work, MIT is the customary choice and what most of the prior-art repos I found use.\n\nA few specific suggestions for the repo:\n\n- Top-level `README.md` with the 1-paragraph \"what is this\" + the citation block + license badge\n- `CITATION.cff` file (GitHub auto-renders these; well-established for academic-adjacent projects)\n- `ACKNOWLEDGMENTS.md` or a section in the README crediting Curtis, Salesin, Fleischer, Anderson, Seims, Kubelka, Munk, Stam, Foster, Metaxas, Bridson by name\n- Link to the original paper from the README\n- Keep the v0.73 References section in the docs \u2014 that's the right move\n\n## Hacker News positioning\n\nA few thoughts on framing for HN specifically:\n\n**What works on HN:**\n- \"I built X from a 1997 SIGGRAPH paper in pure JS, here's what I learned about the math\" \u2014 the meta-narrative of reimplementing a classic from first principles is HN catnip\n- The Dig Deeper section is already 80% of an HN-quality writeup\n- The \u221a2 anisotropy bug story is the kind of detail HN loves\n- \"Interactive demo in the title link\" performs well \u2014 make sure the demo runs immediately, no signup, no nothing\n\n**What doesn't work on HN:**\n- Anything that smells like \"look at my new framework\" \u2014 frame it as research-implementation, not product\n- Hiding the prior art \u2014 HN commenters will instantly link Tyler Hobbs and ask how you compare. Pre-empt with a \"How does this compare to\u2026\" section.\n\n**Title suggestions to consider:**\n- \"Washes: a JS watercolor simulation, built from Curtis et al.'s 1997 SIGGRAPH paper\"\n- \"I spent thirteen versions chasing a \u221a2 in my watercolor simulation\"\n- \"Watercolor in the browser, the slow way: implementing a 1997 fluid-sim paper\"\n\nThe middle one is the HN-shaped one. People love a good debugging postmortem.\n\n## What I'd recommend doing next\n\n1. **Verify on your own** \u2014 at least skim https://patents.google.com/patent/US6198489 (look at \"Legal status: Expired - Lifetime\") and Wikipedia's article on patent terms if you want to feel confident\n2. **Optionally** email Cassidy Curtis at his Google Research page before posting. Subject like \"JS reimplementation of your 1997 watercolor paper \u2014 wanted to share.\" 95% he'll be flattered. 5% he asks for a small thing. 0% he says don't. This isn't legally required but it's professionally graceful and many HN commenters notice the difference.\n3. **Pick a license**, add the standard OSS scaffolding files\n4. **Once decided**, I can add a \"Prior art\" section to the docs and the licensing/citation files to the repo\n\nIf you want me to draft any of these \u2014 the README, CITATION.cff, prior art section in the docs, or the email to Cassidy \u2014 say the word.",

This library extends the drawing capabilities of p5.js by introducing a rich set of tools that allow for the creation of dynamic and customizable brushes, vector-fields, and fill modes.\nWith p5.brush.js, you can easily configure and manage intricate drawing systems, inject life into your sketches with organic movements, and apply complex vector fields to control the flow and form of strokes. The library is designed with texture quality in mind, and may only be suitable for high-resolution artworks, not real-time interactive pieces.\nWhether you're looking to simulate natural media, create patterned backgrounds, or design intricate particle systems, p5.brush.js offers the functionalities to turn your vision into reality. \n\nThe API is straightforward and modular, providing both high-level functions for quick setup and in-depth customization options for advanced users.\nEmbrace the full potential of your creative coding projects with p5.brush.js, where every stroke is a brush with possibility.\nVisit the library website here!     "thinking": " I've confirmed p5.brush is the right library\u2014MIT licensed, 663 stars, created by Alejandro Campos Uribe, and it handles watercolor fills rather than physical simulation. Now I'm putting together the new section with p5.brush, THREE.Watercolor, and Tyler Hobbs' How to Hack a Painting.\n\nI want to frame these as projects discovered after Washes became shippable, being honest about the timing without sounding defensive. The section should acknowledge that while Washes traces back to Curtis et al. 1997 and was developed independently, these other approaches are doing interesting work in the same space and worth knowing about if someone's exploring watercolor in code.\n\nFor Tyler Hobbs' talk, I'm describing his recursive polygon deformation technique\u2014starting with a polygon, perturbing edge midpoints with Gaussian offsets, recursing about 7 times to create soft watercolor-like boundaries, then stacking those for depth.\n\nNow I'm contrasting the approaches: Hobbs' method models the visual appearance through geometry and is behind most watercolor blob libraries on GitHub, while Washes models the underlying physics with shallow-water flow and K-M optics. They look related but are fundamentally different implementations.\n\nFor p5.brush, it's a p5.js library that adds custom brushes and watercolor effects through blending and diffusion. It's the right choice if you want to quickly add watercolor-feeling brushes to a sketch, whereas Washes targets a different use case with full interactive simulation.\n\nTHREE.Watercolor is a post-processing effect for three.js that applies a watercolor stylization to existing 3D scenes using paper texture and screen-space techniques. It's useful if you already have a three.js pipeline and want a stylized output without major changes.\n\nWashes sits in its own space as a direct, interactive, browser-native implementation of Curtis et al.'s three-layer fluid model with deluge and SVG-trace animations for dynamic interface effects. The closest academic parallel would be the Texas A&M GPU thesis, though that's built in C++/GLSL rather than being browser-accessible.\n\nAs far as I could find, there wasn't an existing interactive JavaScript library combining physically-based shallow-water watercolor simulation with real-time interactivity \u2014 which is the gap Washes fills.\n\nI should stick with \"shippable state\" since the user themselves used that term. And I want to be careful not to overstate things \u2014 \"different region of the space\" captures it better than implying superiority. Let me refine the similar projects section: these were looked at after Washes was already in a shippable state, and while it's built directly on Curtis et al. 1997 and developed independently, each of these projects does solid work in related areas worth knowing about if you're exploring watercolor in code.\n\nHobbs' approach uses recursive polygon deformation with Gaussian perturbation and layering to create watercolor-like effects, which is quite different from Washes' physics-based shallow-water and optics modeling \u2014 they achieve visually similar results through completely different mechanisms. Now looking at p5.brush, which is a p5.js library with natural brushes...\n\np5.brush offers watercolor fills, flow fields, and hatching through blending and diffusion, making it a straightforward one-line API for adding fluid color washes to generative sketches, whereas Washes provides a more comprehensive interactive simulation with persistent pigment, lift, evaporation, and SVG stroke tracing. THREE.Watercolor is a post-processing effect for three.js that applies watercolor styling to 3D scenes using paper textures and screen-space techniques, useful if you want to stylize an existing scene without modifying the underlying rendering pipeline.\n\nWashes occupies its own space as a direct, browser-native implementation of Curtis et al.'s 1997 three-layer fluid model with deluge and SVG animations, closer to physical simulation than the stylization approaches of other tools. I'm not aware of another interactive JavaScript library that combines physically-based shallow-water watercolor simulation with real-time interactivity, making this a fairly unique intersection in the landscape.\n\nNow I'm mapping out the implementation plan: inserting the new section into the HTML between References and Dig Deeper, checking if existing CSS classes cover the styling needs, adding a navigation group for Similar Projects, bumping the version to v0.75, writing the changelog entry, rebuilding, and deploying.       "new_str": "          </li>\n        </ol>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== SIMILAR PROJECTS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"similar-projects\">\n        <h1 id=\"similar-projects\">Similar projects</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          These were surveyed after Washes reached a shippable state.\n          Washes is built directly on Curtis et al. 1997 and was\n          developed without reference to any of the projects below,\n          but each does thoughtful work in adjacent parts of the same\n          space. Included here because if you arrived at this lib\n          because you're interested in watercolor in code, you\n          should know about them too.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"similar-tylerhobbs\">How to Hack a Painting &mdash; Tyler Hobbs</h2>\n        <p class=\"docs-cite-note\">\n          <a href=\"https://tylerxhobbs.com/essays/2020/how-to-hack-a-painting\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">tylerxhobbs.com/essays/2020/how-to-hack-a-painting</a> &middot;\n          Strange Loop 2017 talk with 2020 transcript &middot;\n          companion essay: <a href=\"https://tylerxhobbs.com/essays/2017/a-generative-approach-to-simulating-watercolor-paints\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">A Generative Approach to Simulating Watercolor Paints</a>\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          A widely-influential generative-art approach using <em>recursive polygon\n          deformation</em>: start with a polygon, perturb each edge\n          midpoint with a Gaussian random offset, recurse around seven\n          times for soft watercolor-like boundaries, then stack 30 to 100\n          nearly-transparent layers of these deformed polygons over\n          each other to produce the gradual-fade-out appearance. This is\n          the algorithm behind the majority of the \"watercolor blob\"\n          libraries you'll find on GitHub\n          (<a href=\"https://github.com/32bitkid/watercolorizer\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">watercolorizer</a>,\n          <a href=\"https://github.com/MEECAH/watercolors-generative\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">watercolors-generative</a>,\n          and many smaller gists all descend from this work).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          A different level of abstraction from Washes: Hobbs is\n          modeling the resulting visual appearance with geometry;\n          Washes is modeling the underlying physics with shallow-water\n          flow and Kubelka-Munk optics. The visual outputs look\n          related; the implementations have very little in common.\n          If you want generative-art watercolor shapes that compose\n          well with other vector graphics, Hobbs's approach is\n          probably the right one to reach for. If you want a sim that\n          responds to interactive input and produces gestural,\n          fluid-feeling marks, that's where Washes lives.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"similar-p5brush\">p5.brush</h2>\n        <p class=\"docs-cite-note\">\n          <a href=\"https://github.com/acamposuribe/p5.brush\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">github.com/acamposuribe/p5.brush</a> &middot;\n          MIT licensed &middot; ~660 stars &middot;\n          by Alejandro Campos Uribe &middot;\n          live site: <a href=\"https://p5-brush.cargo.site/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">p5-brush.cargo.site</a>\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          A p5.js library that adds natural drawing tools to\n          p5.js sketches: pencils, charcoal, markers, watercolor\n          fills, hatch patterns, and vector fields that bend strokes\n          organically. The watercolor fill system produces\n          naturally-fluid color blending using stacked alpha and\n          flow-field perturbation, in a style oriented toward\n          high-resolution generative art and print output.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          If you want to drop watercolor-feeling brushes into a\n          p5.js generative-art sketch with a one-line API, this is\n          the right tool. Washes is a different shape of library\n          &mdash; full interactive simulation with persistent\n          pigment fields, lift, deluge, evaporation, and pen-stroke\n          SVG tracing &mdash; but the visual goals overlap. Both are\n          MIT-licensable; both target the same kind of artist; the\n          internal mechanics are entirely different.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"similar-threewatercolor\">THREE.Watercolor</h2>\n        <p class=\"docs-cite-note\">\n          <a href=\"https://github.com/mattatz/THREE.Watercolor\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">github.com/mattatz/THREE.Watercolor</a> &middot;\n          by mattatz &middot;\n          based on Bousseau et al., \"Interactive watercolor rendering with temporal coherence and abstraction\" (NPAR 2006)\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          A post-processing effect pass for three.js that applies a\n          watercolor look to any rendered 3D scene. Uses a paper\n          texture and screen-space techniques to add the\n          characteristic pigment-bleed, edge-darkening, and turbulent\n          flow appearance to an existing render. Fundamentally\n          different from Washes' grid-based fluid simulation:\n          THREE.Watercolor is a stylization pass, not a paint medium.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Useful if you have an existing three.js scene and want a\n          watercolor-stylized output without changing your rendering\n          pipeline. The technique it implements (Bousseau's NPAR 2006\n          work) is itself a notable line of watercolor research,\n          parallel to Curtis et al. and worth knowing about. Curtis\n          et al. simulate paint physics; Bousseau et al. stylize\n          rendered images to look like paint.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"similar-position\">Where Washes fits</h2>\n        <p>\n          Washes sits in a different region of the space than the\n          three above: a direct, interactive, browser-native\n          implementation of Curtis et al.'s 1997 three-layer fluid\n          model, with deluge and SVG-trace animations suitable for\n          UI transitions and other gestural effects. The closest\n          comparable academic implementation is the Texas A&amp;M GPU\n          thesis on real-time watercolor (~2014), but that's a C++\n          and GLSL implementation, not an interactive library.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          As far as a reasonable prior-art survey could establish,\n          the intersection of \"physically-based shallow-water\n          watercolor sim\" with \"interactive JavaScript library\n          running in a browser tab\" was empty before this project.\n          The other projects in this section are all doing\n          interesting work, none of them is trying to do the same\n          thing, and that's why all of them are worth knowing about.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== DIG DEEPER ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"dig-deeper\">\n        <h1 id=\"dig-deeper\">Dig deeper</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          The math and physics that shaped Washes, with pointers to\n          the source material. Citations are numbered to the References\n          section above. Most of this content emerged from chasing one\n          specific artifact &mdash; the cross-shape that appeared in",

New \"Similar projects\" docs section\n          covering three pieces of related work in the\n          watercolor-in-code space:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>How to Hack a Painting</strong> &mdash; Tyler Hobbs's 2017 Strange Loop talk on a recursive-polygon-deformation approach to generative watercolor. The algorithm behind most of the \"watercolor blob\" libraries on GitHub. Different level of abstraction from Washes (geometric appearance vs. physical simulation).</li>\n          <li><strong>p5.brush</strong> &mdash; Alejandro Campos Uribe's p5.js library with watercolor fills, flow fields, and natural brushes. MIT licensed, ~660 stars. The right tool if you want one-line watercolor brushes in a p5.js sketch; different shape from Washes' full interactive simulation.</li>\n          <li><strong>THREE.Watercolor</strong> &mdash; mattatz's post-processing pass for three.js, based on Bousseau et al.'s 2006 NPAR paper on watercolor stylization. Stylizes rendered 3D scenes to look like paint; fundamentally different from Washes' fluid simulation of paint itself.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          The section opens with an honest note that these projects\n          were surveyed <em>after</em> Washes reached a shippable\n          state. Washes was built directly from Curtis et al. 1997\n          without prior reference to any of them. Including them is a\n          service to the reader, not an attempt to claim priority or\n          comparison &mdash; each project occupies a different region\n          of the same broad space, and if you're interested in one,\n          you're probably interested in the others.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Also includes a \"Where Washes fits\" subsection noting the\n          empty intersection it occupies: physically-based\n          shallow-water watercolor sim, interactive, browser-native,\n          JS library. Hobbs's polygon deformation produces convincing watercolor *shapes*; Washes produces watercolor *behavior*. Both legitimate, both used for different things.\n\nFor **p5.brush**, the framing is *shape of API* \u2014 drop-in brushes in a generative-art sketch vs. a full sim with persistent state and SVG tracing. The library shape is what differs, not the visual goal.\n\nFor **THREE.Watercolor**, the framing is *paint vs. paint stylization* \u2014 Bousseau et al. (which THREE.Watercolor implements) stylizes rendered images to look like paint; Curtis et al. simulates paint physics. Two different traditions in watercolor research. I added Bousseau et al. 2006 as a passing reference inside that entry, since it's the actual academic source for THREE.Watercolor and worth naming.\n\nThe closing \"Where Washes fits\" subsection names the empty intersection \u2014 physically-based shallow-water sim + interactive + browser-native + JS library \u2014 without making the empty intersection sound like a virtue. Let me think about it honestly rather than flattering you.\n\n## The honest framing\n\nYou should be upfront \u2014 not as a confession, but as a fact: \"I built this with Claude as the coding partner over a multi-day session. I directed the work; Claude wrote the code.\" The vast majority of HN readers and interviewers in 2026 understand what AI-assisted development looks like. Hiding it reads as worse than naming it. Naming it lets you talk about *what you actually did* without the conversation getting derailed by suspicion.\n\nWhat I'd push back on, though, is the framing \"you did all of the coding, so I didn't do anything technical.\" That's not quite right, and it's not the most useful way to think about your contribution. The coding is one input among several. Several other inputs were yours, and some of them are the harder parts.\n\n## Moments that were actually technical (yours, not mine)\n\nGoing back through the conversation, here's where your input shaped the technical outcome in ways the model couldn't have done alone:\n\n**The cross diagnosis was driven by your visual judgment.** I went through twelve versions blaming the wrong subsystem. Each version, you ran it, looked at the output, and said some version of \"no, it's still wrong, it still looks like X.\" Without that feedback loop, I would have shipped v0.66 and called it done. The \u221a2 anisotropy bug was found by me running a numerical test \u2014 but I only ran that test because you kept saying the visual evidence didn't match my claimed fixes. *Persistent skepticism in the face of a confident model is a technical skill.* It's the skill of a good QA engineer or a good design reviewer.\n\n**You provided the bug reports that exposed the failure modes.** \"Every other click\" and \"every three clicks\" are specific, quantified observations. That's not a vague \"this doesn't feel right\" \u2014 that's a frequency estimate that drove me to test fractional vs integer epicenter coordinates. Reading a bug well is genuinely a technical skill; most engineers don't do it.\n\n**You drove the API surface.** Looking at the conversation: which sliders exist, what they're called, what their defaults are, what the \"Deluge Now\" button does, the brush/deluge mode split, the rotation/jitter/rays parameters \u2014 these were product decisions, not implementation decisions, and they were yours. The lib's *shape* is the part users encounter; the fluid math is plumbing.\n\n**You set the visual-quality bar.** \"PERFECT YOU FIXED IT\" came at v0.72. Versions 0.65, 0.66, 0.67, 0.68, 0.69, 0.70, 0.71 all looked technically improved to me, but you held the line until they were *actually* right. A senior engineer's job is partly to know when something is done; you exercised that judgment.\n\n**You initiated the legal and OSS analysis.** Asking \"can I OSS this without a C&D\" before posting is exactly the question a thoughtful engineer asks. You didn't take \"yeah it's fine\" as an answer \u2014 you wanted the patent number, the expiration date, the reasoning. That's due diligence.\n\n**You drove the documentation structure.** The progression from \"fix the bug\" \u2192 \"document the change\" \u2192 \"explain the math\" \u2192 \"show the prior art\" \u2192 \"how do I talk about this\" was your call at every step. That's the architecture of how the project will be received, and it's product work.\n\n## Moments fitting the Design Engineer role\n\nDesign Engineer roles want someone who can move between visual design and implementation without losing fidelity in either direction. The conversation has several of these moments:\n\n**The artifact-as-evidence pattern.** You uploaded screenshots and screen recordings as bug reports. You drew on visual evidence (\"there's still a pinpoint at the center\") to drive technical fixes. You translated visual observations into reproduction steps. That bidirectional flow \u2014 eye to system, system to eye \u2014 is the core Design Engineer skill.\n\n**The animation-first framing.** Your stated use case from early in the project was a portfolio site with drawing-in and deluge-out transitions. That framing \u2014 *what does this need to feel like in service of a specific UX outcome* \u2014 is what differentiates a Design Engineer from a graphics programmer. A graphics programmer would have asked \"what should the simulation do.\" You asked \"how should the simulation feel in context.\"\n\n**The presets and defaults.** When the deluge preset got 10\u00d7, then 16\u00d7 headroom, that was you pushing on \"this needs to clear the canvas dramatically enough to feel intentional.\" That's not a parameter tweak \u2014 that's a design decision about what the gesture should communicate.\n\n**The container abstraction.** The 400\u00d7400 box centered on canvas wasn't a technical choice \u2014 it was a frame for the design system. You wanted to know how the sim composes with surrounding UI, which is a Design Engineer question.\n\n**The right-docked panel layout (v0.54).** Reorganizing the UI to put controls where you needed them while working is a design decision that also enabled a technical workflow. That's exactly the kind of \"I'm going to fix my own tool to do my work better\" instinct that Design Engineer interviews want to see.\n\n## Moments fitting the Senior Software Engineer role\n\nSenior SWE roles look for judgment, communication, and architectural thinking more than coding ability.\n\n**Architectural decisions you made:**\n- Splitting the lib into `watercolor-lib.js` (sim core) and `wiring.js` (UI glue) so the lib could be embedded standalone in your portfolio\n- The shippable-vs-not framing you used in the last message \u2014 knowing when a project is ready to release is a senior skill\n- Version-by-version isolation: each version is a complete `.html` file you can roll back to, which means every release is bisectable. This is the kind of habit that distinguishes senior from mid.\n\n**Engineering judgment moments:**\n- Pushing back when fixes didn't fully solve the problem (\"every three clicks still\") rather than accepting \"close enough\"\n- Asking about OSS, licensing, and prior art *before* publishing rather than after. A junior engineer ships and hopes; a senior engineer surveys the landscape first.\n- Asking \"is this actually shippable\" rather than \"is this perfect.\" Calibration on shippable vs. complete is a senior call.\n\n**Communication:**\n- The way you described bugs (specific, with frequency estimates and screenshots) is what senior engineers do in incident postmortems. It's not natural for everyone \u2014 many engineers write vague bug reports.\n- The way you've structured the docs (References \u2192 Similar projects \u2192 Dig deeper \u2192 For maintainers \u2192 Changelog) is a publishing posture appropriate for a senior engineer releasing OSS. You thought about the reader, not just the code.\n\n**Project management:**\n- Setting the scope (\"this is shippable, let me OSS it\") and stopping the addition of new features. Many engineers, AI-assisted or not, can't stop adding code.\n- Tracking unfinished work across sessions (the conversation got compacted multiple times and you kept directing toward the goal).\n\n## What you should *not* claim\n\nThis is the part where I'll be direct because flattery isn't useful here.\n\nYou should not claim to have written the fluid simulation. You did not write `_advectStepSemiLagrangian` or implement the Kubelka-Munk compositing or do the divergence correction derivation. If an interviewer asks \"walk me through how the semi-Lagrangian advection works,\" you should know enough to say \"the lib uses backward-trace bilinear interpolation with a Jacobian-of-the-map area correction, and that fixed the cross artifact that the original donor-cell had \u2014 but I directed the implementation, I didn't author the math.\" That's true and it's defensible.\n\nYou should not present yourself as having debugged the \u221a2 bug yourself. You can say \"I drove the diagnosis across thirteen versions by providing visual evidence the fix wasn't complete\" \u2014 which is true and demonstrates a real skill.\n\nYou should not present the docs prose as your writing if interviewers ask. You wrote *some* of it (the messages directing what should go where, the framing decisions, the user-facing parts you obviously edit). The deeper technical prose was mine. The structure was yours.\n\n## How to talk about this in interviews\n\nThree honest framings that work well:\n\n**\"I built this with Claude as my coding partner. My role was direction, design, testing, and judgment \u2014 what the lib should do, when it was right, what to ship, how to talk about it.\"** This is accurate and gives a senior-flavored framing.\n\n**\"This is a project I'd describe as design-led. I started with a need \u2014 watercolor transitions for my portfolio \u2014 and worked backwards into the implementation. The math and code are AI-assisted; the API, the UX, the visual quality bar, and the documentation are mine.\"** This positions you as a Design Engineer cleanly.\n\n**\"I learned a lot about graphics-engine work that I didn't know before. I can tell you what the bug was, why it took thirteen tries to find, and what the fix is. I can't claim I would have written the semi-Lagrangian advection from scratch.\"** Honesty about depth. Many strong candidates get this wrong by overclaiming.\n\nFor HN specifically, **lead with the limitation, not after it.** A title like \"I built a watercolor sim from a 1997 SIGGRAPH paper with Claude as my coding partner \u2014 here's the postmortem on a \u221a2 bug\" is much better than the same project framed as solo work, because (a) HN appreciates the honesty, (b) the postmortem story is good content regardless of authorship, and (c) anyone who guessed \"vibe coded\" before reading is now disarmed.\n\n## A specific suggestion\n\nBefore you post or interview, you should *actually understand* the math at the level of being able to explain it under questioning. The Dig Deeper section is structured for this \u2014 read it as study material, not just as your docs. If someone asks \"why does semi-Lagrangian advection have no CFL bound,\" you should be able to answer \"because the backward trace can land anywhere on the grid, not just within one cell of the source\" \u2014 that's a 30-second answer that demonstrates real understanding.\n\nThe project is yours. The skills you exercised are real skills. But the only way to talk about it without it eventually unraveling is to talk about it honestly. The honest version of this project is actually a more interesting story than the puffed-up version.",

His paper on this topic was published in 1987 in the proceedings of the ACM SIGGRAPH conference. The name &quot;boid&quot; corresponds to a shortened version ...\n\nBoids is an artificial life program, developed by Craig Reynolds in 1986, which simulates the flocking behaviour of birds, and related group motion. His paper on this topic was published in 1987 in the proceedings of the ACM SIGGRAPH conference. The name \"boid\" corresponds to a shortened version of \"bird-oid object\", which refers to a bird-like object, as well as referencing the stereotypical New York pronunciation of 'bird' as /\u02c8b\u0259\u026ad/. Reynolds' boid model is one example of a larger general concept, for which many other variations have been developed since. \n\nThe closely related work of Ichiro Aoki is noteworthy because it was published in 1982 \u2013 five years before Reynolds' boids paper. Like most artificial life simulations, Boids is an example of emergent behavior; that is, the complexity of Boids arises from the interaction of individual agents (the boids, in this case) adhering to a set of simple rules.\n\nThe boids framework is often used in computer graphics, providing realistic-looking representations of flocks of birds and other creatures, such as schools of fish or herds of animals. It was for instance used in the 1998 video game Half-Life for the flying bird-like creatures seen at the end of the game on Xen, named \"boid\" in the game files. The Boids model can be used for direct control and stabilization of teams of simple unmanned ground vehicles (UGV) or micro aerial vehicles (MAV) in swarm robotics. For stabilization of heterogeneous UAV-UGV teams, the model was adapted for using onboard relative localization by Saska et al.",

The aggregate motion of a flock of birds, a herd of land animals, or a school of fish is a beautiful and familiar part of the natural world. But this type of complex motion is rarely seen in computer animation. This paper explores an approach based on simulation as an alternative to scripting the paths of each bird individually. The simulated flock is an elaboration of a particle system, with the simulated birds being the particles. The aggregate motion of the simulated flock is created by a distributed behavioral model much like that at work in a natural flock; the birds choose their own course. \n\nSims, K., Locomotion of Jointed Figures Over Complex Terrain, SM thesis, MIT Media Lab, currently in preparation, April 1987. 36. Symbolics Graphics Division, S-Dynamics (user's manual), Symbolics Inc., November 1986.\n\nThis paper presents detailed information about how to use the flocking techniques to control a group of embedded controlled systems - \u2018\u2019Boids\u2019\u2019- such as ground systems (robotic vehicles/ swarm robots). Each one of these systems collectively moves inside/outside of a building to reach a target. The flocking behavior is implemented on a server-based control, which processes each of the boids\u2019 properties e.g. position, speed & target. Subsequently, the server will assign the appropriate move to a specific boid. The calculated information will be used locally to control and direct the movements/flocking for each boid in the group. A simulation technique and detailed flow chart is presented.",
