        "text": "Perlin noise is a type of gradient noise that was introduced and published by Ken Perlin in his paper An image synthesizer in 1985[1]. Perlin further improved the noise function in 2002, where he introduced the simplex noise[2]. Perlin noise significantly impacted computer-generated imagery, ...\n\nPerlin noise is a type of gradient noise that was introduced and published by Ken Perlin in his paper An image synthesizer in 1985[1]. Perlin further improved the noise function in 2002, where he introduced the simplex noise[2]. Perlin noise significantly impacted computer-generated imagery, graphics and visualization, and it is still actively used in procedural texture and terrain generation today (see Minecraft as the most famous example of this). In this project, I reproduced the theoretical basics of the original classic algorithm for 2D Perlin noise generation that I will present in the following sections. \n\nAs an exciting addendum, I derived a gradient field from the created noise map, interpreted it as a force field, and simulated the movement of particles on it. Finally, I visualized their trajectories in a creative/artistic manner. Originally, I implemented the Perlin noise generation and particle tracing using C++17, while the visualizations and data analysis pipeline were made\n\nPerlin\u2019s papers (from 1985 and 2002) and his code implementation were quite laconic. His uncommented and concise example code only demonstrates the principle, while his papers are also short-spoken. While it can be definitely explained more thoroughly in detail, I think a visual approach to Perlin noise generation is a much better way to understand every detail of the algorithm. In this section, I will present the noise generation process step-by-step, using simple and easy-to-understand visualizations.",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Perlin noise is a type of\n[gradient noise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gradient_noise) developed by [Ken Perlin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ken_Perlin) in 1982. It has many uses, including but not limited to: [procedurally generating terrain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scenery_generator), applying [pseudo-random](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pseudo-random) changes to a variable, and assisting in the creation of [image textures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Image_texture). It is most commonly implemented in two, three, or four [dimensions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dimension), but can be defined for any number of dimensions. [Ken Perlin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ken_Perlin) developed Perlin noise in 1982 as a result of his frustration with the \"machine-like\" look of [computer-generated imagery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery) (CGI) at the time. \n\n[[1]](./Perlin_noise#cite_note-ken_powerpoint-1) He formally described his findings in a [SIGGRAPH](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SIGGRAPH) paper in 1985 called \"An Image Synthesizer\". [[2]](./Perlin_noise#cite_note-Perlin:1985:IS:325165.325247-2) He developed it after working on [Disney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disney)'s [computer animated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_animated) [sci-fi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sci-fi) motion picture [Tron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tron) (1982) for the animation company [Mathematical Applications Group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematical_Applications_Group) (MAGI). \n\n[simplex noise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simplex_noise) for [texture synthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Texture_synthesis). Simplex noise has the same purpose, but uses a simpler space-filling grid. Simplex noise alleviates some of the problems with Perlin's \"classic noise\", among them computational complexity and visually-significant directional artifacts. [[8]](./Perlin_noise#cite_note-8)\nPerlin noise is a\n[procedural texture](https://e",

We also believe that recent trends in hard- ware justify the need to take a fresh look at procedural noise. Since 1985, compute speed has increased much faster than \u00b7 memory bandwidth. In a sense, we can now consider that cy- cles are free, in reference to the fact that most programs in \u00b7 today\u2019s architectures spend a large amount of their time wait- ing for cache misses and other kinds of memory access.         "text": "It&#x27;s either a bump or a dent or an &quot;S&quot; like shape between two consecutive lattice points. As a result, the distribution of frequencies in the Perlin noise is more regular than the value noise&#x27;s frequency spectrum (in particular, it removes the low frequencies you can find in the latter).\n\nThis is the second part of a series of lessons devoted to generating procedural noise patterns. If you are not familiar with the subject yet, we recommend you to read the lesson Value Noise and Procedural Pattern: Part1 first. In 1985, Ken Perlin wrote a Siggraph paper called \"An Image Synthesizer\", in which he presented a type of noise function similar to the one we studied in the previous lesson (Noise Part 1) but slightly better. This noise function was improved and extended over the years, primarily by Ken Perlin and others. We will study some of these improvements in this lesson. This noise function is now known under the name of Perlin Noise.",

        "title": "Chapter 5. Implementing Improved Perlin Noise | NVIDIA Developer",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "\"Anatomical Origin and Computational Role of Diversity in the Response Properties of Cortical Neurons.\" In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 7, edited by G. Tesauro, D. S. Touretzky, and T. K. Leen. MIT Press. Perlin, K. 1985.\n\nGiven a Noise implementation with a statistically even distribution of gradient directions, it is fairly straightforward to build procedural expressions that create complex-looking textures. For example, Figure 5-6 shows four successively more complex texture expressions built from Noise. Figure 5-6 Four Texture Expressions Built from Noise \u00b7 Moving clockwise from the top left, these expressions are: In each case, color splines have been applied as a postprocess to tint the results. The improvements to the Noise function are in two specific areas: the nature of the interpolant and the nature of the field of pseudo-random gradients. \n\nAs I discussed in Perlin 2002, the key to improving the interpolant is simply to remove second-order discontinuities. The original cubic interpolant 3t 2 - 2t 3 was chosen because it has a derivative of zero both at t = 0 and at t = 1.\n\nThe initial implementation, first used in 1983 and first published in 1985 (Perlin 1985), defined Noise at any point (x, y, z) by using the following algorithm: At each point in space (i, j, k) that has integer coordinates, assign a value of zero and a pseudo-random gradient that is hashed from (i, j, k). Define the coordinates of (x, y, z) as an integer value plus a fractional remainder: (x, y, z) = (i + u, j + v, k + w). Consider the eight corners of the unit cube surrounding this point: (i, j, k), (i + 1, j, k), . . . (i + 1, j + 1, k + 1). Fit a Hermite spline through these eight points, and evaluate this spline at (x, y, z), using u, v, and w as interpolants.",

I presented this work first at a course in SIGGRAPH 84, and then as a paper in SIGGRAPH 85. Because the techniques were so simple, they quickly got adopted throughout the industry. By around 1988 noise-based shaders where de rigeur in commercial software. I didn&#x27;t patent. As my grandmother would have said...         "text": "The method proved particularly ... Perlin first formally introduced the algorithm in his 1985 SIGGRAPH paper, &quot;An Image Synthesizer,&quot; presented at the Association for Computing Machinery&#x27;s annual conference on computer ...\n\nBy providing a repeatable yet seemingly random pattern, the noise function facilitated efficient texture synthesis directly within rendering pipelines.[11] Initial implementations of Perlin noise were primarily in two dimensions, applied to bump mapping and shading techniques to add surface detail and lighting variation in film visuals. These early uses focused on enhancing the perceptual realism of CGI elements, such as metallic surfaces or organic forms, in production environments like movie visual effects. \n\nThe method proved particularly valuable for its computational efficiency and controllability, enabling artists to layer and scale textures dynamically.[9] Perlin first formally introduced the algorithm in his 1985 SIGGRAPH paper, \"An Image Synthesizer,\" presented at the Association for Computing Machinery's annual conference on computer graphics. In this work, he described the noise as a foundational building block for synthesizing complex images from simpler procedural components\n\n\n\nPerlin noise is a gradient noise function and procedural texture generation algorithm invented by Ken Perlin in 1982 while working on visual effects for the film Tron, and formally described in his 1985 SIGGRAPH paper \"An Image Synthesizer.\"[1][2] It produces smooth, continuous pseudo-random values that mimic natural organic patterns, making it a foundational tool in computer graphics for creating realistic textures without relying on stored images.[3][4] The algorithm operates by defining a regular lattice grid in one, two, or three dimensions, where each integer lattice point is assigned a random gradient vector.[3] For a given point in space, the noise value is computed by finding the surrounding lattice points, calculating dot products betwe",

        "text": "In the original diffusion-limited ... are added, one at a time, to a growing cluster or aggregate of particles via random walk paths starting from outside of the region occupied by the cluster....\n\nIn the original diffusion-limited aggregated (DLA) model of Witten and Sander (1981) \u2018particles\u2019, represented by lattice sites, are added, one at a time, to a growing cluster or aggregate of particles via random walk paths starting from outside of the region occupied by the cluster. In the most simple version of the model (Witten and Sander, 1981), a simulation is started by occupying a site in the center of a square or triangular lattice to represent the \u2018seed\u2019, \u2018growth site\u2019 or \u2018nucleation site\u2019. A site far from the cluster is then selected and a random walk is started from the selected site. If the random walker moves too far from the growing cluster, it is terminated and a new random walk is started. \n\nIf the random walker eventually reaches a site that is the nearest neighbor to a previously occupied site, the random walk is stopped and the unoccupied perimeter site (the last site occupied by the random walker) is filled to represent the growth process.\n\nWitten, T.A. and Sander, L.M., 1981, Diffusion-limited aggregation, a kinetic critical phenomenon, Physical Review Letters 51, 1400\u20131403. ... Witten, T.A. and Sander, L.M., 1983, Diffusion-limited aggregation, Physical Review B27, 5686\u20135697. ... Meakin, P. (1999). The Diffusion-Limited Aggregation Model and Geological Pattern Formation. In: Jamtveit, B., Meakin, P. (eds) Growth, Dissolution and Pattern Formation in Geosystems. Springer, Dordrecht. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-94-015-9179-9_8 ... Sorry, a shareable link is not currently available for this article. ... Chernova\n\nLow Temperature Physics Department, Moscow State University, Moscow 119899, Russia\n\n(Received 16 August 1999; revised manuscript received 30 December 1999)\n\nWe propose a revision of the classic mean-field approach of diffusion-limited aggregation (DLA) model originally introduced by Witten and Sander [Phys. Rev. Lett. 47, 1400 (1981)]. The derived nonlinear mean-field equations providing lattice anisotropy are used to model diffusional growth on square lattice in linear and circular source geometries. The overall cluster shapes obtained from the mean-field calculations are found to satisfy the known scaling behavior experimentally observed for DLA simulations.\n\n## PACS number(s): 68.70.+w, 61.43.Hv, 05.10.Ln\n\n#### I. INTRODUCTION\n\n\n\nThe well-known member of the class of stochastic models simulating the Laplacian systems is the diffusion-limited aggregation (DLA) introduced by Witten and Sander [1]. In this theory, self-similar (fractal) ramified patterns grow via irreversible sticking of random \"walkers.\" Despite the simplicity of the DLA rules, the model shows unexpectedly subtle and complex properties and poses a number of theoretical questions such as noise reduction, influence of lattice anisotropy, free-boundary problem, and asymptotic behavior [2\u201311].\n\nIn order to characterize an ensemble-averaged behavior of the DLA model, various mean-field theories (MFT) have been proposed and developed. MFT is a set of evolution equations illustrating a general continuous formulation for the time development of growing clusters. Constructing of a MFT raises the following main problem: how can be realized the correct transition from discrete units to finite walker and cluster distributions? \n\nA first attempt to establish a mean-field approach for the DLA model goes back to the pioneering work by Witten and Sander [1].         "text": "# Diffusion-limited aggregation: A relationship between surface thermodynamics and crystal morphology\n\nVladislav A. Bogoyavlenskiy\\* and Natasha A. Chernova\n\nLow Temperature Physics Department, Moscow State University, Moscow 119899, Russia\n\n(Received 23 June 1999)\n\nWe have combined the original diffusion-limited aggregation model introduced by Witten and Sander with the surface thermodynamics of the growing solid aggregate. The theory is based on the consideration of the surface chemical potential as a thermodynamic function of the temperature and nearest-neighbor configuration. The Monte Carlo simulations on a two-dimensional square lattice produce the broad range of shapes such as fractal dendritic structures, densely branching patterns, and compact aggregates. The morphology diagram illustrating the relationship between the model parameters and cluster geometry is presented and discussed.\n\nPACS number(s): 81.10.Aj, 02.70.Lq, 68.70.+w, 82.65.Dp\n\n### I. INTRODUCTION\n\n\n\nDiffusion-limited growth processes occur in a broad range of interesting systems ranging from physics to chemistry, material science, and biology [1\u20136]. The common features that we observe in these systems are the formation of complex interfacial structures that vary from compact to fractal [7,8]. The growth processes are described by nonlinear partial-differential equations and both the analytical and the numerical treatments of these equations are extremely difficult even on current computers [9,10]. As a result, many of the questions concerning structure formation and transitions between different growth morphologies have not so far been satisfactorily answered. Much effort has especially been devoted to establishing the relationship between cluster morphology and the growth mechanism.\n\nThe diffusion-limited aggregation (DLA) model introduced by Witten and Sander in 1981 [11] has attracted much attention because of the variety of growth shapes that it can produce [12,13]. \n\nMonte Carlo (MC) sim",

At the beginning of the model, one particle is fixed at \u00b7 the d-dimensional coordinate ...\n\nWitten and Sander proposed the diffusion limited aggregation (DLA) model in 1981. Numerous pattern- forming processes, including as dielectric breakdown, two-fluid flow, and electrochemical deposition, have been proven to be explained by the model. The model starts with one particle fixed at the origin of \u00b7 coordinates in d dimensions. Next, a cluster is created by releasing a random walker from infinity, which \u00b7 is then allowed to roam until it collides with any particle in the cluster. Once there, the new particle is \u00b7 let loose from infinity and joined to the expanding cluster. \n\nThe model was investigated both on and off \u00b7 the lattice in multiple dimensions d 2; DLA has garnered a lot of attention throughout the years as a \u00b7 stunning illustration of fractal objects [1-20].This is a research report on diffusion-limited Aggregation. Diffusion-limited aggregation is a process in which particles of matter stick together (aggregate) as they \u00b7\n\nThe diffusion limited aggregation (DLA) model was presented by Witten and Sander in 1981. The model \u00b7 has been shown to describe a wide range of pattern-forming processes, such as dielectric breakdown, two-fluid flow, and electrochemical deposition. At the beginning of the model, one particle is fixed at \u00b7 the d-dimensional coordinate system's origin. A random walker is then let loose from infinity to build a \u00b7 cluster, after which it is permitted to roam until it collides with any particle within the cluster. The new \u00b7 particle is then released from infinity and added to the growing cluster once it has arrived there.",

It's cheap to compute, deeply satisfying to interact with, and opens up a bunch of UX possibilities: hanging elements, draggable decorations, cloth-like backgrounds, breakable strings.\n\nFor procedural patterns, reaction-diffusion equations from Turing and Gray-Scott are hypnotic\u2014two coupled PDEs that produce patterns identical to coral spots, leopard stripes, zebra patterns, fingerprints. Nothing happens for the first 500 frames, then organic patterns suddenly emerge and become impossible to look away from. Works beautifully as a background or loading animation. L-systems from Lindenmayer are another direction entirely\u2014recursive grammars that grow trees, ferns, flowers with that unmistakable organic branching structure.\n\nPerlin noise is foundational for naturalistic procedural textures, and when combined with flow fields where particles follow the noise gradient, it creates gorgeous organic motion with minimal math. Diffusion-limited aggregation is another fascinating approach\u2014random particles that stick together produce frost, lichen, electric arcs, and river deltas through pure simulation.\n\nFor behavioral systems, Reynolds' boids algorithm is elegant: just three rules per agent (separation, alignment, cohesion) generate convincing flocking behavior that's perfect for interactive UI elements like cursor-following swarms or decorative particle systems. Then there's Wave Function Collapse from 2016, which is a newer approach...\n\nActually, thinking about how to package these for a portfolio\u2014I could combine them into a page transition library where content leaves with a particle effect, assembles with boid behavior on entry, and grows the next page's text using L-systems. The novelty is in the polish, not the algorithm.\n2. **L-systems** as a drawing tool \u2014 let users design their own trees by editing the grammar in a panel and watching it grow. Genuinely educational and beautiful.\n3. **Reaction-diffusion** as a fabric/wallpaper designer \u2014 let users seed patterns and watch them evolve. Surprisingly few apps do this well.\n\nAnything in this list could be a portfolio piece. The key insight from Washes is: pick something with naturalistic / surprising output, implement it carefully, document the journey honestly, and you have something HN-shaped.\n\nI'm noticing the real pattern here \u2014 these projects work because they combine elegant old math with surprising visual results, run efficiently in a browser, and come with a built-in narrative arc that resonates with the HN audience.   {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T03:21:37.729557Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-13T03:22:07.422459Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Yes \u2014 there's a whole genre of papers in this shape: old, elegant math + naturalistic output + small enough to fit in a browser. Here are the ones I'd reach for, grouped by flavor.\n\n## Fluids and physics (closest to Washes)\n\n**Stam, \"Stable Fluids\" (SIGGRAPH 1999)** \u2014 you've already met this one in v0.67. As a *standalone* interactive, it's spectacular: 2D fluid sandbox where the cursor injects velocity and dye, smoke swirls in real time. Jos Stam's original C code is famously ~200 lines. The interaction feels magical and people instinctively keep dragging the cursor. Could be a portfolio hero element. Paper: https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~chaol/data/cs777/stam-stable_fluids.pdf\n\n**Jakobsen, \"Advanced Character Physics\" (GDC 2001)** \u2014 Verlet integration for ropes, chains, cloth, soft bodies. Drag any vertex of a piece of cloth and the rest deforms naturally. The math is just \"remember last frame's position, do `new = 2\u00b7now - last + acceleration\u00b7dt\u00b2`, then enforce distance constraints between points.\" 50-100 lines. Endless UX uses: hanging UI elements, draggable decorations, cloth-like backgrounds, breakable chains. https://www.cs.cmu.edu/afs/cs/academic/class/15462-s13/www/lec_slides/Jakobsen.pdf\n\n## Procedural patterns (gorgeous, slow-building, naturalistic)\n\n**Turing, \"The Chemical Basis of Morphogenesis\" (Phil. Trans. Royal Society 1952)** \u2014 the foundation. Two coupled diffusion equations with feedback produce the patterns of coral, leopard spots, zebra stripes, fingerprints, seashells. Karl Sims' [Reaction-Diffusion Tutorial](https://www.karlsims.com/rd.html) is the canonical browser-shaped intro. Slow-building animation: looks like nothing for ~500 frames, then organic patterns emerge and you can't look away. Beautiful as a generative wallpaper or as a slow page transition.\n\n**Lindenmayer, \"Mathematical models for cellular interactions in development\" (J. Theor. Biology 1968)** \u2014 L-systems. Recursive grammar that produces trees, ferns, flowers, river networks. The canonical reference is Prusinkiewicz & Lindenmayer's *The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants* (1990), now a [free PDF](http://algorithmicbotany.org/papers/abop/abop.pdf) \u2014 one of the most beautiful CS books ever published. Branching structures that grow on demand are incredible as animated decorative elements.\n\n**Perlin, \"An Image Synthesizer\" (SIGGRAPH 1985)** \u2014 gradient noise, the algorithm underlying basically all naturalistic procedural texture in computer graphics since. Combined with flow fields (particles following the gradient), produces gorgeous organic motion. Three lines of math, four decades of influence. https://www.red3d.com/cwr/papers/1987/SIGGRAPH87.pdf is the Reynolds reference; Perlin's paper is at https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/325165.325247.\n\n**Witten & Sander, \"Diffusion-Limited Aggregation\" (Phys. Rev. Lett. 1981)** \u2014 random walk + sticking = frost, lichen, electric arcs, river deltas. The algorithm is literally: \"particles do a random walk until they touch the cluster, then freeze.\" Produces stunning fractal patterns. A few lines of code. Could be a \"growing-on-hover\" effect on portfolio cards. Paper at https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.47.1400.\n\n## Agents and behavior\n\n**Reynolds, \"Flocks, Herds, and Schools\" (SIGGRAPH 1987)** \u2014 boids. Three rules per agent \u2014 separation, alignment, cohesion \u2014 produce convincing flocks, schools, herds. The original 1987 paper is online with full pseudocode at https://www.red3d.com/cwr/papers/1987/. Endlessly useful for portfolio sites: cursor-following swarms, decorative birds across the top of a page, particle systems that react to mouse position. The implementation is small; the design space is huge.\n\n## Sound (an underexplored direction)\n\n**Karplus & Strong, \"Digital Synthesis of Plucked-String...\" (Computer Music Journal 1983)** \u2014 physically-modeled plucked strings using a delay line + averaging filter. About 20 lines of JS, produces beautiful realistic guitar/harp/sitar sounds depending on parameters. With Web Audio API this is browser-native. Click any UI element, hear a satisfying note. The space of \"websites that have thoughtful sound design\" is mostly empty \u2014 this would be a differentiator. Tone.js has Karplus-Strong as a built-in primitive if you want to skip the implementation. Paper: https://www.jstor.org/stable/3680062.\n\n## Placement and sampling\n\n**Bridson, \"Fast Poisson Disk Sampling in Arbitrary Dimensions\" (SIGGRAPH 2007)** \u2014 places points randomly but with a minimum distance between them. The output looks much more natural than uniform random (which clumps) or grid-aligned (which is mechanical). Used everywhere natural-looking-but-not-mechanical placement is needed: foliage, stars, decorative dot patterns, particle initial conditions. ~50 lines. https://www.cs.ubc.ca/~rbridson/docs/bridson-siggraph07-poissondisk.pdf\n\n## A newer one with HN momentum\n\n**Gumin, \"Wave Function Collapse\" (2016)** \u2014 not from a 1980s paper, but the same shape of \"simple algorithm produces magical output.\" Generates tile-based content that satisfies adjacency constraints. Has had several HN front-pages and become a thing in indie game dev. The output is striking \u2014 coherent landscapes, cities, patterns assembled from small tile sets. Could be its own portfolio piece. https://github.com/mxgmn/WaveFunctionCollapse\n\n---\n\n## How to think about picking one\n\nFor your portfolio specifically, the question is what *role* you want the next piece to play:\n\n- **A second showcase library** like Washes \u2192 reaction-diffusion or L-systems. Both have the \"slow magical reveal\" quality and document well.\n- **A page transition collection** \u2192 boids (assembling/scattering), L-systems (growing), Stam fluid (dispersing). Different transitions for different moods.\n- **An interactive playground** \u2192 Wave Function Collapse or L-system grammar editor. Lets visitors create something.\n- **Hero ambient** \u2192 Perlin flow fields, Turing reaction-diffusion, or DLA. Slow, never-twice-the-same.\n- **UI feedback sound** \u2192 Karplus-Strong on hover/click. Almost no one does this.\n\n## The meta-pattern\n\nLooking across these papers, the recipe for the next \"I built this from a 1985 paper\" HN post is:\n\n1. Old paper (1960s\u20132000s, ideally pre-2010 so it has the \"classic\" patina)\n2. Elegant math (small enough to actually understand and explain)\n3. Naturalistic output (people instinctively connect to it)\n4. Small enough to fit in a browser without WebGL gymnastics\n5. A debugging story along the way (Washes' \u221a2 cross was the gift here; you'll find equivalent stories in any of these)\n6. Honest documentation including prior art and the people you built on\n\nIf you do another one, I'd genuinely recommend **L-systems** as the next project. The aesthetic is gorgeous, the math is teachable (recursive grammars are intuitive once you see them), the variation space is huge, and *The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants* is so beautiful that having read it is itself a credibility signal. Plus: nobody has made a really great browser-native L-system playground. The space is there.\n\nThat said \u2014 finish Washes first, ship it, see what the response is. The next project will look different after you've done one round.",
    "citations": []
  }

      "description": "Future projects notebook \u2014 paper-based naturalistic browser projects, with meta-pattern lifted up and L-systems expanded",
      "file_text": "# Future projects: papers worth implementing\n\nA working notebook of paper-based, browser-friendly, naturalistic\nprojects in the same vein as Washes. Each one fits the same\nrecipe: old paper, elegant math, surprising output, small enough to\nrun in a browser tab.\n\nThis list emerged from a conversation about what to build next\nafter Washes. It's not meant to be exhaustive \u2014 just the projects\nI think would be worth doing, organized by what they're good for.\n\n---\n\n## The meta-pattern\n\nLooking across all of these, there's a recipe for the next\n\"I built this from an old paper\" piece of work. The pattern that\nmade Washes a viable project, and the pattern these all share:\n\n1. **Old paper.** Ideally 1960s\u20132000s, so it has the \"classic\" patina. Recent papers feel like keeping up; old papers feel like reaching back.\n2. **Elegant math.** Small enough to actually understand and explain in a paragraph. If you can't explain the core insight on one page, the project will be too heavy.\n3. **Naturalistic output.** People instinctively connect to things that look like nature \u2014 fluid, branches, flocks, crystallization, patterns. This is what differentiates \"neat hack\" from \"I want to look at this for an hour.\"\n4. **Small enough to fit in a browser** without WebGL gymnastics or build-system hell. Canvas2D or simple WebGL. Vanilla JS or one tiny dep. Page loads instantly.\n5. **A debugging story along the way.** Washes had the \u221a2 cross. Any nontrivial project will have its own version \u2014 the off-by-one, the floating-point boundary, the algorithm that \"shouldn't\" work but does. Document it. The debugging story is often more interesting than the final result.\n6. **Honest documentation** including prior art (who else built this, where they got it, what's different about yours) and the people you built on (cite the paper, name the authors). This is how you sit credibly in the lineage.\n\nIf you check those six boxes, you have something that holds\ntogether as a project, reads well to a thoughtful audience, and\nis satisfying to build.\n\n---\n\n## Project ideas, grouped by what they're good for\n\n### Fluids and physics (closest to Washes)\n\n**Stam, \"Stable Fluids\" (SIGGRAPH 1999)**\nYou already know this one from v0.67 of Washes (it's the\nsemi-Lagrangian advection scheme). As a *standalone* interactive,\nit's spectacular: a 2D fluid sandbox where the cursor injects\nvelocity and dye, smoke swirls in real time. Jos Stam's original\nC code is famously ~200 lines. Drag the cursor and people will\nkeep dragging \u2014 there's something hypnotic about it.\n- Paper: https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~chaol/data/cs777/stam-stable_fluids.pdf\n\n**Jakobsen, \"Advanced Character Physics\" (GDC 2001)**\nVerlet integration for ropes, chains, cloth, soft bodies. Drag\nany vertex and the rest deforms naturally. The math is just:\n\"remember last frame's position, do `new = 2*now - last + accel*dt\u00b2`,\nthen enforce distance constraints between points.\" 50-100 lines.\nEndless UX uses: hanging UI elements, draggable decorations, cloth\nbackgrounds, breakable chains.\n- Paper: https://www.cs.cmu.edu/afs/cs/academic/class/15462-s13/www/lec_slides/Jakobsen.pdf\n\n### Procedural patterns (gorgeous, slow-building, naturalistic)\n\n**Turing, \"The Chemical Basis of Morphogenesis\" (Phil. Trans. Royal Society 1952)**\nTwo coupled diffusion equations with feedback produce the patterns\nof coral, leopard spots, zebra stripes, fingerprints, seashells.\nSlow-building animation: looks like nothing for ~500 frames, then\norganic patterns emerge and you can't look away.\n- Karl Sims' tutorial: https://www.karlsims.com/rd.html (canonical browser-shaped intro)\n- Gray-Scott variant is the commonly-implemented form\n\n**Lindenmayer, \"Mathematical models for cellular interactions in development\" (J. Theor. Biology 1968)**\nL-systems. Recursive grammar that produces trees, ferns, flowers,\nriver networks. See expanded section below \u2014 this is the one I'd\nrecommend most.\n- Canonical reference: Prusinkiewicz & Lindenmayer, *The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants* (1990), free PDF at http://algorithmicbotany.org/papers/abop/abop.pdf\n- One of the most beautiful CS books ever published\n\n**Perlin, \"An Image Synthesizer\" (SIGGRAPH 1985)**\nGradient noise. Underlies basically all naturalistic procedural\ntexture in computer graphics. Combined with flow fields (particles\nfollowing the gradient), produces gorgeous organic motion. Three\nlines of math, four decades of influence.\n- Original paper: https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/325165.325247\n- The Book of Shaders chapter: https://thebookofshaders.com/11/\n\n**Witten & Sander, \"Diffusion-Limited Aggregation\" (Phys. Rev. Lett. 1981)**\nRandom walk + sticking = frost, lichen, electric arcs, river\ndeltas. The algorithm is literally \"particles do a random walk\nuntil they touch the cluster, then freeze.\" A few lines of code.\nCould be a \"growing-on-hover\" effect on portfolio cards.\n- Paper: https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.47.1400\n\n### Agents and behavior\n\n**Reynolds, \"Flocks, Herds, and Schools\" (SIGGRAPH 1987)**\nBoids. Three rules per agent \u2014 separation, alignment, cohesion \u2014\nproduce convincing flocks, schools, herds. Endlessly useful for\nportfolio sites: cursor-following swarms, decorative birds across\nthe top of a page, particle systems that react to mouse position.\nThe implementation is small; the design space is huge.\n- Paper (full pseudocode): https://www.red3d.com/cwr/papers/1987/\n- Reynolds' updates page: https://www.red3d.com/cwr/boids/\n\n### Sound (an underexplored direction)\n\n**Karplus & Strong, \"Digital Synthesis of Plucked-String...\" (Computer Music Journal 1983)**\nPhysically-modeled plucked strings using a delay line + averaging\nfilter. ~20 lines of JS, produces beautiful realistic\nguitar/harp/sitar sounds depending on parameters. With Web Audio\nAPI this is browser-native. Click any UI element, hear a\nsatisfying note. The space of \"websites that have thoughtful sound\ndesign\" is mostly empty \u2014 this would be a differentiator.\n- Paper: https://www.jstor.org/stable/3680062\n- Tone.js has Karplus-Strong as a built-in primitive if you want to skip the implementation\n\n### Placement and sampling\n\n**Bridson, \"Fast Poisson Disk Sampling in Arbitrary Dimensions\" (SIGGRAPH 2007)**\nPlaces points randomly but with a minimum distance between them.\nThe output looks much more natural than uniform random (which\nclumps) or grid-aligned (which is mechanical). Used everywhere\nnatural-looking-but-not-mechanical placement is needed: foliage,\nstars, decorative dots, particle initial conditions. ~50 lines.\n- Paper: https://www.cs.ubc.ca/~rbridson/docs/bridson-siggraph07-poissondisk.pdf\n\n### A newer one with HN momentum\n\n**Gumin, \"Wave Function Collapse\" (2016)**\nNot from a 1980s paper, but the same shape of \"simple algorithm\nproduces magical output.\" Generates tile-based content that\nsatisfies adjacency constraints. Has had several HN front-pages\nand become a thing in indie game dev. The output is striking \u2014\ncoherent landscapes, cities, patterns assembled from small tile\nsets. Could be its own portfolio piece.\n- Original repo: https://github.com/mxgmn/WaveFunctionCollapse\n\n---\n\n## L-systems, expanded\n\nIf you do one of these next, this is the one I'd recommend.\n\n### The intuition\n\nAn L-system is a *rewriting rule*. You start with a string (the\n\"axiom\") and a set of rules that replace each character with\nanother string. You apply the rules to every character in\nparallel, and repeat.\n\nThe classic textbook example is algae growth from Lindenmayer's\noriginal 1968 paper:\n\n```\nAxiom:  A\nRules:  A \u2192 AB\n        B \u2192 A\n\nGeneration 0:  A\nGeneration 1:  AB\nGeneration 2:  ABA\nGeneration 3:  ABAAB\nGeneration 4:  ABAABABA\nGeneration 5:  ABAABABAABAAB\n```\n\nThat string is then interpreted as drawing instructions. The\nturtle-graphics convention is:\n\n```\nF  \u2192  draw a line forward\n+  \u2192  turn left by some angle\n-  \u2192  turn right by some angle\n[  \u2192  push current position/heading onto a stack\n]  \u2192  pop position/heading from the stack\n```\n\nWith those primitives, a rule like:\n\n```\nAxiom:  F\nRule:   F \u2192 F[+F]F[-F]F\nAngle:  25.7\u00b0\n```\n\n\u2026produces a tree. Five generations in, a *convincing* tree. The\nbranches go off into branches that go off into branches. Adjust\nthe angle and you get different species. Add stochasticity to\nthe rules and every tree is unique.\n\n### Why it's beautiful\n\n1. **Self-similarity is intrinsic.** Trees are self-similar at multiple scales. So are river networks, lightning, snowflakes, lung airways, blood vessels. L-systems get this for free because the rules apply recursively.\n2. **The grammar is editable in real time.** Change one rule, regenerate, see a wildly different plant. This is intoxicating to play with.\n3. **It's compositional.** A flower can be `F[+L][-L]F[+L][-L]X` where `L` is \"leaf\" with its own rules and `X` is \"blossom\" with its own rules. Each subgrammar is independently designable.\n4. **The math is teachable.** You can explain L-systems on one page. Most procedural-generation techniques can't claim that.\n5. **The output is gorgeous.** *The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants* \u2014 the canonical reference \u2014 is one of the most visually stunning CS books ever made. Reading it is its own pleasure.\n\n### What a good browser-native L-system project might look like\n\nNobody has made a really great L-system playground for the web.\nThe opportunity:\n\n- A two-pane interface: grammar editor on the left, canvas on the right\n- Live regeneration as the user edits rules\n- Animated growth (one generation per second) rather than instant draw\n- Stochastic rule support (each `F \u2192 F[+F]F[-F]F | F[+F]F` chooses randomly among alternatives, weighted)\n- Parametric L-systems (rules with arguments: `F(x) \u2192 F(x*0.7) [+F(x*0.7)] F(x*0.7)`)\n- A gallery of pre-made grammars users can fork: tree, fern, dragon curve, Koch snowflake, Sierpinski, river network, etc.\n- Export to SVG for further use in design work\n- Maybe: 3D variant (turtle in 3D, with `&^/\\` for pitch/roll commands)\n\nThe technical surface is small (a few hundred lines for the core\nengine). The product/design surface is huge (how do you make\ngrammar editing feel natural to someone who's never seen a\ncontext-free rewriting rule?). That's the real opportunity here \u2014\nit's a design problem disguised as a math problem.\n\n### Variations and extensions to know about\n\n- **Stochastic L-systems** \u2014 rules with multiple right-hand sides chosen probabilistically\n- **Context-sensitive L-systems** \u2014 rules that depend on neighboring characters\n- **Parametric L-systems** \u2014 characters carry numeric parameters (length, angle, age)\n- **Environment-sensitive L-systems** \u2014 rules that respond to external fields (light direction, obstacle avoidance)\n- **3D L-systems** \u2014 turtle has full 3D orientation, characters for pitch/roll\n- **Differential L-systems** \u2014 continuous-time growth rather than discrete generations\n\n### Resources to start with\n\n- *The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants* \u2014 Prusinkiewicz & Lindenmayer (1990) \u2014 free PDF: http://algorithmicbotany.org/papers/abop/abop.pdf \u2014 read at least chapter 1\n- Paul Bourke's L-system pages: https://paulbourke.net/fractals/lsys/\n- Karl Sims' page on growth & form: https://www.karlsims.com/\n- Algorithmic Botany (the project Prusinkiewicz still runs): http://algorithmicbotany.org/\n\n### Anticipated debugging stories\n\nThings that will go wrong (and become good documentation):\n\n- **Stack overflow on deep recursion.** You'll generate strings of length 10\u2076+ at high generations. The string-rewriting approach hits memory limits fast. Move to lazy evaluation or generator-based rewriting.\n- **Turtle drift.** Floating-point accumulation in the turtle's position over millions of forward steps. Fix with periodic snap-to-grid or by tracking position in a more numerically stable way.\n- **Aesthetic vs. fidelity.** Real plants don't quite follow L-system rules. Where do you stop adding biological realism? This is a *design* call disguised as a technical one \u2014 the kind of decision a Design Engineer makes.\n- **Performance with stochasticity.** Re-rolling the random seed on every edit destroys flow. Persistent seeds, or \"lock this branch\" gestures, become a UX problem.\n\nThe debugging stories from L-systems would probably read closer\nto \"I spent two weeks figuring out how to make grammar editing\nfeel like drawing\" than \"I spent two weeks debugging math.\" That\nmight actually be a more interesting story to write than Washes'.\n\n---\n\n## How to pick one for your portfolio\n\nThe question is what *role* you want the next piece to play.\n\n| Goal | Best fit |\n|------|----------|\n| Second showcase library like Washes | Reaction-diffusion or L-systems. Both have the \"slow magical reveal\" quality and document well. |\n| Page transition collection | Boids (assembling/scattering), L-systems (growing), Stam fluid (dispersing). Different transitions for different moods. |\n| Interactive playground | L-systems grammar editor, or Wave Function Collapse. Lets visitors create something. |\n| Hero ambient background | Perlin flow fields, Turing reaction-diffusion, or DLA. Slow, never-twice-the-same. |\n| UI feedback sound | Karplus-Strong on hover/click. Almost no one does this. |\n| HN postmortem post | Whichever one has the best debugging story. You can't predict this in advance \u2014 pick the project you'll enjoy, build it, and the story will appear. |\n\n---\n\n## Doing this well\n\nFinish Washes first. Ship it. See what the response is. The\nnext project will look different after one round of public\nfeedback.\n\nWhen you do pick, the meta-pattern up top is the actual heuristic\nto apply. Six criteria, all of them either passable or\nnon-passable. If a project candidate fails any of them, it's the\nwrong project. If it passes all six, it's worth two weeks of\nfocused effort.\n\nAnd one more thing worth saying, because it bears on how to\nthink about all of this: the value of these projects isn't the\nalgorithm. The algorithms are public, the math is published, the\ntechniques are decades old. The value is *taste* \u2014 what you pick\nto build, how you scope it, how you make it feel, how you\ndocument it, and how you talk about it. That's the part that's\nyours. The L-system grammar is Lindenmayer's; the L-system\n*playground that someone wants to spend an hour in* is yours.\n",

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"markdown\", \"code\": \"# Future projects: papers worth implementing\\n\\nA working notebook of paper-based, browser-friendly, naturalistic\\nprojects in the same vein as Washes. Each one fits the same\\nrecipe: old paper, elegant math, surprising output, small enough to\\nrun in a browser tab.\\n\\nThis list emerged from a conversation about what to build next\\nafter Washes. It's not meant to be exhaustive \\u2014 just the projects\\nI think would be worth doing, organized by what they're good for.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## The meta-pattern\\n\\nLooking across all of these, there's a recipe for the next\\n\\\"I built this from an old paper\\\" piece of work. The pattern that\\nmade Washes a viable project, and the pattern these all share:\\n\\n1. **Old paper.** Ideally 1960s\\u20132000s, so it has the \\\"classic\\\" patina. Recent papers feel like keeping up; old papers feel like reaching back.\\n2. **Elegant math.** Small enough to actually understand and explain in a paragraph. If you can't explain the core insight on one page, the project will be too heavy.\\n3. **Naturalistic output.** People instinctively connect to things that look like nature \\u2014 fluid, branches, flocks, crystallization, patterns. This is what differentiates \\\"neat hack\\\" from \\\"I want to look at this for an hour.\\\"\\n4. **Small enough to fit in a browser** without WebGL gymnastics or build-system hell. Canvas2D or simple WebGL. Vanilla JS or one tiny dep. Page loads instantly.\\n5. **A debugging story along the way.** Washes had the \\u221a2 cross. Any nontrivial project will have its own version \\u2014 the off-by-one, the floating-point boundary, the algorithm that \\\"shouldn't\\\" work but does. Document it. The debugging story is often more interesting than the final result.\\n6. **Honest documentation** including prior art (who else built this, where they got it, what's different about yours) and the people you built on (cite the paper, name the authors). This is how you sit credibly in the lineage.\\n\\nIf you check those six boxes, you have something that holds\\ntogether as a project, reads well to a thoughtful audience, and\\nis satisfying to build.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Project ideas, grouped by what they're good for\\n\\n### Fluids and physics (closest to Washes)\\n\\n**Stam, \\\"Stable Fluids\\\" (SIGGRAPH 1999)**\\nYou already know this one from v0.67 of Washes (it's the\\nsemi-Lagrangian advection scheme). As a *standalone* interactive,\\nit's spectacular: a 2D fluid sandbox where the cursor injects\\nvelocity and dye, smoke swirls in real time. Jos Stam's original\\nC code is famously ~200 lines. Drag the cursor and people will\\nkeep dragging \\u2014 there's something hypnotic about it.\\n- Paper: https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~chaol/data/cs777/stam-stable_fluids.pdf\\n\\n**Jakobsen, \\\"Advanced Character Physics\\\" (GDC 2001)**\\nVerlet integration for ropes, chains, cloth, soft bodies. Drag\\nany vertex and the rest deforms naturally. The math is just:\\n\\\"remember last frame's position, do `new = 2*now - last + accel*dt\\u00b2`,\\nthen enforce distance constraints between points.\\\" 50-100 lines.\\nEndless UX uses: hanging UI elements, draggable decorations, cloth\\nbackgrounds, breakable chains.\\n- Paper: https://www.cs.cmu.edu/afs/cs/academic/class/15462-s13/www/lec_slides/Jakobsen.pdf\\n\\n### Procedural patterns (gorgeous, slow-building, naturalistic)\\n\\n**Turing, \\\"The Chemical Basis of Morphogenesis\\\" (Phil. Trans. Royal Society 1952)**\\nTwo coupled diffusion equations with feedback produce the patterns\\nof coral, leopard spots, zebra stripes, fingerprints, seashells.\\nSlow-building animation: looks like nothing for ~500 frames, then\\norganic patterns emerge and you can't look away.\\n- Karl Sims' tutorial: https://www.karlsims.com/rd.html (canonical browser-shaped intro)\\n- Gray-Scott variant is the commonly-implemented form\\n\\n**Lindenmayer, \\\"Mathematical models for cellular interactions in development\\\" (J. Theor. Biology 1968)**\\nL-systems. Recursive grammar that produces trees, ferns, flowers,\\nriver networks. See expanded section below \\u2014 this is the one I'd\\nrecommend most.\\n- Canonical reference: Prusinkiewicz & Lindenmayer, *The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants* (1990), free PDF at http://algorithmicbotany.org/papers/abop/abop.pdf\\n- One of the most beautiful CS books ever published\\n\\n**Perlin, \\\"An Image Synthesizer\\\" (SIGGRAPH 1985)**\\nGradient noise. Underlies basically all naturalistic procedural\\ntexture in computer graphics. Combined with flow fields (particles\\nfollowing the gradient), produces gorgeous organic motion. Three\\nlines of math, four decades of influence.\\n- Original paper: https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/325165.325247\\n- The Book of Shaders chapter: https://thebookofshaders.com/11/\\n\\n**Witten & Sander, \\\"Diffusion-Limited Aggregation\\\" (Phys. Rev. Lett. 1981)**\\nRandom walk + sticking = frost, lichen, electric arcs, river\\ndeltas. The algorithm is literally \\\"particles do a random walk\\nuntil they touch the cluster, then freeze.\\\" A few lines of code.\\nCould be a \\\"growing-on-hover\\\" effect on portfolio cards.\\n- Paper: https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.47.1400\\n\\n### Agents and behavior\\n\\n**Reynolds, \\\"Flocks, Herds, and Schools\\\" (SIGGRAPH 1987)**\\nBoids. Three rules per agent \\u2014 separation, alignment, cohesion \\u2014\\nproduce convincing flocks, schools, herds. Endlessly useful for\\nportfolio sites: cursor-following swarms, decorative birds across\\nthe top of a page, particle systems that react to mouse position.\\nThe implementation is small; the design space is huge.\\n- Paper (full pseudocode): https://www.red3d.com/cwr/papers/1987/\\n- Reynolds' updates page: https://www.red3d.com/cwr/boids/\\n\\n### Sound (an underexplored direction)\\n\\n**Karplus & Strong, \\\"Digital Synthesis of Plucked-String...\\\" (Computer Music Journal 1983)**\\nPhysically-modeled plucked strings using a delay line + averaging\\nfilter. ~20 lines of JS, produces beautiful realistic\\nguitar/harp/sitar sounds depending on parameters. With Web Audio\\nAPI this is browser-native. Click any UI element, hear a\\nsatisfying note. The space of \\\"websites that have thoughtful sound\\ndesign\\\" is mostly empty \\u2014 this would be a differentiator.\\n- Paper: https://www.jstor.org/stable/3680062\\n- Tone.js has Karplus-Strong as a built-in primitive if you want to skip the implementation\\n\\n### Placement and sampling\\n\\n**Bridson, \\\"Fast Poisson Disk Sampling in Arbitrary Dimensions\\\" (SIGGRAPH 2007)**\\nPlaces points randomly but with a minimum distance between them.\\nThe output looks much more natural than uniform random (which\\nclumps) or grid-aligned (which is mechanical). Used everywhere\\nnatural-looking-but-not-mechanical placement is needed: foliage,\\nstars, decorative dots, particle initial conditions. ~50 lines.\\n- Paper: https://www.cs.ubc.ca/~rbridson/docs/bridson-siggraph07-poissondisk.pdf\\n\\n### A newer one with HN momentum\\n\\n**Gumin, \\\"Wave Function Collapse\\\" (2016)**\\nNot from a 1980s paper, but the same shape of \\\"simple algorithm\\nproduces magical output.\\\" Generates tile-based content that\\nsatisfies adjacency constraints. Has had several HN front-pages\\nand become a thing in indie game dev. The output is striking \\u2014\\ncoherent landscapes, cities, patterns assembled from small tile\\nsets. Could be its own portfolio piece.\\n- Original repo: https://github.com/mxgmn/WaveFunctionCollapse\\n\\n---\\n\\n## L-systems, expanded\\n\\nIf you do one of these next, this is the one I'd recommend.\\n\\n### The intuition\\n\\nAn L-system is a *rewriting rule*. You start with a string (the\\n\\\"axiom\\\") and a set of rules that replace each character with\\nanother string. You apply the rules to every character in\\nparallel, and repeat.\\n\\nThe classic textbook example is algae growth from Lindenmayer's\\noriginal 1968 paper:\\n\\n```\\nAxiom:  A\\nRules:  A \\u2192 AB\\n        B \\u2192 A\\n\\nGeneration 0:  A\\nGeneration 1:  AB\\nGeneration 2:  ABA\\nGeneration 3:  ABAAB\\nGeneration 4:  ABAABABA\\nGeneration 5:  ABAABABAABAAB\\n```\\n\\nThat string is then interpreted as drawing instructions. The\\nturtle-graphics convention is:\\n\\n```\\nF  \\u2192  draw a line forward\\n+  \\u2192  turn left by some angle\\n-  \\u2192  turn right by some angle\\n[  \\u2192  push current position/heading onto a stack\\n]  \\u2192  pop position/heading from the stack\\n```\\n\\nWith those primitives, a rule like:\\n\\n```\\nAxiom:  F\\nRule:   F \\u2192 F[+F]F[-F]F\\nAngle:  25.7\\u00b0\\n```\\n\\n\\u2026produces a tree. Five generations in, a *convincing* tree. The\\nbranches go off into branches that go off into branches. Adjust\\nthe angle and you get different species. Add stochasticity to\\nthe rules and every tree is unique.\\n\\n### Why it's beautiful\\n\\n1. **Self-similarity is intrinsic.** Trees are self-similar at multiple scales. Most procedural-generation techniques can't claim that.\\n5. **The output is gorgeous.** *The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants* \\u2014 the canonical reference \\u2014 is one of the most visually stunning CS books ever made. The product/design surface is huge (how do you make\\ngrammar editing feel natural to someone who's never seen a\\ncontext-free rewriting rule?). That's the real opportunity here \\u2014\\nit's a design problem disguised as a math problem.\\n\\n### Variations and extensions to know about\\n\\n- **Stochastic L-systems** \\u2014 rules with multiple right-hand sides chosen probabilistically\\n- **Context-sensitive L-systems** \\u2014 rules that depend on neighboring characters\\n- **Parametric L-systems** \\u2014 characters carry numeric parameters (length, angle, age)\\n- **Environment-sensitive L-systems** \\u2014 rules that respond to external fields (light direction, obstacle avoidance)\\n- **3D L-systems** \\u2014 turtle has full 3D orientation, characters for pitch/roll\\n- **Differential L-systems** \\u2014 continuous-time growth rather than discrete generations\\n\\n### Resources to start with\\n\\n- *The Algorithmic Beauty of Plants* \\u2014 Prusinkiewicz & Lindenmayer (1990) \\u2014 free PDF: http://algorithmicbotany.org/papers/abop/abop.pdf \\u2014 read at least chapter 1\\n- Paul Bourke's L-system pages: https://paulbourke.net/fractals/lsys/\\n- Karl Sims' page on growth & form: https://www.karlsims.com/\\n- Algorithmic Botany (the project Prusinkiewicz still runs): http://algorithmicbotany.org/\\n\\n### Anticipated debugging stories\\n\\nThings that will go wrong (and become good documentation):\\n\\n- **Stack overflow on deep recursion.** You'll generate strings of length 10\\u2076+ at high generations.     "text": " Quick context-check: these aren't watercolor pigments from the Curtis et al. palette (those use Kubelka-Munk K/S coefficients, which are different) \u2014 you're asking about [OKHSL](https://bottosson.github.io/posts/colorpicker/), Bj\u00f6rn Ottosson's perceptually uniform color space. Right?\n\nOKHSL doesn't have canonical values for \"rose,\" \"yellow,\" or \"blue\" \u2014 those are color *categories*, and each spans a range. But here are sensible center points for vivid versions of each:\n\n| Color | H | S | L |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Rose | 0\u00b0 (or 360\u00b0) | 100% | 60% |\n| Yellow | 110\u00b0 | 100% | 90% |\n| Blue | 250\u00b0 | 100% | 50% |\n\nA few notes on what makes OKHSL different from regular HSL:\n\n- **Hue values are not the same as HSL.** OKHSL hues are based on perceptual color rotation, so they don't line up with the HSL hue wheel. Yellow lands around 110\u00b0 in OKHSL (vs. 60\u00b0 in HSL); blue lands around 250\u00b0 (vs. 240\u00b0 in HSL).\n- **Lightness is perceptual.** L=50% in OKHSL means *visually middle gray*, which is why vivid yellow needs L\u224890% (yellow is intrinsically light) while vivid blue sits at L\u224850% (blue is intrinsically darker). HSL gets this wrong \u2014 HSL yellow at L=50% looks weirdly muddy.\n- **Saturation is bounded by gamut.** S=100% means \"as saturated as the sRGB gamut allows at this hue+lightness.\" Lower S values give pastel versions.\n\nIf you want specific watercolor-feeling variants:\n\n- **Rose (more like Quinacridone Rose):** H=350\u00b0, S=85%, L=55% \u2014 slightly cooler, slightly desaturated\n- **Yellow (more like Hansa Yellow):** H=100\u00b0, S=95%, L=85% \u2014 slightly warmer, slightly less bright\n- **Blue (more like Cerulean):** H=235\u00b0, S=80%, L=60% \u2014 slightly greener, lighter\n\nIf you wanted me to extract these from the actual pigments in the Washes lib (which are defined in RGB K/S coefficient pairs, not OKHSL), I'd need to convert RGB \u2192 OKLab \u2192 OKHSL. Want me to do that for the full pigment palette?",

[
  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-13T03:39:31.305673Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-13T03:39:56.091250Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " OKLab is a perceptually uniform color space designed by Bj\u00f6rn Ottosson, published in [his 2020 blog post](https://bottosson.github.io/posts/oklab/). The benefits are real and worth understanding \u2014 most CSS and design code today uses sRGB or HSL, and both have specific failures that OKLab fixes.\n\n## The core benefit: perceptual uniformity\n\nIn OKLab, **equal numerical distance = equal perceived color difference**. This sounds abstract until you see what it fixes.\n\nIf you interpolate between two colors in sRGB (or in HSL, or in HSV), you get a path through color space that *passes through dead-looking grays in the middle*. The canonical example: interpolate from pure blue `#0000FF` to pure yellow `#FFFF00`. In sRGB the midpoint is a muddy grey-brown. In OKLab the midpoint is a clean neutral green-grey that feels like a real color a designer might pick. This matters every time you do a gradient, a color animation, or a hover transition.\n\nThe reason is that sRGB encodes colors for *display gamma*, not for perception. The math treats `(127, 127, 127)` as halfway between black and white, but the human eye sees it as much darker than that. OKLab does the perceptual transformation properly, so the math matches the eye.\n\n## Specific benefits in practice\n\n**Gradients look right.** Any color-to-color gradient in OKLab keeps consistent saturation and brightness across the path. Try `linear-gradient(to right, blue, yellow)` in regular CSS vs. `linear-gradient(in oklab, blue, yellow)` \u2014 the OKLab version stays vibrant through the middle; the default goes muddy.\n\n**Lightness is meaningful.** In HSL, \"lightness 50%\" of yellow and \"lightness 50%\" of blue look nothing alike \u2014 yellow at 50% looks dark, blue at 50% looks fine. In OKLab (and OKLCH/OKHSL, which are derived from it), L=50% means *the same visual lightness* across all hues. You can build a design system where all your accent colors at L=60% look balanced together.\n\n**Color palette generation is sane.** Want a palette of 8 evenly-spaced colors around a hue wheel? In HSL the result looks lopsided because some hues feel inherently brighter than others. In OKLCH (the cylindrical form of OKLab), evenly-spaced hues *look* evenly spaced.\n\n**Accessibility math works.** WCAG contrast ratios are sRGB-based and famously imperfect \u2014 black text on a pure-blue background passes WCAG but is hard to read, while the same text on a yellow background fails despite being easier. APCA and OKLab-based contrast metrics get this right because they're built on perceptual lightness.\n\n**Color blending matches paint intuition.** Mixing blue + yellow in sRGB gives grey. Mixing in OKLab gives green \u2014 closer to what people expect from paint mixing.\n\n## OKLab vs. related spaces\n\n| Space | What it's for |\n|---|---|\n| OKLab | The base perceptual space: `L` (lightness), `a` (green\u2194red), `b` (blue\u2194yellow). Cartesian. Best for color math, interpolation, distance metrics. |\n| OKLCH | OKLab in polar coordinates: `L`, `C` (chroma), `H` (hue). Better for picking colors by hand \u2014 \"I want this hue, that saturation.\" |\n| OKHSL | OKLab adjusted so saturation is bounded by the sRGB gamut. Behaves more like classic HSL but with the perceptual fixes. Best for color pickers and design tools. |\n\nFor everyday CSS work, **OKLCH is usually what you want.** Hue is a number you can rotate, chroma is \"how vivid,\" lightness is \"how light\" \u2014 and all three are perceptually meaningful.\n\n## Browser support\n\nCSS Color Module Level 4 supports `oklab()`, `oklch()`, and gradient interpolation in OKLab as of late 2023 / early 2024. Safari, Chrome, Firefox all support it. Tailwind v4 uses OKLCH for its color palette internally.\n\nYou can write:\n\n```css\n.button {\n  background: oklch(60% 0.2 250);          /* a vivid blue */\n}\n\n.gradient {\n  background: linear-gradient(in oklch, oklch(60% 0.2 30), oklch(80% 0.15 110));\n}\n```\n\n## Where it doesn't help\n\nA few caveats:\n\n- **For brand colors handed to you in hex, the conversion is one-way useful.** You can convert `#1A73E8` to OKLCH and use it for variations, but the brand value is still the hex.\n- **OKLab is not magic for color mixing in the painterly sense.** Mixing yellow and blue gives green-grey, not vivid green. Kubelka-Munk (what Washes uses for pigment compositing) does the *physical* paint-mixing model and gives different (and arguably more \"watercolor-correct\") results. OKLab gives *perceptually smooth interpolation*, which is a different thing.\n- **For data visualization, perceptually uniform colormaps like viridis, magma, or cividis** are still the canonical recommendation. Those were designed specifically for sequential data and have OKLab-like properties baked in.\n\n## The short version\n\nOKLab fixes the three places color goes wrong in CSS/design code: gradients lose vividness in the middle, lightness across hues doesn't match perception, and palettes generated from hue rotation look uneven. It's a drop-in replacement for HSL/sRGB in most design contexts, and it's now natively supported in modern browsers.\n\nFor your portfolio site specifically: if any of your pages do color transitions, palette generation, or hover states with color shifts, switching the interpolation space to OKLab/OKLCH is one of the cheapest visual upgrades you can make. Single CSS keyword change (`in oklch` inside a gradient), noticeably better result.\n\nWant me to convert the Washes pigment palette to OKLCH values so you can compare what the pigments look like in perceptual terms?",
    "citations": []
  }

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Before I touch any code, let me think about what this actually means \u2014 there are a few possible interpretations and they're very different in scope.\n\n## What \"OKLCH mode\" could mean\n\n**Option A: User-facing color input in OKLCH.** The API currently takes colors however it takes them (RGB hex, etc.). Add a way to specify pigment color in OKLCH so users can pick perceptually. Lowest-risk change.\n\n**Option B: Internal pigment interpolation in OKLCH.** When pigments mix during the simulation, do the mixing in OKLCH space instead of however it currently mixes. Risk: this would *change the simulation's visual output*, possibly meaningfully.\n\n**Option C: Replace Kubelka-Munk compositing with OKLCH-based compositing.** Bigger architectural change. The lib currently uses K-M for layered glaze rendering (Curtis et al.'s whole point). Replacing it would be a different lib at that point.\n\n**Option D: Add an OKLCH-aware color picker / palette to the UI.** Pure UI addition, no simulation changes. Lets users pick pigment colors using a perceptual picker.\n\nWhich one were you thinking? My guess from context is **A or D** \u2014 but I want to check before doing anything, because B and C are much bigger and I'd argue against them.\n\n## Why I'd push back on B and C\n\nThe Curtis et al. paper does color compositing via Kubelka-Munk for a specific reason: K-M models the *physical* layering of pigment glazes \u2014 how light scatters and absorbs through translucent paint layers. That's what gives watercolor its luminous, glaze-y quality. Replacing K-M with OKLCH interpolation would lose that. The output would be more \"perceptually smooth color blends\" and less \"looks like real watercolor.\"\n\nOKLCH and K-M are answering different questions:\n- **K-M**: \"What color do I see when I look at thin translucent paint over paper?\"\n- **OKLCH**: \"What's a perceptually smooth path between two color values?\"\n\nThese are not interchangeable. If we mix them in the wrong place, the lib stops being a watercolor sim and becomes a generic color-gradient toy.\n\n## What I'd actually recommend\n\n**Option A done well**: add a way to specify pigments via OKLCH input that converts to the K-M absorption/scattering coefficients the lib needs internally. The conversion is well-defined \u2014 Curtis et al. describe it (the user picks how the pigment appears over white and over black, and that derives K and S). We could extend it: user picks one OKLCH color and a \"transparency\" parameter, we derive plausible K and S.\n\nThat gets you perceptual color input without disturbing the simulation physics. The lib stays a watercolor lib; the API becomes more designer-friendly.\n\n**Or option D**: a color picker in the UI that uses OKLCH internally for the sliders, but still feeds RGB to the lib's pigment-define API. Even smaller change \u2014 no lib changes at all, just wiring.\n\n## My questions before any code\n\n1. **Which option do you want?** A (perceptual pigment input), D (perceptual color picker UI), or something else I haven't considered?\n2. **For your portfolio use specifically** \u2014 do you care more about *picking colors* perceptually, or about *animating between colors* perceptually (e.g., a hover state that shifts a pigment's hue)?\n3. **Is this aimed at the public library API or just your own use?** Affects how much polish vs. how much \"make it work for me\" the right answer is.\n\nIf you tell me which of these matches what you had in mind, I can scope properly. My instinct is the smallest change that gets you the perceptual-color benefit without touching the simulation \u2014 but I want to make sure I'm solving the right problem.",
    "citations": []
  }

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Here you go \u2014 computed using the actual K-M equations from the lib, with the exact K and S coefficients used for each pigment (these come from Figure 5 of Curtis et al. 1997).\n\n## Canonical values (typical wash, x=1.0, over white paper)\n\n| Pigment | Hex | RGB |\n|---|---|---|\n| Quinacridone Rose | `#a50e53` | `rgb(165, 14, 83)` |\n| Hansa Yellow | `#e3af08` | `rgb(227, 175, 8)` |\n| Cerulean Blue | `#108ba0` | `rgb(16, 139, 160)` |\n\n## The thickness-dependent versions\n\nThis is the part the K-M model gets right that simple RGB blending doesn't \u2014 real watercolors change *hue* (not just intensity) as the layer gets thicker. So depending on how loaded your brush is:\n\n**Quinacridone Rose**\n- Delicate wash: `#cd3b90` \u2014 bright pink\n- Typical wash: `#a50e53` \u2014 deep magenta\n- Heavy load: `#4a000d` \u2014 almost black-red\n\n**Hansa Yellow**\n- Delicate wash: `#f0d02b` \u2014 bright lemon\n- Typical wash: `#e3af08` \u2014 saturated gold\n- Heavy load: `#bc8501` \u2014 dark amber-brown\n\n**Cerulean Blue**\n- Delicate wash: `#39bac7` \u2014 bright sky teal\n- Typical wash: `#108ba0` \u2014 deep cerulean\n- Heavy load: `#054762` \u2014 dark teal-navy\n\nThat hue shift across thicknesses is exactly what makes K-M \"look like watercolor\" rather than \"look like a sprite-and-alpha rendering.\" Quinacridone Rose at a delicate thickness reads pink, but at heavy load it crosses into almost-black with a red undertone \u2014 which is what real Quinacridone Rose actually does on paper.\n\nIf you need a single value per pigment for a brand palette or design system, use the **typical wash** column. If you're picking colors to match what people see in the sim during a deluge, the delicate-wash values are closer (a deluge spreads thin).",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "text": " Got it \u2014 large background blocks behind your portfolio content. A few thoughts on the right approach, because just making the brush huge has trade-offs.\n\n## The trade-offs of giant brushes\n\nA 2000px brush works for a single stamp, but for *blocking in backgrounds* there are some quirks:\n\n- **One stamp = one pigment color.** You can't get the layered, varied feel of real watercolor wash with a single huge circle.\n- **The edge is circular.** A giant brush leaves a giant circle, which reads as \"I dragged a huge brush\" rather than \"this is a wash.\"\n- **Pigment dynamics are flattened.** Watercolor's appeal is the variation that happens *during* drying \u2014 granulation, edge darkening, lift, pigment migration. A single huge stamp doesn't get to play through that dynamic; it just lands as a flat disc.\n\n## Better options for backgrounds (using what the lib already does)\n\n**Option 1: Deluge with a wide radius.** This is probably what you want. The deluge mechanism is designed to fill large areas with naturalistic watercolor variation \u2014 it creates outward-radiating wavefronts with mass conservation, lift, edge accumulation, granulation. The deluge \"radius\" parameter controls how wide it goes. A 2000-radius deluge would cover a much larger area than a 2000-px brush *and* it would look like a wash, not a circle.\n\n**Option 2: paintImage() with a solid-color source.** The lib has a `paintImage()` API that traces an image and lays down pigment according to it. Feed it a solid rectangle and it'll fill that rectangle with naturalistic watercolor texture. This is the right tool for \"block in this region with this color, but let it look like watercolor.\"\n\n**Option 3: traceSVG() with a large path.** If your background blocks are rectangles or geometric shapes, an SVG path drawn with the pen-stroke trace animation would look much more intentional than a giant circle stamp.\n\n## What I'd actually recommend for the BG use case\n\nIf you're trying to lay down large color regions as a portfolio background, the right approach is probably:\n\n1. **Define your regions as SVG rects or paths** (whatever shapes you want behind your content)\n2. **Use `paintImage()` or `traceSVG()`** to fill them with pigment that flows and looks like watercolor\n3. **Optionally add a deluge or two** to soften edges and add ambient wetness\n\nYou'd get backgrounds that look like *deliberately composed watercolor washes* rather than \"the brush is really big now.\"\n\n## My questions before any more code\n\nBefore I do anything else, I want to make sure I'm building toward your actual goal:\n\n1. **What's the shape of the BG elements?** Rectangles? Organic shapes? Full-bleed gradients? Big abstract regions?\n2. **Are these stamped once and stay there, or do they animate in/out as users navigate?**\n3. **Is this happening in the playground UI you've been testing in, or are you embedding the lib directly into your portfolio site code?**\n\nThe honest read: I think you've been making the brush bigger because it's the slider that's available, but the lib has more appropriate tools for what you actually want. Tell me what the BG looks like in your head and I can point you at the right ones.",

      "command": "node << 'EOF'\n// K-M reflectance for layered pigments. Mirrors kmReflect in the lib.\nfunction kmReflect(K, S, x, Rbg) {\n  if (S < 1e-5) S = 1e-5;\n  const a = 1 + K / S;\n  const b = Math.sqrt(Math.max(0, a * a - 1));\n  let bSx = b * S * x;\n  if (bSx > 12) bSx = 12;\n  const sh = Math.sinh(bSx);\n  const ch = Math.cosh(bSx);\n  const denom = a * sh + b * ch;\n  if (denom < 1e-9) return 0;\n  const Rlayer = sh / denom;\n  const Tlayer = b  / denom;\n  let R = Rlayer + (Tlayer * Tlayer * Rbg) / (1 - Rlayer * Rbg);\n  if (R < 0) R = 0; else if (R > 1) R = 1;\n  return R;\n}\n\n// Pigments from the lib (Curtis et al. 1997 Figure 5)\nconst ROSE   = { K:[0.22, 1.47, 0.57], S:[0.05, 0.003, 0.03] };\nconst YELLOW = { K:[0.06, 0.21, 1.78], S:[0.50, 0.88, 0.009] };\nconst BLUE   = { K:[1.52, 0.32, 0.25], S:[0.06, 0.26, 0.40] };\n\n// For K-M layered compositing, the standard approach is to composite each\n// layer over the previous result. Each pigment is a separate physical layer.\n// (The lib does this via per-cell pigment accumulation but the math is the\n// same when multiple pigments are present at one cell.)\nfunction layered(layers, x_each, paper) {\n  let R = [paper, paper, paper];\n  for (const p of layers) {\n    R = [\n      kmReflect(p.K[0], p.S[0], x_each, R[0]),\n      kmReflect(p.K[1], p.S[1], x_each, R[1]),\n      kmReflect(p.K[2], p.S[2], x_each, R[2]),\n    ];\n  }\n  return R;\n}\n\nfunction toHex(R) {\n  return '#' + R.map(v => Math.round(v*255).toString(16).padStart(2,'0')).join('');\n}\n\n// \"Light paint load\" \u2014 what does that mean in K-M thickness?\n// Default brush stamps are usually x = 1.0 - 3.0\n// A \"light wash\" should be much lower. Let's try a few.\nconst loads = [\n  { x: 0.15, label: 'very light (x=0.15)' },\n  { x: 0.30, label: 'light       (x=0.30)' },\n  { x: 0.50, label: 'medium      (x=0.50)' },\n];\n\nconst paper = 0.95; // ~ivory paper, the lib's default\n\nconsole.log('\\nFor each load, the 7 Venn regions:');\nconsole.log('(R=rose, Y=yellow, B=blue, RY=rose+yellow, RB=rose+blue, YB=yellow+blue, RYB=all three)\\n');\n\nfor (const load of loads) {\n  console.log(`=== ${load.label} ===`);\n  const regions = {\n    'R    (rose only)':       layered([ROSE],          load.x, paper),\n    'Y    (yellow only)':     layered([YELLOW],        load.x, paper),\n    'B    (blue only)':       layered([BLUE],          load.x, paper),\n    'RY   (rose+yellow)':     layered([ROSE,YELLOW],   load.x, paper),\n    'RB   (rose+blue)':       layered([ROSE,BLUE],     load.x, paper),\n    'YB   (yellow+blue)':     layered([YELLOW,BLUE],   load.x, paper),\n    'RYB  (all three)':       layered([ROSE,YELLOW,BLUE], load.x, paper),\n  };\n  for (const [name, R] of Object.entries(regions)) {\n    const hex = toHex(R);\n    const rgb = R.map(v => Math.round(v*255)).join(',');\n    console.log(`  ${name.padEnd(22)} ${hex}    rgb(${rgb})`);\n  }\n  console.log('');\n}\nEOF",
      "description": "Compute K-M Venn region colors for several load levels"
    },

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"bash\", \"code\": \"node << 'EOF'\\n// K-M reflectance for layered pigments. Mirrors kmReflect in the lib.\\nfunction kmReflect(K, S, x, Rbg) {\\n  if (S < 1e-5) S = 1e-5;\\n  const a = 1 + K / S;\\n  const b = Math.sqrt(Math.max(0, a * a - 1));\\n  let bSx = b * S * x;\\n  if (bSx > 12) bSx = 12;\\n  const sh = Math.sinh(bSx);\\n  const ch = Math.cosh(bSx);\\n  const denom = a * sh + b * ch;\\n  if (denom < 1e-9) return 0;\\n  const Rlayer = sh / denom;\\n  const Tlayer = b  / denom;\\n  let R = Rlayer + (Tlayer * Tlayer * Rbg) / (1 - Rlayer * Rbg);\\n  if (R < 0) R = 0; else if (R > 1) R = 1;\\n  return R;\\n}\\n\\n// Pigments from the lib (Curtis et al. 1997 Figure 5)\\nconst ROSE   = { K:[0.22, 1.47, 0.57], S:[0.05, 0.003, 0.03] };\\nconst YELLOW = { K:[0.06, 0.21, 1.78], S:[0.50, 0.88, 0.009] };\\nconst BLUE   = { K:[1.52, 0.32, 0.25], S:[0.06, 0.26, 0.40] };\\n\\n// For K-M layered compositing, the standard approach is to composite each\\n// layer over the previous result. Each pigment is a separate physical layer.\\n// (The lib does this via per-cell pigment accumulation but the math is the\\n// same when multiple pigments are present at one cell.)\\nfunction layered(layers, x_each, paper) {\\n  let R = [paper, paper, paper];\\n  for (const p of layers) {\\n    R = [\\n      kmReflect(p.K[0], p.S[0], x_each, R[0]),\\n      kmReflect(p.K[1], p.S[1], x_each, R[1]),\\n      kmReflect(p.K[2], p.S[2], x_each, R[2]),\\n    ];\\n  }\\n  return R;\\n}\\n\\nfunction toHex(R) {\\n  return '#' + R.map(v => Math.round(v*255).toString(16).padStart(2,'0')).join('');\\n}\\n\\n// \\\"Light paint load\\\" \\u2014 what does that mean in K-M thickness?\\n// Default brush stamps are usually x = 1.0 - 3.0\\n// A \\\"light wash\\\" should be much lower. Let's try a few.\\nconst loads = [\\n  { x: 0.15, label: 'very light (x=0.15)' },\\n  { x: 0.30, label: 'light       (x=0.30)' },\\n  { x: 0.50, label: 'medium      (x=0.50)' },\\n];\\n\\nconst paper = 0.95; // ~ivory paper, the lib's default\\n\\nconsole.log('\\\\nFor each load, the 7 Venn regions:');\\nconsole.log('(R=rose, Y=yellow, B=blue, RY=rose+yellow, RB=rose+blue, YB=yellow+blue, RYB=all three)\\\\n');\\n\\nfor (const load of loads) {\\n  console.log(`=== ${load.label} ===`);\\n  const regions = {\\n    'R    (rose only)':       layered([ROSE],          load.x, paper),\\n    'Y    (yellow only)':     layered([YELLOW],        load.x, paper),\\n    'B    (blue only)':       layered([BLUE],          load.x, paper),\\n    'RY   (rose+yellow)':     layered([ROSE,YELLOW],   load.x, paper),\\n    'RB   (rose+blue)':       layered([ROSE,BLUE],     load.x, paper),\\n    'YB   (yellow+blue)':     layered([YELLOW,BLUE],   load.x, paper),\\n    'RYB  (all three)':       layered([ROSE,YELLOW,BLUE], load.x, paper),\\n  };\\n  for (const [name, R] of Object.entries(regions)) {\\n    const hex = toHex(R);\\n    const rgb = R.map(v => Math.round(v*255)).join(',');\\n    console.log(`  ${name.padEnd(22)} ${hex}    rgb(${rgb})`);\\n  }\\n  console.log('');\\n}\\nEOF\"}"
