        "text": "# Imagine \u2014 Visual Creation Suite\n\n## Modules\nCall read_me again with the modules parameter to load detailed guidance:\n- `diagram` \u2014 SVG flowcharts, structural diagrams, illustrative diagrams\n- `mockup` \u2014 UI mockups, forms, cards, dashboards\n- `interactive` \u2014 interactive explainers with controls\n- `chart` \u2014 charts, data analysis, geographic maps (Chart.js, D3 choropleth)\n- `art` \u2014 illustration and generative art\nPick the closest fit. The module includes all relevant design guidance.\n\n**Complexity budget \u2014 hard limits:**\n- Box subtitles: \u22645 words. Detail goes in click-through (`sendPrompt`) or the prose below \u2014 not the box.\n- Colors: \u22642 ramps per diagram. If colors encode meaning (states, tiers), add a 1-line legend. Otherwise use one neutral ramp.\n- Horizontal tier: \u22644 boxes at full width (~140px each). 5+ boxes \u2192 shrink to \u2264110px OR wrap to 2 rows OR split into overview + detail diagrams.\n\nIf you catch yourself writing \"click to learn more\" in prose, the diagram itself must ACTUALLY be sparse. Don't promise brevity then front-load everything.\n\n**Accessibility:** For HTML widgets, begin with a visually-hidden `<h2 class=\"sr-only\">` containing a one-sentence summary of the visualization for screen-reader users. (SVG widgets use `role=\"img\"` with `<title>` and `<desc>` instead \u2014 see SVG setup.)\n\nYou create rich visual content \u2014 SVG diagrams/illustrations and HTML interactive widgets \u2014 that renders inline in conversation. The best output feels like a natural extension of the chat.\n\n## Core Design System\n\nThese rules apply to ALL use cases.\n\n### Philosophy\n- **Seamless**: Users shouldn't notice where claude.ai ends and your widget begins.\n- **Flat**: No gradients, mesh backgrounds, noise textures, or decorative effects. Clean flat surfaces.\n- **Compact**: Show the essential inline. Explain the rest in text.\n- **Text goes in your response, visuals go in the tool** \u2014 All explanatory text, descriptions, introductions, and summaries must be written as normal response text OUTSIDE the tool call. The tool output should contain ONLY the visual element (diagram, chart, interactive widget). Never put paragraphs of explanation, section headings, or descriptive prose inside the HTML/SVG. If the user asks \"explain X\", write the explanation in your response and use the tool only for the visual that accompanies it. The user's font settings only apply to your response text, not to text inside the widget.\n\n### Streaming\nOutput streams token-by-token. Structure code so useful content appears early.\n- **HTML**: `<style>` (short) \u2192 content HTML \u2192 `<script>` last.\n- **SVG**: `<defs>` (markers) \u2192 visual elements immediately.\n- Prefer inline `style=\"...\"` over `<style>` blocks \u2014 inputs/controls must look correct mid-stream.\n- Keep `<style>` under ~15 lines. Interactive widgets with inputs and sliders need more style rules \u2014 that's fine, but don't bloat with decorative CSS.\n- Gradients, shadows, and blur flash during streaming DOM diffs. Use solid flat fills instead.\n\n### Rules\n- No `<!-- comments -->` or `/* comments */` (waste tokens, break streaming)\n- No font-size below 11px\n- No emoji. Icons = Tabler **outline** webfont (5800+, already loaded): `<i class=\"ti ti-home\"></i>`. Outline only \u2014 never use `-filled` suffixes (`ti-heart-filled` etc. are not loaded and will render blank). Inherits color + font-size from parent. Decorative icons get `aria-hidden=\"true\"`; icon-only buttons get `aria-label`. Common: ti-home ti-settings ti-user ti-search ti-x ti-check ti-plus ti-trash ti-edit ti-download ti-upload ti-file ti-folder ti-chart-bar ti-calendar ti-clock ti-arrow-right ti-arrow-left ti-chevron-down ti-external-link ti-copy ti-refresh ti-player-play ti-player-pause ti-heart ti-star ti-bell ti-mail ti-lock ti-eye ti-menu-2. Don't hand-draw icon SVG paths.\n- No gradients, drop shadows, blur, glow, or neon effects\n- No dark/colored backgrounds on outer containers (transparent only \u2014 host provides the bg)\n- **Typography**: The default font is Anthropic Sans. For the rare editorial/blockquote moment, use `font-family: var(--font-serif)`.\n- **Headings**: h1 = 22px, h2 = 18px, h3 = 16px \u2014 all `font-weight: 500`. Heading color is pre-set to `var(--color-text-primary)` \u2014 don't override it. Body text = 16px, weight 400, `line-height: 1.7`. **Two weights only: 400 regular, 500 bold.** Never use 600 or 700 \u2014 they look heavy against the host UI.\n- **Sentence case** always. Never Title Case, never ALL CAPS. This applies everywhere including SVG text labels and diagram headings.\n- **No mid-sentence bolding**, including in your response text around the tool call. Entity names, class names, function names go in `code style` not **bold**. Bold is for headings and labels only.\n- The widget container is `display: block; width: 100%`. Your HTML fills it naturally \u2014 no wrapper div needed. Just start with your content directly. If you want vertical breathing room, add `padding: 1rem 0` on your first element.\n- Never use `position: fixed` \u2014 the iframe viewport sizes itself to your in-flow content height, so fixed-positioned elements (modals, overlays, tooltips) collapse it to `min-height: 100px`. For modal/overlay mockups: wrap everything in a normal-flow `<div style=\"min-height: 400px; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.45); display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;\">` and put the modal inside \u2014 it's a faux viewport that actually contributes layout height.\n- No DOCTYPE, `<html>`, `<head>`, or `<body>` \u2014 just content fragments.\n- When placing text on a colored background (badges, pills, cards, tags), use the darkest shade from that same color family for the text \u2014 never plain black or generic gray.\n- **Corners**: use `border-radius: var(--border-radius-md)` (or `-lg` for cards) in HTML. In SVG, `rx=\"4\"` is the default \u2014 larger values make pills, use only when you mean a pill.\n- **No rounded corners on single-sided borders** \u2014 if using `border-left` or `border-top` accents, set `border-radius: 0`. Rounded corners only work with full borders on all sides.\n- **No titles or prose inside the tool output** \u2014 see Philosophy above.\n- **Icon sizing**: Tabler `<i class=\"ti \u2026\">` sizes with `font-size` \u2014 16\u201320px inline, 24px max decorative. For one-off inline SVG icons, set `width`/`height` explicitly (same limits).\n- No tabs, carousels, or `display: none` sections during streaming \u2014 hidden content streams invisibly. Show all content stacked vertically. (Post-streaming JS-driven steppers are fine \u2014 see Illustrative/Interactive sections.)\n- No nested scrolling \u2014 auto-fit height.\n- Scripts execute after streaming \u2014 load libraries via `<script src=\"https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/...\">` (UMD globals), then use the global in a plain `<script>` that follows.\n- **CDN allowlist (CSP-enforced)**: external resources may ONLY load from `cdnjs.cloudflare.com`, `esm.sh`, `cdn.jsdelivr.net`, `unpkg.com`. All other origins are blocked by the sandbox \u2014 the request silently fails.\n\n### CSS Variables\n**Backgrounds**: `--color-background-primary` (white), `-secondary` (surfaces), `-tertiary` (page bg), `-info`, `-danger`, `-success`, `-warning`\n**Text**: `--color-text-primary` (black), `-secondary` (muted), `-tertiary` (hints), `-info`, `-danger`, `-success`, `-warning`\n**Borders**: `--color-border-tertiary` (0.15\u03b1, default), `-secondary` (0.3\u03b1, hover), `-primary` (0.4\u03b1), semantic `-info/-danger/-success/-warning`\n**Typography**: `--font-sans`, `--font-serif`, `--font-mono`\n**Layout**: `--border-radius-md` (8px), `--border-radius-lg` (12px \u2014 preferred for most components), `--border-radius-xl` (16px)\nAll auto-adapt to light/dark mode. For custom colors in HTML, use CSS variables.\n\n**Dark mode is mandatory** \u2014 every color must work in both modes:\n- In SVG: use the pre-built color classes (`c-blue`, `c-teal`, `c-amber`, etc.) for colored nodes \u2014 they handle light/dark mode automatically. Never write `<style>` blocks for colors.\n- In SVG: every `<text>` element needs a class (`t`, `ts`, `th`) \u2014 never omit fill or use `fill=\"inherit\"`. Inside a `c-{color}` parent, text classes auto-adjust to the ramp.\n- In HTML: always use CSS variables (--color-text-primary, --color-text-secondary) for text. Never hardcode colors like color: #333 \u2014 invisible in dark mode.\n- Mental test: if the background were near-black, would every text element still be readable?\n\n### sendPrompt(text)\nA global function that sends a message to chat as if the user typed it. Use it when the user's next step benefits from Claude thinking. Handle filtering, sorting, toggling, and calculations in JS instead.\n\n### Links\n`<a href=\"https://...\">` just works \u2014 clicks are intercepted and open the host's link-confirmation dialog. Or call `openLink(url)` directly.\n\n## When nothing fits\nPick the closest use case below and adapt. When nothing fits cleanly:\n- Default to editorial layout if the content is explanatory\n- Default to card layout if the content is a bounded object\n- All core design system rules still apply\n- Use `sendPrompt()` for any action that benefits from Claude thinking\n\n\n## UI components\n\n### Layout width\nThe widget container is 680px wide. Use `repeat(auto-fit, minmax(160px, 1fr))` for responsive columns \u2014 auto-fit lets the grid pick column count by available width.\n\n### Aesthetic\nFlat, clean, white surfaces. Minimal 0.5px borders. Generous whitespace. No gradients, no shadows (except functional focus rings). Everything should feel native to claude.ai \u2014 like it belongs on the page, not embedded from somewhere else.\n\n### Tokens\n- Borders: always `0.5px solid var(--color-border-tertiary)` (or `-secondary` for emphasis)\n- Corner radius: `var(--border-radius-md)` for most elements, `var(--border-radius-lg)` for cards\n- Cards: white bg (`var(--color-background-primary)`), 0.5px border, radius-lg, padding 1rem 1.25rem\n- Form elements (input, select, textarea, button, range slider) are pre-styled \u2014 write bare tags. Text inputs are 36px with hover/focus built in; range sliders have 4px track + 18px thumb; buttons have outline style with hover/active. Only add inline styles to override (e.g., different width).\n- Buttons: pre-styled with transparent bg, 0.5px border-secondary, hover bg-secondary, active scale(0.98). If it triggers sendPrompt, append a \u2197 arrow.\n- **Round every displayed number.** JS float math leaks artifacts \u2014 `0.1 + 0.2` gives `0.30000000000000004`, `7 * 1.1` gives `7.700000000000001`. Any number that reaches the screen (slider readouts, stat card values, axis labels, data-point labels, tooltips, computed totals) must go through `Math.round()`, `.toFixed(n)`, or `Intl.NumberFormat`. Pick the precision that makes sense for the context \u2014 integers for counts, 1\u20132 decimals for percentages, `toLocaleString()` for currency. For range sliders, also set `step=\"1\"` (or step=\"0.1\" etc.) so the input itself emits round values.\n- Spacing: use rem for vertical rhythm (1rem, 1.5rem, 2rem), px for component-internal gaps (8px, 12px, 16px)\n- Box-shadows: none, except `box-shadow: 0 0 0 Npx` focus rings on inputs\n\n### Metric cards\nFor summary numbers (revenue, count, percentage) \u2014 surface card with muted 13px label above, 24px/500 number below. `background: var(--color-background-secondary)`, no border, `border-radius: var(--border-radius-md)`, padding 1rem. Use in grids of 2-4 with `gap: 12px`. Distinct from raised cards (which have white bg + border).\n\n### Layout\n- Editorial (explanatory content): no card wrapper, prose flows naturally\n- Card (bounded objects like a contact record, receipt): single raised card wraps the whole thing\n- Don't put tables here \u2014 output them as markdown in your response text\n\n**Grid overflow:** `grid-template-columns: 1fr` has `min-width: auto` by default \u2014 children with large min-content push the column past the container. Use `minmax(0, 1fr)` to clamp.\n\n**Table overflow:** Tables with many columns auto-expand past `width: 100%` if cell contents exceed it. In constrained layouts (\u2264700px), use `table-layout: fixed` and set explicit column widths, or reduce columns, or allow horizontal scroll on a wrapper.\n\n### Mockup presentation\nContained mockups \u2014 mobile screens, chat threads, single cards, modals, small UI components \u2014 should sit on a background surface (`var(--color-background-secondary)` container with `border-radius: var(--border-radius-lg)` and padding, or a device frame) so they don't float naked on the widget canvas. Full-width mockups like dashboards, settings pages, or data tables that naturally fill the viewport do not need an extra wrapper.\n\n### 1. Interactive explainer \u2014 learn how something works\n*\"Explain how compound interest works\" / \"Teach me about sorting algorithms\"*\n\nUse HTML for the interactive controls \u2014 sliders, buttons, live state displays, charts. Keep prose explanations in your normal response text (outside the tool call), not embedded in the HTML. No card wrapper. Whitespace is the container.\n\n```html\n<div style=\"display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 12px; margin: 0 0 1.5rem;\">\n  <label style=\"font-size: 14px; color: var(--color-text-secondary);\">Years</label>\n  <input type=\"range\" min=\"1\" max=\"40\" value=\"20\" id=\"years\" style=\"flex: 1;\" />\n  <span style=\"font-size: 14px; font-weight: 500; min-width: 24px;\" id=\"years-out\">20</span>\n</div>\n\n<div style=\"display: flex; align-items: baseline; gap: 8px; margin: 0 0 1.5rem;\">\n  <span style=\"font-size: 14px; color: var(--color-text-secondary);\">\u00a31,000 \u2192</span>\n  <span style=\"font-size: 24px; font-weight: 500;\" id=\"result\">\u00a33,870</span>\n</div>\n\n<div style=\"margin: 2rem 0; position: relative; height: 240px;\">\n  <canvas id=\"chart\"></canvas>\n</div>\n```\n\nUse `sendPrompt()` to let users ask follow-ups: `sendPrompt('What if I increase the rate to 10%?')`\n\n### 2. Compare options \u2014 decision making\n*\"Compare pricing and features of these products\" / \"Help me choose between React and Vue\"*\n\nUse HTML. Side-by-side card grid for options. Highlight differences with semantic colors. Interactive elements for filtering or weighting.\n\n- Each option in a card. Use badges for key differentiators. A leading Tabler icon (`<i class=\"ti ti-NAME\">` at 20px, `aria-hidden`) anchors each option visually \u2014 pick the most apt name per option.\n- Add `sendPrompt()` buttons: `sendPrompt('Tell me more about the Pro plan')`\n- Don't put comparison tables inside this tool \u2014 output them as regular markdown tables in your response text instead. The tool is for the visual card grid only.\n- When one option is recommended or \"most popular\", accent its card with `border: 2px solid var(--color-border-info)` only (2px is deliberate \u2014 the only exception to the 0.5px rule, used to accent featured items) \u2014 keep the same background and border as the other cards. Add a small badge (e.g. \"Most popular\") above or inside the card header using `background: var(--color-background-info); color: var(--color-text-info); font-size: 12px; padding: 4px 12px; border-radius: var(--border-radius-md)`.\n\n### 3. Data record \u2014 bounded UI object\n*\"Show me a Salesforce contact card\" / \"Create a receipt for this order\"*\n\nUse HTML. Wrap the entire thing in a single raised card. All content is sans-serif since it's pure UI. Use an avatar/initials circle for people (see example below).\n\n```html\n<div style=\"background: var(--color-background-primary); border-radius: var(--border-radius-lg); border: 0.5px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); padding: 1rem 1.25rem;\">\n  <div style=\"display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 12px; margin-bottom: 16px;\">\n    <div style=\"width: 44px; height: 44px; border-radius: 50%; background: var(--color-background-info); display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; font-weight: 500; font-size: 14px; color: var(--color-text-info);\">MR</div>\n    <div>\n      <p style=\"font-weight: 500; font-size: 15px; margin: 0;\">Maya Rodriguez</p>\n      <p style=\"font-size: 13px; color: var(--color-text-secondary); margin: 0;\">VP of Engineering</p>\n    </div>\n  </div>\n  <div style=\"border-top: 0.5px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); padding-top: 12px;\">\n    <table style=\"width: 100%; font-size: 13px;\">\n      <tr><td style=\"color: var(--color-text-secondary); padding: 4px 0;\"><i class=\"ti ti-mail\" style=\"font-size:16px; vertical-align:-2px; margin-right:6px\" aria-hidden=\"true\"></i>Email</td><td style=\"text-align: right; padding: 4px 0; color: var(--color-text-info);\">m.rodriguez@acme.com</td></tr>\n      <tr><td style=\"color: var(--color-text-secondary); padding: 4px 0;\"><i class=\"ti ti-phone\" style=\"font-size:16px; vertical-align:-2px; margin-right:6px\" aria-hidden=\"true\"></i>Phone</td><td style=\"text-align: right; padding: 4px 0;\">+1 (415) 555-0172</td></tr>\n    </table>\n  </div>\n</div>\n```\n\n\n## Color palette\n\n9 color ramps, each with 7 stops from lightest to darkest. 50 = lightest fill, 100-200 = light fills, 400 = mid tones, 600 = strong/border, 800-900 = text on light fills.\n\n| Class | Ramp | 50 (lightest) | 100 | 200 | 400 | 600 | 800 | 900 (darkest) |\n|-------|------|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|\n| `c-purple` | Purple | #EEEDFE | #CECBF6 | #AFA9EC | #7F77DD | #534AB7 | #3C3489 | #26215C |\n| `c-teal` | Teal | #E1F5EE | #9FE1CB | #5DCAA5 | #1D9E75 | #0F6E56 | #085041 | #04342C |\n| `c-coral` | Coral | #FAECE7 | #F5C4B3 | #F0997B | #D85A30 | #993C1D | #712B13 | #4A1B0C |\n| `c-pink` | Pink | #FBEAF0 | #F4C0D1 | #ED93B1 | #D4537E | #993556 | #72243E | #4B1528 |\n| `c-gray` | Gray | #F1EFE8 | #D3D1C7 | #B4B2A9 | #888780 | #5F5E5A | #444441 | #2C2C2A |\n| `c-blue` | Blue | #E6F1FB | #B5D4F4 | #85B7EB | #378ADD | #185FA5 | #0C447C | #042C53 |\n| `c-green` | Green | #EAF3DE | #C0DD97 | #97C459 | #639922 | #3B6D11 | #27500A | #173404 |\n| `c-amber` | Amber | #FAEEDA | #FAC775 | #EF9F27 | #BA7517 | #854F0B | #633806 | #412402 |\n| `c-red` | Red | #FCEBEB | #F7C1C1 | #F09595 | #E24B4A | #A32D2D | #791F1F | #501313 |\n\n**How to assign colors**: Color should encode meaning, not sequence. Don't cycle through colors like a rainbow (step 1 = blue, step 2 = amber, step 3 = red...). Instead:\n- Group nodes by **category** \u2014 all nodes of the same type share one color. E.g. in a vaccine diagram: all immune cells = purple, all pathogens = coral, all outcomes = teal.\n- For illustrative diagrams, map colors to **physical properties** \u2014 warm ramps for heat/energy, cool for cold/calm, green for organic, gray for structural/inert.\n- Use **gray for neutral/structural** nodes (start, end, generic steps).\n- Use **2-3 colors per diagram**, not 6+. More colors = more visual noise. A diagram with gray + purple + teal is cleaner than one using every ramp.\n- **Prefer purple, teal, coral, pink** for general diagram categories. Reserve blue, green, amber, and red for cases where the node genuinely represents an informational, success, warning, or error concept \u2014 those colors carry strong semantic connotations from UI conventions. (Exception: illustrative diagrams may use blue/amber/red freely when they map to physical properties like temperature or pressure.)\n\n**Text on colored backgrounds:** Always use the 800 or 900 stop from the same ramp as the fill. Never use black, gray, or --color-text-primary on colored fills. **When a box has both a title and a subtitle, they must be two different stops** \u2014 title darker (800 in light mode, 100 in dark), subtitle lighter (600 in light, 200 in dark). Same stop for both reads flat; the weight difference alone isn't enough. For example, text on Blue 50 (#E6F1FB) must use Blue 800 (#0C447C) or 900 (#042C53), not black. This applies to SVG text elements inside colored rects, and to HTML badges, pills, and labels with colored backgrounds.\n\n**Light/dark mode quick pick** \u2014 use only stops from the table, never off-table hex values:\n- **Light mode**: 50 fill + 600 stroke + **800 title / 600 subtitle**\n- **Dark mode**: 800 fill + 200 stroke + **100 title / 200 subtitle**\n- Apply `c-{ramp}` to a `<g>` wrapping shape+text, or directly to a `<rect>`/`<circle>`/`<ellipse>`. Never to `<path>` \u2014 paths don't get ramp fill. For colored connector strokes use inline `stroke=\"#...\"` (any mid-ramp hex works in both modes). Dark mode is automatic for ramp classes. Available: c-gray, c-blue, c-red, c-amber, c-green, c-teal, c-purple, c-coral, c-pink.\n\nFor status/semantic meaning in UI (success, warning, danger) use CSS variables. For categorical coloring in both diagrams and UI, use these ramps.\n",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Three observations from the preview that are worth thinking about before coding:\n\n**The light load (middle one, outlined) is probably your target.** Watercolor-y, pigments clearly distinguishable, intersections read as their own colors rather than \"rose with yellow on top.\" The very-light version is so pastel it might disappear into the page; the medium version is rich but starting to feel like a logo more than a wash.\n\n**The intersection colors are physically real but might surprise you.** The all-three center is `#546143` \u2014 a muted olive-brown. That's K-M doing its job: three pigments physically stacked absorb most light, leaving a desaturated dark earth tone. *This is correct watercolor behavior* (it's what happens on paper), but it's notably different from CMY subtractive theory which says R+Y+B should be black. If you want a darker, more dramatic center, you'd need to bump up the load. If you want a *lighter* center, the load is already pretty low.\n\n**A small caveat about the preview.** I used `mix-blend-mode: multiply` to approximate the K-M layering, with the per-region colors precomputed from K-M math. The intersections show the *exact* colors the lib would produce (those are the swatches below). But CSS `multiply` blending isn't quite the same operation as K-M \u2014 the actual lib output may have slightly more interesting variation at the boundaries (granulation, edge darkening, slightly different mixing near the wet edges). The preview is faithful in color, approximate in texture.\n\n## Questions before building the preset\n\nIf this looks like roughly what you want:\n\n1. **Are you good with the \"light load\" version**, or want to see something between very-light and light?\n2. **The classic Venn arrangement** has the three circles overlapping in the center. The preview uses a tight arrangement (~70% of each circle overlaps another). Want me to try a looser arrangement (only ~30% overlap, so the single-pigment areas are more dominant)?\n3. **Background paper color** matters. The preview uses an ivory paper. Would white paper, or a warmer cream, work better with your portfolio's palette?\n\nIf the colors are wrong (the orange R+Y, the muted-olive center) or the proportions feel off, *now* is the time to say so \u2014 much cheaper to adjust here than after we've wired the preset.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

        "text": "Flexoki uses warm monochromatic base values that blend the black value with the paper value. 8 values are used in light and dark mode: ... Incremental values can be derived using opacity.\n\nFlexoki is an inky color scheme for prose and code. Flexoki is designed for reading and writing on digital screens. It is inspired by analog inks and warm shades of paper. Flexoki is minimalistic and high-contrast. The colors are calibrated for legibility and perceptual balance across devices and when switching between light and dark modes. Flexoki is open-source under the MIT license. Flexoki is available for dozens of popular apps, including Obsidian using my theme Minimal. Flexoki is the color palette used on this site. To switch between light and dark mode press the D key or use the toggle at the top of the page. Click any swatch to copy a color to your clipboard. ... I created Flexoki for my personal site, stephango.com. You\u2019re reading it now. I wanted the colors to feel distinctive yet familiar. Like ink on paper. The name Flexoki comes from flexography \u2014 a common printing process for paper and cardboard1.\n\nNote that paper and black are special values that represent the lightest and darkest colors in the palette, equivalent to 0 and 1000. Flexoki emulates the feeling of pigment on paper by exponentially increasing intensity as colors get lighter or darker. This makes the colors feel vibrant and warm, like watercolor inks. ... This table describes how to use each variable in the context of user interfaces and syntax highlighting. Flexoki is available for the following apps and tools. See the full list. ... Flexoki is MIT licensed. You are free to port Flexoki to any app. Please include attribution and a link to stephango.com/flexoki. You can submit your port to the list via pull request on the Flexoki repo. I also have a dog named Flexo whose greatness deserved to be immortalized.\n\nAnother challenge with digital color is human perception across color spaces. For example, yellow appears much brighter than blue. Ethan Schoonover\u2019s color scheme Solarized (2011) was an important inspiration for Flexoki. His emphasis on lightness relationships in the CIELAB color space helped me understand how to find colors that appear cohesive. Flexoki derives colors from the more recent Oklab color space to maintain those perceptual relationships at the light and dark ends of the spectrum \u2014 ramping up color intensity exponentially to emulate the vibrancy that pigments exhibit even when diluted. I found that choosing colors with perfect perceptual consistency can be at odds with the distinctiveness of colors in practical applications like syntax highlighting. If you adhere too closely to evenness in perceptual lightness you can end up with a palette that looks washed out and difficult to parse.\n\nFlexoki uses warm monochromatic base values that blend the black value with the paper value. 8 values are used in light and dark mode: ... Incremental values can be derived using opacity. For example, you can use a 60% opacity black value on top of the paper value to create the 600 value. 8 accent colors are available for accents and syntax highlighting. Unlike the base values, accent values cannot be derived using opacity because this desaturates the pigment effect. Use the extended palette for the full range of values. The following 16 values are the main accent values used for syntax highlighting and interface elements like buttons and links. Light themes should use 600 for syntax highlighted text, dark themes should use 400. If you wish to use Flexoki for more complex applications beyond syntax highlighting and basic color schemes, the extended palette includes a complete set of values for every accent color from 50 to 950.",

This package provides: Dark and light ggplot2 themes that match the aesthetic of Flexoki\u2019s code editor styles. Custom color and fill scales for discrete, continuous, and binned data using Flexoki\u2019s extended color schemes. Color palettes in the flexoki object, which is a list of palette vectors. Flexoki focuses on color schemes rather than traditional UI components: Base Palette: Core colors for prose and code, including grayscale and accent colors (400/600 values). Extended Palette: Full range of values (50-950) for UI design and complex projects. CSS Variables: Defined in flexoki.css for easy integration into projects.\n\nFlexoki is a free, open-source color scheme created by Steph Ango, aimed at enhancing the reading and writing experience on digital screens. Its primary goal is to bring the comfort of analog color\u2014reminiscent of ink on paper\u2014to emissive screens, making it ideal for developers, writers, and designers working on syntax highlighting, UI design, and prose. The scheme draws inspiration from flexography, a printing process for paper, and uses the Oklab color space to maintain perceptual consistency while emulating the vibrancy of watercolor pigments, ensuring colors remain distinct and warm across light and dark themes. Analog-Inspired Colors: Mimics ink-on-paper aesthetics with warm paper tones and vibrant hues. Extended Palette: Includes a full range of values (50 to 950) for every accent color, plus special paper and black values. Light and Dark Modes: Offers both modes with exponentially increasing intensity for vibrant yet cohesive colors.\n\nCode Editors: Ports for VS Code, Neovim, Emacs, and iTerm2, among others. Design Tools: Available for Figma, enabling designers to use the scheme in mockups. Obsidian: Integrated as a theme, created by Steph Ango for his personal vault. Web Projects: Used in Jekyll-based sites like stephango.com, compiled with Netlify. Flexoki is a free color scheme for digital screens, inspired by analog inks. It has been ported to Tailwind CSS by the community, allowing its colors to be used as Tailwind classes. Yes, it\u2019s fully free and open-source under the MIT license. Continue reading \u2192\n\nAbout a year ago at SIGGRAPH Asia 2021 (which took place as a hybrid conference both online and on site at the Tokyo International Forum) one of the technical papers that caught my attention was the publication by \u0160\u00e1rka Sochorov\u00e1 and Ond\u0159ej Jamri\u0161ka on color mixing. Color mixing in most digital painting tools is infamously unsatisfying, often limited to a linear interpolation in RBG space, resulting in unpleasing gradients very different from what one would expect. Ten years ago I mentioned this article that presented the color mixing of the application Paper, which tried to solve this very problem. \n\nThis time, the core idea is to model colors as pigments: estimate the pigment concentration based on the color, so in a way, move from RGB space to \u201cpigment space\u201d, and interpolate the pigment concentration, before converting back to RGB space.\n\nFinally, the implementation of the proposed model, Mixbox, is available under a CC BY-NC license: https://github.com/scrtwpns/mixbox Two Minute Papers did a video on this paper as well: ... Paper is a drawing application made by FiftyThree for the iPad, that has earned some attention. FastCompany has a story... ...         "text": "In this paper, we introduce a practical approach that enables mixing colors with K-M while keeping everything in RGB. We achieve this by establishing a latent color space, where RGB colors are represented as mixtures of primary pigments together with additive residuals.\n\nPractical Pigment Mixing for Digital Painting \u00b7 Session08: Natural Phenomena [Q&A Session] Authors \u00b7 \u0160\u00e1rka Sochorov\u00e1 \u00b7 Ond\u0159ej Jamri\u0161ka \u00b7 Event Type \u00b7 Technical Papers \u00b7 Hybrid Formats \u00b7 Registration Categories \u00b7 TimeWednesday, December 1511:44am - 11:55am JST \u00b7 LocationHall B5 (1) (5F, B Block) & Virtual Platform \u00b7 DescriptionThere is a significant flaw in today's painting software: the colors do not mix like actual paints. E.g., blue and yellow make gray instead of green. This is because the software is built around the RGB representation, which models the mixing of colored lights. Paints, however, get their color from pigments, whose mixing behavior is predicted by the Kubelka-Munk model (K-M). \n\nAlthough it was introduced to computer graphics almost 30 years ago, the K-M model has never been adopted by painting software in practice as it would require giving up the RGB representation, growing the number of per-pixel channels substantially, and depriving the users of painting with arbi\n\nIn this paper, we introduce a practical approach that enables mixing colors with K-M while keeping everything in RGB. We achieve this by establishing a latent color space, where RGB colors are represented as mixtures of primary pigments together with additive residuals. The latents can be manipulated with linear operations, leading to expected, plausible results. We describe the conversion between RGB and our latent representation and show how to implement it efficiently. \n\nWe prove the viability of our approach on the case of major painting software whose developers integrated our mixing method with minimal effort, making it the first real-world software to provide realistic color mixing in history.",

E.g., blue and yellow make gray instead of green. This is because the software is built around the RGB representation, which models the mixing of colored lights. Paints, however, get their color from pigments, whose mixing behavior is predicted by the Kubelka-Munk model (K-M). Although it was introduced to computer graphics almost 30 years ago, the K-M model has never been adopted by painting software in practice as it would require giving up the RGB representation, growing the number of per-pixel channels substantially, and depriving the users of painting with arbitrary RGB colors. In this paper, we introduce a practical approach that enables mixing colors with K-M while keeping everything in RGB.\n\n\n\n\u0160\u00e1rka Sochorov\u00e1 Ond\u0159ej Jamri\u0161ka \u00b7 ACM Transactions on Graphics (Proceedings of SIGGRAPH Asia 2021) 40(6):234, 2021 \u00b7 There is a significant flaw in today's painting software: the colors do not mix like actual paints. Paints, however, get their color from pigments, whose mixing behavior is predicted by the Kubelka-Munk model (K-M). Although it was introduced to computer graphics almost 30 years ago, the K-M model has never been adopted by painting software in practice as it would require giving up the RGB representation, growing the number of per-pixel channels substantially, and depriving the users of painting with arbitrary RGB colors. In this paper, we introduce a practical approach that enables mixing colors with K-M while keeping everything in RGB.",

        "title": "GitHub - justinh-rahb/filament-mixer: Physics-based color mixing for 3D printer filaments using Kubelka-Munk theory. \u00b7 GitHub",
        "url": "https://github.com/justinh-rahb/filament-mixer",
        "metadata": {
          "type": "webpage_metadata",
          "site_domain": "github.com",
          "favicon_url": "https://www.google.com/s2/favicons?sz=64&domain=github.com",

        "is_missing": false,
        "text": "Based on the Mixbox paper by Sochorov\u00e1 &amp; Jamri\u0161ka (2021). The result: mixing blue + yellow gives you green, not gray. Production Ready. This project implements the Kubelka-Munk mixing pipeline with two production-ready learned models: PolyMixer ...\n\nReplaces naive RGB interpolation \u2014 which produces muddy, desaturated blends \u2014 with the same spectral approach used for real paint/pigment mixing. Based on the Mixbox paper by Sochorov\u00e1 & Jamri\u0161ka (2021). The result: mixing blue + yellow gives you green, not gray. Production Ready. \n\nSpectral Mixing (K-M Theory): Each filament is defined by its absorption (K) and scattering (S) spectra across 38 wavelengths (380\u2013750nm). Colors are mixed by linearly combining K and S values \u2014 this is physically correct for pigment mixtures. Reflectance \u2192 Color: The mixed K/S spectra are converted to a reflectance curve, then integrated against the CIE 1931 observer functions and D65 illuminant to produce XYZ tristimulus values, which are finally transformed to sRGB. Unmixing (Inverse Problem): Given a target RGB color, constrained optimization (SLSQP) finds the pigment concentrations that best reproduce it \u2014 this is how get_filament_ratios() works.\n\nApproximate spectra. Our pigment K/S curves are Gaussian approximations, not measured from real filaments. Certain color combinations will be less accurate than others. Runtime optimization. unmix() solves a constrained optimization (SLSQP) on every call \u2014 slow compared to Mixbox's LUT lookup. \n\nThe lut.py module can precompute tables, but they're based on the same approximate spectra. No end-to-end training. Mixbox trains its encoder/decoder and LUT jointly to minimize perceptual error across the full gamut. We don't do that. Limited gamut. Colors far outside the palette's reproducible gamut rely on the RGB residual, which is a linear correction \u2014 it works but it's not perceptually optimal. Mixbox is CC BY-NC 4.0 (non-commercial only, commercial license required). This",

        "text": "To make realistic pigment mixing more practical, we created Mixbox. It hides all the physics inside an RGB-in RGB-out box, making it easy to implement into any painting software. Doing graphics research is really exciting.\n\nHi, I'm \u0160\u00e1rka Sochorov\u00e1, and together with my Co-Founder Ond\u0159ej Jamri\u0161ka we started Secret Weapons, a software company that makes powerful tools for visual artists. We met at the Czech Technical University in Prague, where Ond\u0159ej worked as a computer graphics researcher, and I joined their team to do my Ph.D. I had a background in Computer Science and Traditional Animation, so Ond\u0159ej's research was fascinating to me because it was the perfect merge of art and technology. He co-authored several papers on example-based synthesis (LazyFluids, StyLit, FaceStyle), which is a style-transfer method where you provide an example of the look you want to achieve, and the algorithm applies it on a 3D model, CG simulation, or photograph. \n\nSoon after I joined, we started experimenting with transferring the style from paintings to arbitrary videos. That was the birth of EbSynth and the first project we worked on together.\n\nRecently, we published our latest research on practical pigment mixing for digital painting. Surprisingly, the majority of painting programs don\u2019t mix colors as real paints even though the mathematical model for simulating it is well known. To make realistic pigment mixing more practical, we created Mixbox. It hides all the physics inside an RGB-in RGB-out box, making it easy to implement into any painting software. Doing graphics research is really exciting. But after years of doing SIGGRAPH papers, Ond\u0159ej really wanted to start getting those techniques into the hands of artists. I loved the idea because I never saw myself as an academic anyway. We both enjoyed doing research, but we really wanted to turn it into something practical that regular people could actually use.",

    "text": "Confirmed. **\"Practical Pigment Mixing for Digital Painting\"** by \u0160\u00e1rka Sochorov\u00e1 & Ond\u0159ej Jamri\u0161ka, SIGGRAPH Asia 2021 (Mixbox). And the connection is *beautiful* \u2014 Mixbox is a different approach to the same K-M problem Washes solves. They published in 2021; Curtis et al. (the paper Washes is built on) published in 1997; both use Kubelka-Munk. Mixbox compresses K-M into a \"RGB-in RGB-out box\" for general digital painting tools; Washes implements the full physical sim. They're sibling approaches to the same lineage, 24 years apart.\n\nThat's a real provenance story worth telling. Let me write the section.       "new_str": "      <!-- ============================== ORIGIN ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"origin\">\n        <h1 id=\"origin\">Origin</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          Washes exists because of a chain of conversations at the\n          Recurse Center. The lineage is worth recording &mdash; both\n          out of gratitude to the people involved, and because the\n          path that led here is itself an example of how good\n          technical work tends to happen: through casual recommendation,\n          shared enthusiasm, and the slow accretion of someone else's\n          interesting work into your own.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"origin-nerd-snipe\">The nerd-snipe</h2>\n        <p>\n          The project began as a nerd-snipe by\n          <strong>Dan Knutson</strong>, via\n          <strong>Cyrene Zhang</strong>, and\n          <strong>Kanad Gupta</strong>, all at the Recurse Center.\n          The combination of inputs:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Dan</strong> had implemented the watercolor paper texture &mdash; the fibrous, slightly absorbent substrate that real watercolor washes interact with &mdash; by hand. Seeing someone build the <em>paper</em> rather than the paint made the question \"what would the paint look like running over this?\" feel concrete and answerable.</li>\n          <li><strong>Kanad</strong> posted his favorite coding color palette: <a href=\"https://stephango.com/flexoki\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Flexoki</a> by Steph Ango, an inky color scheme designed around the feeling of pigment on paper. Flexoki itself is built on the Oklab color space and explicitly emulates the way watercolor pigments grow vibrant as they're diluted &mdash; the same physical intuition that drives the Kubelka-Munk model underneath Washes.</li>\n          <li>The Flexoki page <a href=\"https://stephango.com/flexoki\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">on stephango.com</a> links to a SIGGRAPH paper presentation that turned out to be the conceptual catalyst: <em>Practical Pigment Mixing for Digital Painting</em> by \u0160\u00e1rka Sochorov\u00e1 and Ond\u0159ej Jamri\u0161ka (SIGGRAPH Asia 2021) &mdash; the Mixbox paper. Watching the <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6JVjHqas60Q\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">fast-forward presentation</a> made vivid how badly most digital painting tools mix color, and that there was a well-understood physical model for fixing it.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Mixbox and Washes are siblings in the same intellectual\n          lineage. Both use Kubelka-Munk pigment compositing; both\n          treat colors as physical materials rather than additive\n          light. Mixbox publishes a <em>practical</em>\n          RGB-in-RGB-out approximation that any painting tool can\n          drop in. Washes implements the <em>full physical sim</em>\n          from Curtis et al. 1997, including the shallow-water\n          fluid layer underneath. They're answering the same\n          underlying question &mdash; what if digital paint behaved\n          like real paint? &mdash; from different angles and 24 years\n          apart.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"origin-conversations\">Conversations along the way</h2>\n        <p>\n          The project grew through extended conversations with\n          <strong>Evan Gedrich Pintado</strong>,\n          <strong>Iris Fernandes Valdez</strong>,\n          <strong>Lissa Hyacinth</strong>, and\n          <strong>Jonathan King</strong> &mdash; all of whom helped\n          turn \"this is a cool video\" into \"I'm going to actually\n          build this.\" The conversations were partly about the math\n          (Kubelka-Munk, advection, the shallow-water equations) and\n          partly about the design (what the interaction should feel\n          like, what the API surface should look like, what makes a\n          watercolor sim feel watercolor-y rather than fluid-sim-y).\n          Both kinds of conversation mattered, and both kinds of\n          person are reflected in the lib's eventual shape.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"origin-recurse-center\">The Recurse Center</h2>\n        <p>\n          Worth naming explicitly: this is the kind of project that\n          gets built at the <a href=\"https://www.recurse.com/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Recurse Center</a>\n          and very rarely elsewhere. The combination of people who\n          will recommend you a 28-year-old SIGGRAPH paper at\n          lunchtime, a culture that treats \"I want to build a thing\n          that does X\" as a sufficient justification on its own, and\n          an environment where domain crossover happens naturally\n          &mdash; someone implementing paper textures, someone\n          curating coding color palettes, someone interested in\n          pigment physics &mdash; is what makes a project like this\n          conceivable in the first place.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The references and prior-art sections that follow are\n          academic in tone, but the project's actual origin is more\n          social than academic. Curtis et al. published the paper in\n          1997. Sochorov\u00e1 &amp; Jamri\u0161ka republished an approximation\n          in 2021. Steph Ango wrote about pigments on paper. Dan\n          built the paper. Kanad shared the palette. Cyrene made the\n          introduction. Evan, Iris, Lissa, and Jonathan kept the\n          enthusiasm going. The math came from the academy; the\n          motivation came from people.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== REFERENCES ============================== -->",

Each of these experiments and my passion for generative art and design systems, has led me ...\n\nColor is my laboratory of choice. Choosing colors one by one and checking them against each other always felt slow and brittle. So inspired by Pixel Artist, I started with RampenSau, which explores color ramps by cycling hues and easing curves, surfacing palettes I wouldn't have thought to try. This led me to Poline, which lets you place anchors and draw relationships between them, then lets math do the blending. Later came RYBitten, which translates colors through Johannes Itten's subtractive wheel\u2014an experiment in making digital color behave more like paint. Each of these experiments and my passion for generative art and design systems, has led me further into the idea that relationships matter more than individual choices. Lately I've been exploring what happens when palettes themselves become dynamic. Color Router treats color decisions as formulas rather than fixed values: \"darken primary by 15%,\" \"choose highest-contrast brand color.\"\n\nI keep coming back to this idea: what if we spent less time making individual decisions and more time designing how decisions get made? Less manual color picking, more relationship building. Less fixed outcomes, more intelligent systems. Maybe that's all these tools really are\u2014ways to be lazy more intelligently. Ways to automate the boring parts so I can focus on the parts that actually matter: the logic, the relationships, the unexpected combinations that emerge when systems start talking to each other. ... Color palette generation function using hue cycling and simple easing functions. ... Color-Description turns a technical color representation into a human readable description. ... Implementation of pseudo RYB color conversions derived from Johannes Itten's color wheel. ... I use my passion and enthusiasm as a tool to promote ideas and skills.\n\nAn interactive color picker where palettes are sculpted directly on a color model using perceptual distance. A palette extractor that picks image colors in OKLCh with harmony-aware, perceptual selection. Transforms a single color into a full palette by copying the perceptual structure of existing design systems. A TypeScript library for generating perceptually tuned OKLCh color harmonies and palette variations. A circular playground for exploring palettes and color relationships with the Poline engine. A reactive TypeScript color system that treats palettes like spreadsheets with references, functions, and cascading updates. A large-format book featuring generative gradients created with Rik Oostenbroek, exploring where handcrafted color transitions meet algorithmic composition. Featured at Paris Photo 2024. A color conversion library for translating between RGB and Itten-inspired RYB color spaces.\n\nA generative study of color interaction inspired by Josef Albers and the square. A micro-library for generating color palettes by interpolating HSL in cartesian space. ... Most Popular color-name REST API returning color names for any given color value. ... Palette generation library using hue cycling and easing functions. A collection of generative art pieces released as on-chain tokens on fxhash. A library that turns technical color values into human-readable color descriptions. The companion site for a collaborative color bot that posts random colors and collects invented names. A generative color harmony cycler that moves through hue ranges to build palettes. A curated dataset of more than 30,000 color names collected and invented over the years. Plots the entire color-names collection across multiple 3D color models to reveal where the dataset is dense, sparse, or unevenly distributed.",

This is the 56th issue of the Gorilla Newsletter - a weekly online publication that sums up everything noteworthy from the past week in generative art, creative coding, web3, tech and AI - with a spritz of gorilla updates. That said, hope that you're all having an awesome start into the new week!\n\nDavid Aerne is a software dev, creative coder, generative artist with a big passion for colors - most of his time you'll find him diligently building tools for the purpose of manipulating colors in all sorts of different ways; from their nomenclature, to palette configuration and other related tasks. David recently announced his newest creation titled RYBitten - a little library that lets you generate color spectrums according to Johannes Itten's color wheel theory: ... So, who's Johannes Itten and what did he have to say about colors? What's actually the RYB color space? And how do these two things come together in Aerne's tool? Johannes Itten is a famous expressionist painter, teacher and writer that was associated with the early Bauhaus between 1919 to 1922. Most famously he's known for his contributions in color theory however. In his book \"The Art of Color\" he suggests a 12 part color wheel that is constructed by interpolating between the primary and secondary colors in the center\n\nWhen David Morgan created his esoteric programming language in 1991 he initially wanted to call it Mondrian - but apparently some people had beat him to it. Piet is however a much more interesting language and has acquired somewhat of a cult following in the 30+ years of its existence. Here's some examples of what Piet programs look like: A Hello World program by David Morgan, a program that solves the Towers of Hanoi problem, and a Prime Number Generator by Sylvain Tintillier, respectively. Clearly, written in the Piet programming language. After reading Morgan's specification page a couple of times, I think I've been able to acquire a rudimentary understanding of the language - and will make an attempt at providing an explanation. Conceptually it's quite simple, but the complexity emerges from the manner in which instructions are coded into these colorful bitmaps.\n\nIf you want to learn more about David Morgan, the origin story of the Piet programming language, and some of his perspectives on eso-langs in general, you'll want to check out this interview from 2015 on Esoteric Codes: ... David has created some of the best-known esolangs, including Chef and Piet, which exress code within other rule-based systems, and Whenever, a language that overturns a key element of how code is controlled. ... But that's just scratching the tip of the esolang iceberg - there's an entire culture around it. A number of other interesting Eso-langs is introduced in this article: ... Open-ended, community based, and collaborative, \u201cesolangs\u201d serve as a reminder that digital art has other histories and other futures. ... Big shoutout to Chris Reid and his own genart Newsletter - only reason I actually discovered Piet. Arguably the most important piece of content in the genart space from last week was Camille Roux's new genart podcast.",

      "new_str": "      <!-- ============================== ORIGIN ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"origin\">\n        <h1 id=\"origin\">Origin</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          Washes exists because of a chain of conversations and\n          shared links among friends. The path that led here is\n          worth recording, both out of gratitude and because it's\n          itself an example of how technical work tends to happen\n          &mdash; through casual recommendation, shared enthusiasm,\n          and the slow accretion of other people's interesting\n          work into one's own.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"origin-nerd-snipe\">The nerd-snipe</h2>\n        <p>\n          The project began as a nerd-snipe by\n          <strong>Dan Knutson</strong>, via\n          <strong>Cyrene Zhang</strong>, and\n          <strong>Kanad Gupta</strong>. The combination of inputs:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Dan</strong> had implemented the watercolor paper texture &mdash; the fibrous, slightly absorbent substrate that real watercolor washes interact with &mdash; by hand. Seeing someone build the <em>paper</em> rather than the paint made the question \"what would the paint look like running over this?\" feel concrete and answerable.</li>\n          <li><strong>Kanad</strong> shared his favorite coding color palette: <a href=\"https://stephango.com/flexoki\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Flexoki</a> by Steph Ango, an inky color scheme designed around the feeling of pigment on paper. Flexoki is built on the Oklab color space and explicitly emulates the way watercolor pigments grow vibrant as they're diluted &mdash; the same physical intuition that drives the Kubelka-Munk model underneath Washes.</li>\n          <li>The Flexoki page <a href=\"https://stephango.com/flexoki\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">on stephango.com</a> links to a SIGGRAPH paper presentation that turned out to be the conceptual catalyst: <em>Practical Pigment Mixing for Digital Painting</em> by \u0160\u00e1rka Sochorov\u00e1 and Ond\u0159ej Jamri\u0161ka (SIGGRAPH Asia 2021) &mdash; the Mixbox paper. Watching the <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6JVjHqas60Q\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">fast-forward presentation</a> made vivid how badly most digital painting tools mix color, and that there was a well-understood physical model for fixing it.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Mixbox and Washes are siblings in the same intellectual\n          lineage. Both use Kubelka-Munk pigment compositing; both\n          treat colors as physical materials rather than additive\n          light. Mixbox publishes a <em>practical</em>\n          RGB-in-RGB-out approximation that any painting tool can\n          drop in. Washes implements the <em>full physical sim</em>\n          from Curtis et al. 1997, including the shallow-water\n          fluid layer underneath. They're answering the same\n          underlying question &mdash; what if digital paint\n          behaved like real paint? &mdash; from different angles\n          and 24 years apart.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"origin-conversations\">Conversations about color and math</h2>\n        <p>\n          Once the project was underway, extended conversations\n          with <strong>Evan Gedrich Pintado</strong>,\n          <strong>Iris Fernandes Valdez</strong>,\n          <strong>Lissa Hyacinth</strong>, and\n          <strong>Jonathan King</strong> shaped the project's\n          intellectual scaffolding &mdash; high-level discussion\n          of the math and physics (Kubelka-Munk, the shallow-water\n          equations, advection) and of other color tools doing\n          adjacent work in the same broad space.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Two of those tools deserve specific mention, both by\n          <strong>David Aerne</strong> (meodai), because they're\n          working on closely-related problems:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong><a href=\"https://meodai.github.io/RYBitten/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">RYBitten</a></strong> &mdash; translates digital colors through Johannes Itten's RYB (red/yellow/blue) subtractive color wheel from the Bauhaus tradition. The explicit goal is \"making digital color behave more like paint\" &mdash; the same underlying impulse that motivates K-M, Mixbox, and Washes, approached through art-theoretic rather than physics-based mixing.</li>\n          <li><strong><a href=\"https://okpalette.color.pizza/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">OKPalette</a></strong> &mdash; a palette extractor that picks colors from images using OKLCh perceptual distance and harmony-aware selection. Different problem (extracting palettes vs. mixing pigments) but the same recognition that <em>perceptual</em> color spaces and <em>physical</em> mixing models both matter for color work that goes beyond the default RGB representation.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Both tools come from the same observation Mixbox makes\n          and Washes embodies: that digital color work has\n          something to learn from how color actually behaves in\n          the physical world (as pigment, as ink, as light\n          falling on paper), and that the default representations\n          in most software are working against that intuition. The\n          Washes project sits inside this broader conversation,\n          and the conversations made the project feel like part\n          of a movement rather than an isolated reimplementation.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"origin-context\">Context</h2>\n        <p>\n          For the record, much of this happened during a batch at\n          the <a href=\"https://www.recurse.com/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Recurse Center</a>\n          &mdash; the kind of environment where someone will\n          recommend you a 28-year-old SIGGRAPH paper at lunch and\n          treat that as an unremarkable thing to do. References\n          and prior-art sections that follow are academic in\n          tone, but the project's actual origin is more social\n          than academic. Curtis et al. published the paper in\n          1997. Sochorov\u00e1 &amp; Jamri\u0161ka republished an\n          approximation in 2021. Aerne built RYBitten and\n          OKPalette. Steph Ango wrote about pigments on paper.\n          Dan built the paper. Kanad shared the palette. Cyrene\n          made the introduction. Evan, Iris, Lissa, and Jonathan\n          kept the math and color discussions going. The math\n          came from the academy; the motivation came from people.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== REFERENCES ============================== -->",

Both use Kubelka-Munk pigment compositing; both\n          treat colors as physical materials rather than additive\n          light. Mixbox publishes a <em>practical</em>\n          RGB-in-RGB-out approximation that any painting tool can\n          drop in. Washes implements the <em>full physical sim</em>\n          from Curtis et al. 1997, including the shallow-water\n          fluid layer underneath. They're answering the same\n          underlying question &mdash; what if digital paint behaved\n          like real paint? &mdash; from different angles and 24 years\n          apart.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"origin-conversations\">Conversations along the way</h2>\n        <p>\n          The project grew through extended conversations with\n          <strong>Evan Gedrich Pintado</strong>,\n          <strong>Iris Fernandes Valdez</strong>,\n          <strong>Lissa Hyacinth</strong>, and\n          <strong>Jonathan King</strong> &mdash; all of whom helped\n          turn \"this is a cool video\" into \"I'm going to actually\n          build this.\" The conversations were partly about the math\n          (Kubelka-Munk, advection, the shallow-water equations) and\n          partly about the design (what the interaction should feel\n          like, what the API surface should look like, what makes a\n          watercolor sim feel watercolor-y rather than fluid-sim-y).\n          Both kinds of conversation mattered, and both kinds of\n          person are reflected in the lib's eventual shape.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"origin-recurse-center\">The Recurse Center</h2>\n        <p>\n          Worth naming explicitly: this is the kind of project that\n          gets built at the <a href=\"https://www.recurse.com/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\">Recurse Center</a>\n          and very rarely elsewhere. The combination of people who\n          will recommend you a 28-year-old SIGGRAPH paper at\n          lunchtime, a culture that treats \"I want to build a thing\n          that does X\" as a sufficient justification on its own, and\n          an environment where domain crossover happens naturally\n          &mdash; someone implementing paper textures, someone\n          curating coding color palettes, someone interested in\n          pigment physics &mdash; is what makes a project like this\n          conceivable in the first place.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The references and prior-art sections that follow are\n          academic in tone, but the project's actual origin is more\n          social than academic. Curtis et al. published the paper in\n          1997. Sochorov\u00e1 &amp; Jamri\u0161ka republished an approximation\n          in 2021. Steph Ango wrote about pigments on paper. Dan\n          built the paper. Kanad shared the palette. Cyrene made the\n          introduction. Evan, Iris, Lissa, and Jonathan kept the\n          enthusiasm going. RC softened to a passing mention.** No longer its own subsection \u2014 now a single sentence at the start of a new \"Context\" subsection at the very end of the Origin section. The framing is \"for the record, much of this happened during a batch at RC\" rather than \"this kind of project only happens at RC.\" The intellectual lineage doesn't depend on the venue, and the docs now reflect that.\n\n**2. Conversations re-scoped.** The subsection title changed from \"Conversations along the way\" to \"Conversations about color and math,\" and the description was completely rewritten. The old version said the conversations covered both math *and* design (API surface, interaction shape) \u2014 that was an inference I made that wasn't accurate. Now correctly describes them as high-level discussion of the math and physics, plus other color tools in adjacent work.\n\n**3. RYBitten and OKPalette added.** Both by David Aerne, both fit the theme beautifully:\n\n- **RYBitten** \u2014 translates digital color through Johannes Itten's RYB subtractive wheel (Bauhaus tradition). David's own words: \"making digital color behave more like paint.\" Same underlying impulse as K-M and Mixbox, approached through art theory rather than physics. Linked to https://meodai.github.io/RYBitten/\n- **OKPalette** \u2014 palette extraction using OKLCh perceptual distance and harmony-aware selection. Linked to https://okpalette.color.pizza/\n\nThe closing paragraph now situates Washes inside a broader conversation: *\"The Washes project sits inside this broader conversation, and the conversations made the project feel like part of a movement rather than an isolated reimplementation.\"*\n\nThat's actually a more honest framing of where Washes sits than the previous draft. There's a real moment happening right now in digital color tools \u2014 Mixbox, Flexoki, RYBitten, OKPalette, and Washes are all responding to the same underlying observation that default RGB/HSL representations don't serve color work well. Naming that movement makes Washes feel like a legitimate contribution to it rather than a one-off reimplementation of a 28-year-old paper. That's a better story.",

Same strict\\n// match semantics: only the exact pure primaries return a pigment\\n// index; anything else returns null.\\nfunction _colorToPigment(color) {\\n  const rgb = _parseSvgColor(color);\\n  if (rgb === null) return null;\\n  // Round to 0..255 ints and check for the exact trigger primaries.\\n  const r = Math.round(rgb[0] * 255);\\n  const g = Math.round(rgb[1] * 255);\\n  const b = Math.round(rgb[2] * 255);\\n  if (r === 255 && g === 0   && b === 0  ) return 0;              // rose\\n  if (r === 255 && g === 255 && b === 0  ) return 1;              // yellow\\n  if (r === 0   && g === 0   && b === 255) return 2;              // blue\\n  if (r === 0   && g === 255 && b === 0  ) return MASK_INDEX;     // mask\\n  if (r === 255 && g === 0   && b === 255) return RAINBOW_INDEX;  // rainbow\\n  return null;\\n}\\n\\n// v0.43 \\u2014 Parse a CSS/SVG color string into [r, g, b] components in\\n// 0..1, or null if unparseable. Supports:\\n//   - 3-digit hex:  #F00\\n//   - 6-digit hex:  #FF0000\\n//   - rgb(r,g,b):   absolute 0..255 OR percentages\\n//   - rgba(...):    alpha ignored\\n//   - named colors: a curated subset covering common usage\\n// Returns null for 'none', 'transparent', empty, null, or unrecognized.\\n//\\n// Named colors omit the trigger set (red/yellow/blue/lime/magenta/\\n// fuchsia) since those go through _colorToPigment for trigger handling\\n// first. If approximation reaches here with one of those names, the\\n// caller should already have intercepted it.\\nfunction _parseSvgColor(c) {\\n  if (!c || typeof c !== 'string') return null;\\n  let s = c.trim().toLowerCase();\\n  if (!s || s === 'none' || s === 'transparent' || s === 'currentcolor') {\\n    return null;\\n  }\\n  // 3-digit hex\\n  let m = s.match(/^#([0-9a-f])([0-9a-f])([0-9a-f])$/);\\n  if (m) return [\\n    parseInt(m[1] + m[1], 16) / 255,\\n    parseInt(m[2] + m[2], 16) / 255,\\n    parseInt(m[3] + m[3], 16) / 255,\\n  ];\\n  // 6-digit hex\\n  m = s.match(/^#([0-9a-f]{2})([0-9a-f]{2})([0-9a-f]{2})$/);\\n  if (m) return [\\n    parseInt(m[1], 16) / 255,\\n    parseInt(m[2], 16) / 255,\\n    parseInt(m[3], 16) / 255,\\n  ];\\n  // rgb() or rgba() \\u2014 absolute (0..255) or percentage; alpha ignored.\\n  // Accepts both comma and whitespace-separated forms.\\n  m = s.match(/^rgba?\\\\s*\\\\(\\\\s*([\\\\d.]+)\\\\s*(%?)[\\\\s,]+([\\\\d.]+)\\\\s*(%?)[\\\\s,]+([\\\\d.]+)\\\\s*(%?)(?:[\\\\s,]+[\\\\d.]+\\\\s*%?)?\\\\s*\\\\)$/);\\n  if (m) {\\n    const parse = (v, isPct) => {\\n      const n = parseFloat(v);\\n      if (!isFinite(n)) return null;\\n      return isPct ? n / 100 : n / 255;\\n    };\\n    const r = parse(m[1], m[2] === '%');\\n    const g = parse(m[3], m[4] === '%');\\n    const b = parse(m[5], m[6] === '%');\\n    if (r === null || g === null || b === null) return null;\\n    return [\\n      Math.max(0, Math.min(1, r)),\\n      Math.max(0, Math.min(1, g)),\\n      Math.max(0, Math.min(1, b)),\\n    ];\\n  }\\n  // Named CSS colors (subset). I just need to set it as the default *if no option is provided* (currently defaults to `false`). \n\nNow #4 (open boundaries) \u2014 this is the substantive one. Let me think.\n\nThe lib currently treats canvas edges as **closed boundaries** \u2014 mass is conserved by the divergence correction (v0.68), and pigment accumulates at the corners (the \"expected corner hotspots\" from the test harness). For pigment/water to *fall off* the edge naturally:\n\n1. **Velocity at the edge** needs to *advect mass out* \u2014 fluid should flow past the boundary rather than reflecting/stagnating.\n2. **Pigment that advects past the edge** needs to be *discarded* rather than clamped back.\n3. **Pressure at the edge** needs an open-boundary condition \u2014 currently it's likely Dirichlet (fixed) or Neumann (reflective).\n\nThis is doable but it's a meaningful change.     "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Found it. The clamp `sx = max(0, min(GW-1.001, sx))` is what holds pigment at the edge \u2014 when a cell near the right edge tries to back-trace to a position past the right edge, it gets clamped to the edge, so it re-samples edge cells indefinitely, leading to accumulation.\n\nFor \"fall off the edge\" behavior, when the back-trace lands *outside* the grid, the cell should sample the **background (paper, zero pigment)** instead of clamping. Let me think about this more carefully.\n\nActually, the cleanest formulation: a cell whose back-trace position is *outside* the grid means \"in the previous frame, this cell's contents came from off-canvas.\" Treat that as **empty pigment** (paper color), which means the cell receives no pigment. Combined with the fact that pigment which advects toward the edge has its source-cell clamped \u2014 wait, that's the wrong direction.\n\nLet me re-think. Semi-Lagrangian is backward-trace. For an outflow at the right edge:\n- A cell just inside the right edge has velocity pointing *right* (outward)\n- Backward-trace from this cell goes *left* (inward) \u2192 samples interior pigment \u2192 correct\n- A cell *outside* the right edge would back-trace from further outside... but there are no cells outside the grid\n\nThe issue is different. Cells *outside* the grid don't exist, so what happens at the boundary now:\n- A cell at x = GW-2 has velocity u pointing outward (+u)\n- After one timestep, that pigment *should have moved* to x = GW-1 + \u03b5 (outside)\n- But in backward-trace, the cell at x = GW-1 has u also pointing outward, so it backs to x = GW-2 \u2192 samples interior \u2192 keeps pigment\n- Net effect: pigment piles up at the edge because nothing carries it away, and the clamp at sx >= GW-1.001 also feeds edge to edge\n\nFor outflow boundaries, the right approach is: cells **at** the edge get their values reset from \"off-canvas background\" (i.e., zero pigment, paper-equivalent state) rather than from interior values. This is a one-line change at the right place, but it also needs to be done for `wet`, `pressure`, and the deposited `d[]` array.\n\nLet me think about whether I should suggest this or just check with you first.\n\n---\n\n**Stopping here before I touch boundary code.** I want to flag what's at stake for #4.\n\nItems 1, 2, 3 are straightforward additions. I can do them now. Item 4 is a real architectural change to the boundary conditions and warrants discussion first:\n\n## On #4 (open boundaries)\n\n**What it would mean:** Change the canvas edges from \"closed walls that conserve mass\" to \"open boundaries that let mass leave.\" Currently, the test harness shows the only persistent hotspots in v0.72 are at the canvas corners \u2014 that's pigment accumulating at boundaries because mass-conserving advection has nowhere to send it. With open boundaries, that accumulation would instead exit the grid.\n\n**Visual effect:** A splash near an edge would have its outer ring \"fall off\" the canvas as if the paper had no edge there. Strokes that run off the canvas would lose their tail rather than building up an edge artifact.\n\n**What it changes:**\n- Pigment, water, and pressure that advects past the boundary is *discarded*, not reflected or clamped.\n- Mass is no longer conserved globally. The simulation no longer satisfies the physical invariant Curtis et al. specified.\n- The lib stops being \"watercolor on a piece of paper\" and becomes \"watercolor on an infinite paper that you can only see a window of.\"\n\n**Two things I'd want to verify before doing it:**\n\n1. **Edge darkening interacts with boundaries.** The edge-darkening code (Curtis et al. \u00a73.2) currently treats the canvas perimeter the way it treats any wet/dry boundary \u2014 it darkens. With open boundaries, the canvas edge would no longer be a wet/dry boundary in the physical sense, but the code might still darken it. The visual result might look wrong \u2014 a dark line where pigment should be falling off.\n\n2. **There's an alternative that might be what you actually want.** Instead of open boundaries (changes the physics), the simpler change is **render fade-out at the edges** \u2014 keep the physics closed but visually fade the rendered pigment to transparent in the last few pixels at each edge. This gives the \"falls off\" *look* without changing what the simulation is doing. It's a 10-line shader change rather than a boundary-condition rewrite.\n\n## My questions\n\nFor #4 specifically:\n\n- **Is the goal visual (falls-off appearance) or physical (mass actually leaves)?** The render-fade option is cheaper, lower-risk, and probably indistinguishable in your portfolio use case. The full open-boundary option is more \"correct\" in some sense but breaks Curtis et al.'s assumptions.\n- **Which edges?** All four? Or just the bottom (paint falling off the edge of a tilted page)?\n\nIf you confirm 1, 2, 3 plus your preference on 4 (render-fade, full open-boundary, all four edges, just bottom, etc.), I'll do them in one pass.",
    "citations": []
  }

Same strict\n// match semantics: only the exact pure primaries return a pigment\n// index; anything else returns null.\n//\n// v0.80 \u2014 Also recognize the K-M rendered hex values of each pigment\n// at typical wash thickness (x=1.0 over white paper). These are the\n// colors a designer would copy from the docs to mean \"draw this in\n// quinacridone rose / hansa yellow / cerulean blue\" \u2014 natural to\n// reach for, but they don't match any chroma-key. The motivation: when a\n          designer pulls a \"quinacridone rose\" value from the docs\n          and uses it as a fill color in their SVG, the lib should\n          recognize that as a direct pigment assignment. The\n          previous chroma-key system required them to know that\n          <code>#FF0000</code> meant rose &mdash; now either\n          convention works. Mask (<code>#00FF00</code>) and rainbow\n          (<code>#FF00FF</code>) remain chroma-key only since they\n          don't have rendered-pigment equivalents.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Mask tint toggle</h3>\n        <p>\n          New \"Mask tint\" toggle button in the toggles row, on by\n          default. Useful for taking screenshots where the\n          mask shouldn't be visible, or for previewing what a final\n          composition will look like with the mask in place but not\n          shown.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Public API: <code>wc.maskTint(v)</code> &mdash; pass true/\n          false to set, no argument to read. Forces an immediate\n          render on change so the toggle feels instantaneous.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Gouache: Auto by default</h3>\n        <p>\n          The Gouache toggle now starts at <strong>Auto</strong>\n          instead of Off in the playground. Auto LERPs between the\n          watercolor and gouache pigment sets based on paper\n          darkness &mdash; so dark papers automatically get more\n          opaque, light-toned pigments. This is the right default\n          for a \"just looks good on whatever paper\" experience and\n          removes a step from new users' first impression.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Embedding note: the lib's <code>create()</code> default\n          for <code>gouacheMode</code> is still <code>false</code>\n          (closed wash watercolor) for backward compatibility. Let me think this through carefully before touching code.\n\n## What \"falling off the edge\" needs to mean physically\n\nWhen pigment flows toward the canvas boundary, three things need to happen for it to *fall off* rather than *pile up*:\n\n1. **Pigment that advects past the edge must be discarded.** Currently the semi-Lagrangian back-trace clamps source positions to the interior (`sx = max(0, min(GW-1.001, sx))`) \u2014 that's what's causing the corner hotspots in the test harness. Cells near the edge sample interior cells indefinitely.\n\n2. **Water and pressure must also drain.** If pigment leaves but water stays, you get a dry edge with pigment-colored residue accumulating just inside the boundary \u2014 looks wrong, like a dam.\n\n3. **Velocity at the edge can't be reflective.** A closed (Neumann) boundary on velocity effectively bounces flow back into the interior, which is what creates the corner pileups. For outflow, velocity should just be free.\n\n## Two design choices I want to flag\n\n**Choice A \u2014 symmetric four-edge outflow.** Simple: every edge is open. Paint flows off any direction. Visual: a stroke that runs to the right disappears off the right edge; a deluge near the bottom corner loses a chunk of its outer wavefront.\n\n**Choice B \u2014 gravity-asymmetric (bottom-heavy).** Only the bottom edge drains freely; the other three are still closed but soft. Visual: paint pools at the bottom and falls off only there, like a tilted page. This is what real watercolor does on a held sheet \u2014 but it's more code, and it might fight the user's natural workflow of painting in arbitrary orientations.\n\n**My recommendation: A, with a clean API.** Symmetric is simpler, faster to validate, and gives you the \"infinite canvas window\" feel that's most useful for portfolio backgrounds (you can position a wash so it intentionally bleeds off one edge for compositional effect). If you want gravity later, that's a separate feature (\"tilt the canvas\").\n\n## What I expect to go wrong\n\nA few things I'm watching for, based on the kind of surprises this lib has had before:\n\n1. **Edge darkening might leave a dark line at the boundary.** The edge-darkening code treats canvas boundary as a wet/dry transition and darkens it. With open boundaries, that's no longer physically meaningful (there's no \"dry\" past the edge \u2014 there's nothing). The dark line would look like a bug.\n\n2. **The divergence correction depends on neighbors.** `div = (u[i+1] - u[i-1]) * 0.5 + (v[i+GW] - v[i-GW]) * 0.5`. At the edge, `u[i+1]` is across the boundary. Currently it reads whatever's in the array's edge cell. With open boundaries, the \"outside\" should be treated as having matching velocity (zero gradient = no extrapolated mass change). Easy fix but easy to miss.\n\n3. **Lift, evaporation, and paper diffusion at edges.** These operations read neighbor values. They'll need to behave the same as if the cells past the edge had paper-default values (zero pigment, dry).\n\n## Plan\n\nI'll do this carefully:\n\n1. Add a new lib option `openBoundaries` defaulting to `false` so existing embedders are unaffected.\n2. In the playground, default it to `true` so you get the new behavior.\n3. Modify the semi-Lagrangian back-trace: when `sx` or `sy` falls outside `[0, GW-1)` / `[0, GH-1)`, the cell receives **zero pigment** (paper background) instead of clamping.\n4. Modify divergence and pressure update to use \"zero-gradient outside\" assumption at edges (so the boundary doesn't reflect flow back).\n5. Modify wet/water update similarly \u2014 water that leaves doesn't come back.\n6. Check the edge-darkening interaction explicitly. If there's a dark line, attenuate edge darkening near the canvas boundary when open boundaries are on.\n7. Run the test harness: anisotropy should be unchanged; hotspots should be **gone** (the four canvas-corner hotspots in v0.72-0.80 should disappear).\n8. Visual smoke test: a deluge near a corner should lose part of its wavefront.\n\nThis is a real change, not a tweak. Worth getting right. Want me to proceed, or do you want to adjust the plan first?",
    "citations": []
  }
