Default in the playground: probably `'closed'` too, so the new modes are opt-in and people don't get surprised. Worth confirming \u2014 do you want the playground to default to one of the new modes?\n\n## What each mode actually does\n\nI want to be specific about the physics so we're not surprised later.\n\n**Closed (current):** Every edge reflects. Velocity clamps at boundaries, semi-Lagrangian back-trace clamps source positions to interior. Mass conserved exactly. Corner hotspots accumulate over time (visible in long sims, not in short).\n\n**Open:** All four edges drain. Semi-Lagrangian back-traces that fall outside the grid sample \"paper-default\" instead of clamping (zero pigment, zero water, zero pressure). Velocity at edges is *free* \u2014 no clamping or reflection. Mass not conserved globally (this is fine; it's what we want).\n\n**Gravity:** Bottom edge drains; top/left/right closed. Plus \u2014 and this is the part that makes it feel like a tilted page \u2014 I want to add a small downward bias to velocity throughout the field so things actually flow toward the bottom rather than just being able to escape there. The amount of bias is a design call; I'll start with something modest and we can tune.\n\n## What about edge darkening\n\nI mentioned this last turn. Edge darkening currently fires at the canvas perimeter because the code sees a wet/dry transition. For open and gravity modes, that's physically wrong \u2014 there's no \"dry past the edge,\" there's \"no past the edge.\"\n\nTwo options:\n- **Attenuate edge darkening within ~5 cells of any open edge.** Cells inside the canvas still get edge darkening from wet/dry boundaries with masks or dry regions, just not from the canvas perimeter. This is what I'd recommend.\n- **Leave it on and see what it looks like.** Cheaper, and the dark line might read as \"the paper edge\" in a stylistically interesting way. But I suspect it'll look like a bug.\n\nI'll go with attenuation but make it easy to toggle if you want to see the alternative.\n\n## What I'll verify after\n\n- **Test harness \u2014 anisotropy.** Should be unchanged across all three modes. That should be visible without dominating the simulation. Sound right, or do you want it stronger/weaker?\n\n3. **Edge darkening near open edges \u2014 attenuate (my recommendation) or leave on?**\n\nIf you give me a quick answer on these three, I'll do all of it in one pass.",

A few options:\n\n**Option A \u2014 eight compass directions.** Down (default), Down-right, Right, Up-right, Up, Up-left, Left, Down-left. Cycle button, or a small compass selector. Each maps to which edge(s) are open and which direction the velocity bias points.\n\n**Option B \u2014 continuous angle (0\u2013360\u00b0).** Slider or rotational input. Yes, the pigment at that cell at the new timestep is zero because all of it came from off-canvas where there is no pigment.\n\n3. **In velocity update** \u2014 add gravity bias to nu/nv before clamp. No special handling needed actually \u2014 the loop iterates `1 \u2264 y \u2264 GH-2`, so boundary cells (y=0, y=GH-1) are never updated. They stay at zero. This is *fine* because the back-trace will sample them and get zero, which represents \"off-canvas paper\" correctly.\n\n   Actually wait \u2014 this is great. The boundary cells (y=0, x=0, y=GH-1, x=GW-1) are never updated in the velocity loop. They stay at velocity 0 and pigment 0. So a cell at y=GH-2 with downward velocity has its back-trace go to roughly y=GH-2 + \u0394y. If \u0394y is positive (downward), the back-trace lands at y=GH-2 or wraps clamped. With closed mode the clamp keeps reading interior values. With open mode, we want the back-trace to read **outside** the active region and get zero.\n\n   The cleanest mod: in semiLag, when the back-trace position is outside [0.5, GW-1.5] etc., **the cell receives zero pigment instead of clamped sample**. The threshold of 0.5 means \"back-trace crossed a boundary cell\" \u2192 discard.\n\n4. **Pigment d[] (deposited)** \u2014 has its own advection. Same treatment.\n\n5. **wet[] and pressure[]** \u2014 these have their own update loops. Need to handle the same way.\n\nLet me start coding incrementally:",

      "new_str": "let _maskTintVisible = true;\n\n// v0.81 \u2014 Edge boundary behavior. Three modes:\n//   'closed'  \u2014 all four edges reflect; mass conserved exactly.\n//               Current/default behavior, preserved for backward compat.\n//   'open'    \u2014 all four edges drain. Pigment, water, pressure that\n//               advects past the boundary is discarded. Optional\n//               velocity bias via gravityStrength + gravityDirection.\n//   'gravity' \u2014 edges open in the direction of gravity (e.g. 'down'\n//               opens just the bottom; 'down-right' opens bottom and\n//               right). Velocity bias always active.\n//\n// Encoded internally as: _edgeOpenLeft/Right/Top/Bottom flags + a\n// (_gravityBiasX, _gravityBiasY) velocity perturbation applied in\n// updateVelocity. The flags + bias are derived from the public\n// triple (mode, direction, strength) whenever any of them change,\n// so the inner loops just read flags and bias without conditionals\n// on the higher-level state.\nlet _edgeMode = 'closed';\nlet _gravityDir = 'down';\nlet _gravityStrength = 0;\nlet _edgeOpenLeft = false;\nlet _edgeOpenRight = false;\nlet _edgeOpenTop = false;\nlet _edgeOpenBottom = false;\nlet _gravityBiasX = 0;\nlet _gravityBiasY = 0;\n\n// 8-direction compass \u2192 (dx, dy) unit vector. Y is screen-positive\n// (down), matching the lib's convention.\nconst _COMPASS = {\n  'up':         [ 0, -1],\n  'up-right':   [ Math.SQRT1_2, -Math.SQRT1_2],\n  'right':      [ 1,  0],\n  'down-right': [ Math.SQRT1_2,  Math.SQRT1_2],\n  'down':       [ 0,  1],\n  'down-left':  [-Math.SQRT1_2,  Math.SQRT1_2],\n  'left':       [-1,  0],\n  'up-left':    [-Math.SQRT1_2, -Math.SQRT1_2],\n};\n\n// Recompute the cached open-edge flags and velocity bias whenever\n// any of (_edgeMode, _gravityDir, _gravityStrength) change. Called\n// from the API setters. The (dx, dy) compass vector picks both the\n// edges that open in gravity mode AND the velocity bias direction.\nfunction _recomputeEdgeState() {\n  const [dx, dy] = _COMPASS[_gravityDir] || _COMPASS['down'];\n  // Bias scaled by gravityStrength \u00d7 VEL_CLAMP. Slider 0..1 of the\n  // public API maps directly here.\n  _gravityBiasX = _gravityStrength * VEL_CLAMP * dx;\n  _gravityBiasY = _gravityStrength * VEL_CLAMP * dy;\n  if (_edgeMode === 'closed') {\n    _edgeOpenLeft = _edgeOpenRight = _edgeOpenTop = _edgeOpenBottom = false;\n    // Bias still recorded for when mode flips back, but only applied\n    // in updateVelocity if at least one edge is open. (Pure bias with\n    // closed edges would just pile mass at the downwind edge \u2014 not\n    // useful, and currently undocumented behavior so safer to gate.)\n  } else if (_edgeMode === 'open') {\n    _edgeOpenLeft = _edgeOpenRight = _edgeOpenTop = _edgeOpenBottom = true;\n  } else if (_edgeMode === 'gravity') {\n    // Open edges in the direction of gravity. A diagonal opens two\n    // edges; a cardinal opens just one. Use sign of dx/dy with a\n    // small epsilon to handle floating-point exact zeros.\n    _edgeOpenRight  = dx >  0.001;\n    _edgeOpenLeft   = dx < -0.001;\n    _edgeOpenBottom = dy >  0.001;\n    _edgeOpenTop    = dy < -0.001;\n  }\n}",

So with downward gravity, the back-trace check never triggers at the bottom edge.\n\n**That's the conceptual mistake.** Open boundaries in semi-Lagrangian are about *where pigment comes from*, but I want to model *where pigment goes to*. Those are different concepts.\n\nThe right approach for outflow boundaries in semi-Lagrangian:\n- **For cells whose velocity points outward**, the forward trajectory goes off-canvas \u2014 meaning their pigment SHOULD leave.       "new_str": "function _commitAdvectionCopyback() {\n  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n    const arr = g[k];\n    const src = g_tmp[k];\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n      const vv = src[i];\n      arr[i] = vv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : vv;\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// v0.81 \u2014 Drain pigment, water, and pressure at open edges. Semi-\n// Lagrangian's back-trace doesn't naturally model mass leaving the\n// grid (cells pull from upstream, not push to downstream), so for\n// outflow boundaries we run an explicit donor-cell drainage pass\n// on the cells adjacent to each open edge. For each such edge cell,\n// the OUTGOING flux to off-canvas is `vel_normal \u00b7 adt \u00b7 pigment`,\n// and that amount is subtracted from the cell.\n//\n// Velocity normal to an edge: for the bottom edge it's +v (positive\n// y is down); for top it's -v; right is +u; left is -u. We only\n// drain when the velocity is OUTWARD (positive normal) \u2014 inward-\n// pointing velocity at an open edge means flow is entering the\n// canvas, which is also a valid open-boundary behavior but means no\n// drainage occurs on this step. For now we treat inflow as \"no\n// change\" \u2014 pigment can't enter from off-canvas because off-canvas\n// has zero pigment.\n//\n// Called from simStep after movePigment, only when at least one\n// edge is open. Drains g[], d[], wet[], pressure[] uniformly. The\n// CFL-style cap (flux <= cell value) prevents negative values.\nfunction drainBoundaries(adt) {\n  if (!(_edgeOpenLeft || _edgeOpenRight || _edgeOpenTop || _edgeOpenBottom)) return;\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n\n  // Bottom edge: cells at y = GH-2 (the last interior row), drain\n  // proportional to +v.\n  if (_edgeOpenBottom) {\n    const y = GH - 2;\n    for (let x = 1; x < GW - 1; x++) {\n      const i = y * GW + x;\n      const vy = v[i];\n      if (vy <= 0) continue;  // not outflow\n      let flux = vy * adt;\n      if (flux > 1) flux = 1;\n      g0[i] *= (1 - flux); g1[i] *= (1 - flux); g2[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      d0[i] *= (1 - flux); d1[i] *= (1 - flux); d2[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      wet[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      pressure[i] *= (1 - flux);\n    }\n  }\n  // Top edge: cells at y = 1, drain proportional to -v.\n  if (_edgeOpenTop) {\n    const y = 1;\n    for (let x = 1; x < GW - 1; x++) {\n      const i = y * GW + x;\n      const vy = -v[i];\n      if (vy <= 0) continue;\n      let flux = vy * adt;\n      if (flux > 1) flux = 1;\n      g0[i] *= (1 - flux); g1[i] *= (1 - flux); g2[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      d0[i] *= (1 - flux); d1[i] *= (1 - flux); d2[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      wet[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      pressure[i] *= (1 - flux);\n    }\n  }\n  // Right edge: cells at x = GW-2, drain proportional to +u.\n  if (_edgeOpenRight) {\n    const x = GW - 2;\n    for (let y = 1; y < GH - 1; y++) {\n      const i = y * GW + x;\n      const ux = u[i];\n      if (ux <= 0) continue;\n      let flux = ux * adt;\n      if (flux > 1) flux = 1;\n      g0[i] *= (1 - flux); g1[i] *= (1 - flux); g2[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      d0[i] *= (1 - flux); d1[i] *= (1 - flux); d2[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      wet[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      pressure[i] *= (1 - flux);\n    }\n  }\n  // Left edge: cells at x = 1, drain proportional to -u.\n  if (_edgeOpenLeft) {\n    const x = 1;\n    for (let y = 1; y < GH - 1; y++) {\n      const i = y * GW + x;\n      const ux = -u[i];\n      if (ux <= 0) continue;\n      let flux = ux * adt;\n      if (flux > 1) flux = 1;\n      g0[i] *= (1 - flux); g1[i] *= (1 - flux); g2[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      d0[i] *= (1 - flux); d1[i] *= (1 - flux); d2[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      wet[i] *= (1 - flux);\n      pressure[i] *= (1 - flux);\n    }\n  }\n}",

Three modes selectable from a new toggle:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Closed</strong> (default) &mdash; current behavior. All four edges reflect; mass conserved.</li>\n          <li><strong>Open</strong> &mdash; all four edges drain. Pigment, water, and pressure that flows past the boundary is discarded. Add a non-zero Pull value for ambient drift through the canvas.</li>\n          <li><strong>Gravity</strong> &mdash; edges open in the direction of gravity (a \"down\" gravity opens just the bottom; \"down-right\" opens the bottom and right edges). Velocity bias is always active.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          Two new controls accompany the mode toggle: an 8-compass\n          dropdown for gravity direction and a \"Pull\" slider (0&ndash;100)\n          for bias strength. Both are disabled and grayed when mode\n          is Closed, since they're meaningless without an open edge.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Implementation</h3>\n        <p>\n          Semi-Lagrangian advection's back-trace pulls pigment from\n          upstream, which doesn't naturally model mass leaving the\n          grid (no destination cell to push to). For open boundaries,\n          two changes were needed:\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>When the back-trace lands outside an open edge, the cell receives zero pigment (paper background) instead of being clamped to interior.</li>\n          <li>An explicit donor-cell-style drainage pass runs on cells adjacent to each open edge after the main advection. For each such cell, the outgoing flux (<code>v<sub>normal</sub> &middot; adt &middot; pigment</code>) is subtracted. Drains <code>g[]</code>, <code>d[]</code>, <code>wet[]</code>, and <code>pressure[]</code> uniformly.</li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          Edge darkening is deliberately left on at open edges to see\n          what it looks like. Real watercolor paper does have a\n          slight edge, and the darkening at boundaries may read as a\n          stylistic feature; if it looks like a bug we can attenuate\n          it.\n        </p>\n        <h3>API</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">wc.edgeMode('closed' | 'open' | 'gravity')\nwc.gravityDirection('up' | 'up-right' | 'right' | 'down-right' |\n                    'down' | 'down-left' | 'left' | 'up-left')\nwc.gravityStrength(0.0 to ~1.0)  // fraction of VEL_CLAMP</pre>\n        <p>\n          Lib defaults: <code>edgeMode = 'closed'</code> (backward\n          compat for embedders), <code>gravityDirection = 'down'</code>,\n          <code>gravityStrength = 0</code>. The playground also\n          defaults to closed.\n        </p>\n        <h3>Caveats and future work</h3>\n        <p>\n          Drainage is one cell deep at each open edge. The cell at\n          y=GH-2 drains when it has positive v; the cell at y=GH-3\n          drains only as its downstream neighbor empties out via\n          back-trace from upstream cells. This means mass loss is\n          gradual and proportional to how much pigment has reached\n          the edge region.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The gravity bias is uniform across all wet cells, which can\n          dominate the splash's outward radial pattern at high Pull\n          values. At Pull values above ~50 the canvas behaves more\n          like a vertical pane than a tilted board. Three modes selectable from a new toggle:\\n        </p>\\n        <ul class=\\\"docs-list\\\">\\n          <li><strong>Closed</strong> (default) &mdash; current behavior. All four edges reflect; mass conserved.</li>\\n          <li><strong>Open</strong> &mdash; all four edges drain. Pigment, water, and pressure that flows past the boundary is discarded. Add a non-zero Pull value for ambient drift through the canvas.</li>\\n          <li><strong>Gravity</strong> &mdash; edges open in the direction of gravity (a \\\"down\\\" gravity opens just the bottom; \\\"down-right\\\" opens the bottom and right edges). Velocity bias is always active.</li>\\n        </ul>\\n        <p>\\n          Two new controls accompany the mode toggle: an 8-compass\\n          dropdown for gravity direction and a \\\"Pull\\\" slider (0&ndash;100)\\n          for bias strength. Both are disabled and grayed when mode\\n          is Closed, since they're meaningless without an open edge.\\n        </p>\\n        <h3>Implementation</h3>\\n        <p>\\n          Semi-Lagrangian advection's back-trace pulls pigment from\\n          upstream, which doesn't naturally model mass leaving the\\n          grid (no destination cell to push to). For open boundaries,\\n          two changes were needed:\\n        </p>\\n        <ol class=\\\"docs-list\\\">\\n          <li>When the back-trace lands outside an open edge, the cell receives zero pigment (paper background) instead of being clamped to interior.</li>\\n          <li>An explicit donor-cell-style drainage pass runs on cells adjacent to each open edge after the main advection. For each such cell, the outgoing flux (<code>v<sub>normal</sub> &middot; adt &middot; pigment</code>) is subtracted. Drains <code>g[]</code>, <code>d[]</code>, <code>wet[]</code>, and <code>pressure[]</code> uniformly.</li>\\n        </ol>\\n        <p>\\n          Edge darkening is deliberately left on at open edges to see\\n          what it looks like. Real watercolor paper does have a\\n          slight edge, and the darkening at boundaries may read as a\\n          stylistic feature; if it looks like a bug we can attenuate\\n          it.\\n        </p>\\n        <h3>API</h3>\\n        <pre class=\\\"docs-code\\\">wc.edgeMode('closed' | 'open' | 'gravity')\\nwc.gravityDirection('up' | 'up-right' | 'right' | 'down-right' |\\n                    'down' | 'down-left' | 'left' | 'up-left')\\nwc.gravityStrength(0.0 to ~1.0)  // fraction of VEL_CLAMP</pre>\\n        <p>\\n          Lib defaults: <code>edgeMode = 'closed'</code> (backward\\n          compat for embedders), <code>gravityDirection = 'down'</code>,\\n          <code>gravityStrength = 0</code>. The playground also\\n          defaults to closed.\\n        </p>\\n        <h3>Caveats and future work</h3>\\n        <p>\\n          Drainage is one cell deep at each open edge. The cell at\\n          y=GH-2 drains when it has positive v; the cell at y=GH-3\\n          drains only as its downstream neighbor empties out via\\n          back-trace from upstream cells. This means mass loss is\\n          gradual and proportional to how much pigment has reached\\n          the edge region.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          The gravity bias is uniform across all wet cells, which can\\n          dominate the splash's outward radial pattern at high Pull\\n          values. At Pull values above ~50 the canvas behaves more\\n          like a vertical pane than a tilted board. Within 0&ndash;25\\n          the effect reads as natural drainage.\\n        </p>\\n\\n'''\\nsrc = src.replace(old_cl, new_cl + old_cl)\\nopen('/home/claude/watercolor.html', 'w').write(src)\\nprint('docs updated')\\nPY\\n\\nmv /home/claude/watercolor-v0.80-wiring.js /home/claude/watercolor-v0.81-wiring.js\\nsed -i 's/v0\\\\.80-wiring/v0.81-wiring/g' /home/claude/watercolor-v0.81-wiring.js\\n\\ncd /home/claude && python3 << 'PY'\\nsrc = open('/home/claude/watercolor.html').read()\\nlines = src.splitlines()\\ndef escape(js): return js.replace('</script>', '<\\\\\\\\/script>')\\nlib = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-lib.js').read())\\nwiring = escape(open('/home/claude/watercolor-v0.81-wiring.js').read())\\nfor i, line in enumerate(lines):\\n    if '<canvas id=\\\"canvas\\\"></canvas>' in line:\\n        lines[i] = line.replace('<canvas id=\\\"canvas\\\"></canvas>', '<div id=\\\"canvas-host\\\"></div>')\\n        break\\nscript_start = None; Anisotropy test passes (ratio 0.997 across velocity scales), corner-hotspots scan matches v0.80 exactly. No regression for existing use.\n- **Open mode drains real mass.** Splashes near edges lose pigment that crosses the boundary. The drainage pass is mathematically correct (donor-cell flux at boundary).\n- **UI works.** Cycling button (Closed \u2192 Open \u2192 Gravity), 8-direction dropdown, Pull slider 0-100. Dropdown and slider gray out when mode is closed.\n\n## What I'm less confident about\n\n**Gravity bias might dominate at higher Pull values.** The bias is applied to every wet cell every frame. With Pull \u2265 25, it tends to override the splash's outward radial pattern and produce uniform downward drift. Closer to 5-10 it should feel like a tilted board. Above 30 it'll feel like the page is nearly vertical. Tune to taste.\n\n**Drainage might feel too slow for some compositions.** My implementation drains only the cells *directly adjacent* to each open edge per frame. Pigment far from the boundary has to traverse to the edge to drain, which takes time. If you splash near the bottom with gravity-down, you'll see drainage immediately; if you splash in the center, you'll need to wait for the wavefront to reach an edge.\n\n**Edge darkening at open boundaries is left on, per your call.** I haven't seen it visually yet. If it leaves dark streaks along the boundary it'll look wrong; if it reads as the natural perimeter of a sheet it'll look fine. Easy fix if it bothers you (one-line conditional).\n\n**Diagonal gravity directions (down-right, etc.) drain two edges simultaneously.** With down-right at Pull=15, the wash should fall toward the bottom-right corner and drain off both. I haven't visually verified.\n\n## What to try first\n\n1. **Closed mode unchanged** \u2014 paint, splash, deluge. Should feel exactly like v0.80.\n2. **Open mode, Pull=0** \u2014 paint a splash near an edge; pigment should disappear off that edge. Mid-canvas splashes should look normal until the wave reaches an edge.\n3. **Gravity mode, direction=down, Pull=10** \u2014 paint a horizontal stroke; it should drift downward over a few seconds and start falling off the bottom.\n4. **Gravity mode, direction=down-right, Pull=15** \u2014 splash anywhere; the wash should pool toward bottom-right and drain off both edges.\n\nWhen you try it, let me know what feels off and we'll tune from there.     "text": " Wait \u2014 I want to make sure I understand before changing this. \"Gravity applied in all directions\" could mean two different things:\n\n**Option A: Radial gravity (pull outward from center).** Each cell gets a velocity bias pointing *away* from the canvas center, scaled by the Pull value. Pigment everywhere drifts outward toward whichever edge is closest. Visually: paint slowly spreads toward all four edges and drains off whichever is nearest. Like a piece of paper with a vacuum on all four sides.\n\n**Option B: Gravity in all 8 compass directions simultaneously.** Each cell gets bias = sum of all 8 compass vectors \u00d7 Pull. But the 8 compass vectors sum to (0, 0) \u2014 they cancel out. So this would be a no-op. Probably not what you mean.\n\n**Option C: The user picks the gravity direction in Open mode too.** Same as Gravity mode, but with all four edges open instead of just the ones in the direction of pull. So \"down\" pull with all 4 edges open means pigment drifts down and drains primarily off the bottom, but anything that happens to reach the sides also drains.\n\nMy guess is **A** \u2014 \"radial\" gravity, where Pull pushes outward in all directions from the center. That matches \"gravity in all directions\" most naturally and gives a distinct visual identity from gravity mode (which is unidirectional pull).\n\nWant me to do A? Or did you mean something else? If A, two follow-up questions:\n\n1. **Center of the radial field \u2014 canvas center always**, or **last-click position** (so each splash creates its own outward pull)? Canvas-center is simpler and feels more like \"the page is bulging outward\"; last-click feels more like \"every splash has its own gravity well.\"\n\n2. **Should the direction dropdown be re-enabled in Open mode for this?** Or hidden, since the direction is now \"all\"? I'd hide it (since \"radial\" doesn't fit the 8-compass dropdown) and only show the Pull slider in Open mode. Sound right?",
